identity.research_question: : Analysis of a large cohort of 265 HGSC patients using 10 TMAs (four cores per tumor) to profile HLA-class II expression on cancer cells and its association with survival and clinical outcomes. This study was provisionally accepted/published in *Cancer Discovery* 2026 (doi:10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1
identity.project_summary: : Analysis of a large cohort of 265 HGSC patients using 10 TMAs (four cores per tumor) to profile HLA-class II expression on cancer cells and its association with survival and clinical outcomes. This study was provisionally accepted/published in Cancer Discovery 2026 (doi:10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492). Integrates 30-antibody t-CycIF proteomics, Whole Slide Image (WSI) validations from the Oncosys-OVA (HRPritti) cohort, and GeoMx Spatial Transcriptomics. Custom preprocessing was developed using OpenCV denoising, Ilastik (DAPI+LaminB) pixel classification, CellProfiler segmentation, and Tribus cell-type calling. MultiNicheNetR ligand-receptor profiling was performed to map tumor-immune cell interactions (CD4+, CD8+, regulatory T cells, DCs vs tumor cells) in MHCII-expressing niches, validated via GeoMx. Includes immunopeptidomic profiling (with Cerullo's laboratory) of MHCII peptides from 12 HGSC study samples (Anastasia). Description: Analysis of a large cohort of 265 HGSC patients using 10 TMAs (four cores per tumor) to profile HLA-class II expression on cancer cells and its association with survival and clinical outcomes. This study was provisionally accepted/published in Cancer Discovery 2026 (doi:10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492). Integrates 30-antibody t-CycIF proteomics, Whole Slide Image (WSI) validations from the Oncosys-OVA (HRPritti) cohort, and GeoMx Spatial Transcriptomics. Custom preprocessing was developed using OpenCV denoising, Ilastik (DAPI+LaminB) pixel classification, CellProfiler segmentation, and Tribus cell-type calling. MultiNicheNetR ligand-receptor profiling was performed to map tumor-immune cell interactions (CD4+, CD8+, regulatory T cells, DCs vs tumor cells) in MHCII-expressing niches, validated via GeoMx. Includes immunopeptidomic profiling (with Cerullo's laboratory) of MHCII peptides from 12 HGSC study samples (Anastasia).
identity.responsible: Fernando Perez (Julia Casado, Anastasia Kachalova, Aleksandra Shabanova)

[We need to make a collaboration agreement]. Viccenzo lab can do the contract. - We could get results maybe by the end of March. - Cell suspension or frozen tissue is good. The gold standard is 1g of tissue. - Funding discussion - Ja

Improved resolution of tumor-specific expression of MHCII - with **Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data, additional spatial proteomics representations, scRNAseq and imaging data analysis** excluding the possibility of artefacts/spill over and demonstrating true MHCII expression in the tumor cells. 2. document_index.excerpt: REVISION [[Fernando Perez]](mailto:frpvillatoro@gmail.com) Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ): The authors have addressed most of my comments to satisfaction, I only The authors present new data supporting notion that MHCII is expressed by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging. While this is appreciated and supported by their functional experiments, I am wondering why in Supp 3g and in the point-by-point response, the values for MHCII on cancer cells don't line up between the different correlation analyses (i.e. the correlations with CD11b, MHCII and CD3). In the MHCII in cancer vs. neighbor plot there are lots of cancer MHCII values between 11 and 15 while these are missing from the cancer MHCII vs max(CD3d or CD11b). document_index.name: Untitled presentation.pptx
document_index.title: Objectives of the study & their impact
document_index.excerpt: Objectives of the study & their impact The project aims to discover how the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and MHC dysregulation facilitate immune escape in ovarian cancer: Objective 1. Identify MHC patterns in HGSC subtypes and determine their clinical significance. Using t-CycIF, we profiled single-cell spatial landscapes of HGSC in 265 samples and characterized cellular communities and their interactions. Through machine-learning–guided feature extraction, we identified distinct MHC dysregulation patterns associated with specific HGSC molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes. This work demonstrates that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is a key determinant of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness.  def __init__(self, exp_name = None, channels = None, channels_to_transform = None, channels_to_outliers = None, channels_to_scale = None, cells = None, target = None, therapies = None, mol_profiles = None, scaler = None, df = None, random_seed = None):

 # Save results

 def prep_train_test_data(self):
 
 # To receive same number of patients independent of runs
 np.random.seed(self.rng)
 
 # Group df by Molecular profile and therapy
 grouped = self.df.groupby(['Molecular.profile2', 'therapy_sequence'])

 # Assign 80% of patients from each unique group of Molecular profile and therapy to training and rest to test sets
 self.X_train_full = grouped.apply(lambda x: x.loc[x['patient'].isin(np.random.choice(x['patient'].unique(), size=int(0.8*len(x['patient'].unique())), replace=False))])
 self.X_test_full = grouped.apply(lambda x: x.loc[x['patient'].isin(np.setdiff1d(x['patient'].unique(), self.X_train_full['patient']))])
 
 # Assign target columns to y_train and y_test
 self.y_train=self.X_train_full[self.target]
 self.y_test=self.X_test_full[self.target]
 
 # Drop target columns
 self.X_train = self.X_train_full.drop(columns = [self.target, 'patient', 'cycif.slide','Molecular.profile2'])
 self.X_test = self.X_test_full.drop(columns = [self.target, 'patient', 'cycif.slide','Molecular.profile2'])
 
 logger.info(f"\nPrepared X_train of size {self.X_train.shape}, y_train of size {len(self.y_train)} and X_test of size {self.X_test.shape}, y_test of size {len(self.y_test)} in experiment {self.exp_name}.")

 def find_RF_by_Bayesian(self, X_train, y_train):
 '''
 inputs:

red X_train of size {self.X_train.shape}, y_train of size {len(self.y_train)} and X_test of size {self.X_test.shape}, y_test of size {len(self.y_test)} in experiment {self.exp_name}.")

 def find_RF_by_Bayesian(self, X_train, y_train):
 '''
 inputs:
 - X_train: features training set
 - y_train: classes' labels of training set
 '''
 try:
 # Define hyperparameters search space 
 space = [Integer(200, 900, name='min_samples_split'),
 Integer(200, 900, name='min_samples_leaf'),
 Integer(50, 200, name='n_estimators')]
 
 # Store standard deviations of cross-validated scores
 std_devs = []
 
 # Define evaluation for each call within hyperparameters' space
 @use_named_args(space)
 def evaluate_model(**params):
 with parallel_backend('threading', n_jobs=-1):
 scores = cross_val_score(self.RF, X_train, y_train, scoring="f1_weighted", cv=self.cv, n_jobs=-1)
 std_devs.append(np.std(scores))
 return -np.mean(scores)
 
 # Perform Bayesian search on defined hyperparameters search space 
 start = time() # Get start time
 self.train_results = gp_minimize(evaluate_model, space, random_state=self.rng, n_random_starts=5, n_calls = 30)
 end = time() # Get end time
 
 # Calculate the training time
 self.train_time = end - start

 logger.info(f"\nAverage standard deviation of cross-validated scores in {self.exp_name}: {np.mean(std_devs)}")
 logger.info(f"\nTraining_time in {self.exp_name}: {self.train_time}")
 
 # Store the best hyperparameters
 self.best_params = {'min_samples_split': self.train_results.x[0],
 'min_samples_leaf': self.train_results.x[1],
 'n_estimators': self.train_results.x[2]}
 
 # Create RF model with stored best hyperparameters
 self.RF = RandomForestClassifier(**self.best_params, random_state = self.rng, class_weight = 'balanced')

'min_samples_leaf': self.train_results.x[1],
 'n_estimators': self.train_results.x[2]}
 
 # Create RF model with stored best hyperparameters
 self.RF = RandomForestClassifier(**self.best_params, random_state = self.rng, class_weight = 'balanced') 

 logger.info(f"\nSuccessfully created RF model in {self.exp_name} with hyperparameters: {self.train_results.x} ( score - {-self.train_results.fun:.5f})")
 
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred in find_RF_by_Bayesian during {self.exp_name}: {e}", exc_info=True)
 
 def test(self, X_train, y_train, X_test_full, X_test, y_test):
 '''
 inputs:
 - X_train: features training set
 - y_train: classes' labels of training set
 - X_test_full: features testing set with saved patient and slide IDs
 - X_test: features testing set
 - y_test: classes' labels of testing set
 '''
 
 # Training step: Fit the learner to the training data
 self.RF.fit(X_train, y_train)
 
 # Get the predictions on the training set(X_train),
 # then get predictions on the test samples(X_test)
 self.predictions_train = self.RF.predict(X_train)
 # Test step:
 self.predictions_test = self.RF.predict(X_test)
 
 # Save the predictions of X_test as df:
 # 1. Convert predictions_test to a pandas series
 predictions_series = pd.Series(self.predictions_test, name='predicted_label')
 # 2. Convert MultiIndex to a flat index in X_test
 X_test_flat = X_test_full.reset_index(drop=True)
 # 3. Concatenate X_test_flat and predictions_series along axis=1 to receive df of test set predictions 
 self.predictions_df = pd.concat([X_test_flat, predictions_series], axis=1)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on training samples
 self.balanced_accuracy_score_train = balanced_accuracy_score(y_train, self.predictions_train)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on the test set

f = pd.concat([X_test_flat, predictions_series], axis=1)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on training samples
 self.balanced_accuracy_score_train = balanced_accuracy_score(y_train, self.predictions_train)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on the test set
 self.balanced_accuracy_score_test = balanced_accuracy_score(y_test, self.predictions_test)
 
 # Compute weighted F1-score on training samples
 self.f1_score_train = f1_score(y_train, self.predictions_train, average='weighted')
 
 # Compute weighted F1-score on the test set
 self.f1_score_test = f1_score(y_test, self.predictions_test, average='weighted')
 
 logger.info(f"\nSuccessfully tested RF model in {self.exp_name}")
 
 def calculate_mean_per_patient(self):
 name = self.exp_name + '_patients_predictions.csv'
 patients_predictions = self.predictions_df.groupby('patient')['predicted_label'].mean().reset_index()
 patients_predictions.to_csv(name, index=False)
 
 logger.info(f"\nSuccessfully calculated patients' predictions of RF model in {self.exp_name}")

 def eliminate_features(self, X_train, y_train):
 '''
 inputs:
 - X_train: features training set
 - y_train: classes' labels of training set
 - save_statement: define if you want to save figures 
 '''
 try:
 # Define cross-validated recursive feature elimination 
 # RFECV automatically selects the best number of features based on the provided scoring function of accuracy
 rfecv = RFECV(
 estimator=self.RF,
 min_features_to_select=5,
 step=5,
 n_jobs=-1,
 scoring="accuracy",
 cv=self.cv,
 )

 # Run cross-validated recursive feature elimination to select the most predictive features
 rfecv.fit(X_train.values, y_train.values)
 
 # Get the support mask indicating which features were selected by RFECV, where True values in the mask correspond to important features.

cursive feature elimination to select the most predictive features
 rfecv.fit(X_train.values, y_train.values)
 
 # Get the support mask indicating which features were selected by RFECV, where True values in the mask correspond to important features.
 support = rfecv.support_

 # Get the list of most predictive and eliminated features
 features = X_train.columns
 self.eliminated_features = list(features[~support])
 self.important_features = features[support]

 logger.info(f"\nImportant features in {self.exp_name}: {self.important_features}")
 logger.info(f"\nEliminated features in {self.exp_name}: {self.eliminated_features}")

 # Calculate feature importances using mean decrease in impurity (Gini impurity)
 importances = rfecv.estimator_.feature_importances_

 self.X_train_transformed = X_train[self.important_features]

 # Calculate permutation importances
 permutation_scores = permutation_importance(rfecv.estimator_, self.X_train_transformed.values, y_train.values, n_repeats=10, random_state=self.rng, n_jobs=-1)

 feature_permutations = dict(zip(self.important_features, permutation_scores.importances_mean))
 self.permutation_scores_dict = dict(sorted(feature_permutations.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True))

 # For gini impurity:
 # Create dictionary with important feature names as keys and importances as values
 feature_importances = dict(zip(self.important_features, importances))
 self.importances = dict(sorted(feature_importances.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True))
 
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred during {self.exp_name} in eliminate_features: {e}", exc_info=True)

 def save_values(self):

 # Compute balanced accuracy on training samples
 self.test_results['balanced_acc_train'] = self.balanced_accuracy_score_train

error(f"\nAn error occurred during {self.exp_name} in eliminate_features: {e}", exc_info=True)

 # Compute weighted F1-score on the test set
 self.test_results['f1_test'] = self.f1_score_test

 self.feature_selection_results['importances'] = self.importances

 self.feature_selection_results['permutation_scores'] = self.permutation_scores_dict

def perform_ml_run(exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale, cells, target, classes, therapies, scaler, best_params, acc_train, acc_test, f_train, f_test, pred_features, eliminated_features, permutation_scores, random_seed, name, exps, df):

 model = Model(exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale, cells, target, classes, therapies, scaler, df)

 model.choose_features()
 model.transform_df()
 model.remove_outliers()
 model.scaling()
 model.choose_types_of_cells()
 model.choose_mol_profiles()
 model.choose_therapy_sequences()

 model.prepare_categorical_inputs()
 model.prep_train_test_data()
 model.find_RF_by_Bayesian(model.X_train.values, model.y_train.values)
 model.test(model.X_train.values, model.y_train.values, model.X_test_full, model.X_test.values, model.y_test.values)
 model.calculate_mean_per_patient()
 model.eliminate_features(model.X_train, model.y_train)
 model.save_values()

 # Collect the results in a dictionary
 results = {
 'exp_name': exp_name,
 'best_parameters': model.best_params,
 'balanced_acc_train': model.test_results['balanced_acc_train'],
 'balanced_acc_test': model.test_results['balanced_acc_test'],
 'f1_train': model.test_results['f1_train'],
 'f1_test': model.test_results['f1_test'],
 'most_predictive_features': model.feature_selection_results['importances'],
 'eliminated_features': model.feature_selection_results['eliminated_features'],
 'permutation_scores': model.feature_selection_results['permutation_scores']
 }

['f1_test'],
 'most_predictive_features': model.feature_selection_results['importances'],
 'eliminated_features': model.feature_selection_results['eliminated_features'],
 'permutation_scores': model.feature_selection_results['permutation_scores']
 }

 logger.info(f"\n{exp_name} is done") 

 return results

def run_ml_experiments(data):
 exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale, types_of_cells, classes_column, \
 therapies, mol_profiles, scaling_type, best_parameters, balanced_acc_train, balanced_acc_test, f1_train, f1_test, \
 most_predictive_features, eliminated_features, permutation_scores, random_seed, name, exps, df = data

 # Call the perform_ml_run function with the provided parameters
 result = perform_ml_run(exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale,
 types_of_cells, classes_column, therapies, mol_profiles, scaling_type, best_parameters,
 balanced_acc_train, balanced_acc_test, f1_train, f1_test, most_predictive_features,
 eliminated_features, permutation_scores, random_seed, name, exps, df)

 return result

if __name__ == '__main__':

 try:
 df = pd.read_csv("~/33_66_percentiles_clinical_outcome_primary_samples_112025.csv")
 df['Molecular.profile2'] = df['Molecular.profile2'].replace('BRCAmut/met', 'BRCAmutmet')
 
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred while reading dataset file: {e}", exc_info=True) 
 
 # Get the number of available CPU cores for parallel processing
 max_workers = mp.cpu_count()
 
 # Specify your exps files
 names = ['1_Cancer_BRCAmutmet_HRD_PDS.csv']

 for name in names:
 
 try:
 exps = pd.read_csv(name)
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred while reading experiments' file: {e}", exc_info=True)

nt()
 
 # Specify your exps files
 names = ['1_Cancer_BRCAmutmet_HRD_PDS.csv']

 for name in names:
 
 try:
 exps = pd.read_csv(name)
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred while reading experiments' file: {e}", exc_info=True) 

 # Use loky's get_reusable_executor for parallel execution of multiple experiments
 with get_reusable_executor(max_workers=max_workers, timeout=2) as executor:
 data_list = [(d['experiment'], d['channels'], d['channels_to_transform'], d['channels_to_outliers'], d['channels_to_scale'], d['types_of_cells'], d['classes_column'], d['therapies'], d['mol_profiles'], d['scaling_type'], d['best_parameters'], d['balanced_acc_train'], d['balanced_acc_test'], d['f1_train'], d['f1_test'], d['most_predictive_features'], d['eliminated_features'], d['permutation_scores'], d['random_seed'], name, exps, df) for i, d in exps.iterrows()]
 
 # Perform multiple machine learning runs in parallel using executor.map()
 results = list(executor.map(run_ml_experiments, data_list))

 # Update the experiment DataFrame with the results obtained from the machine learning runs
 for result in results:
 exp_name = result['exp_name']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'best_parameters'] = str(result['best_parameters'])
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'balanced_acc_train'] = result['balanced_acc_train']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'balanced_acc_test'] = result['balanced_acc_test']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'f1_train'] = result['f1_train']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'f1_test'] = result['f1_test']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'most_predictive_features'] = str(result['most_predictive_features'])

exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'f1_train'] = result['f1_train']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'f1_test'] = result['f1_test']
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'most_predictive_features'] = str(result['most_predictive_features'])
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'eliminated_features'] = str(result['eliminated_features'])
 exps.loc[exps['experiment'] == exp_name, 'permutation_scores'] = str(result['permutation_scores'])

 # Save the updated DataFrame to experiments file
 exps.to_csv(name, index=False)
 logger.info("\nStored successfully experiments' file")

**Brief description**

This folder contains files related with the whole slide tissue tCycif
staining for the validation of NKI project results and for the TRIBUS
validation set

The samples correspond to the Oncosys-ova cohort and were selected by
Ada and Anniina. The description of the panel used is in the file 'Copy
of 4_2_NKI_exp.xlsx'

**Any questions about this files ask:**

Fernando Perez

Ada Junquera

Ziqi Kang

**File description**

*Copy of 4_2_NKI_exp.xlsx* - File that describes the panel used and the
list of samples used.

*Logic_table_Ada_Fernando_tribus.xlsx* - Logic table to annotate cells
using TRIBUS.

**Where is the data**

The data is in the workstation DH9-3049-11090

In the folder:

**Where are the analysis scripts**

The analysis scripts are in the repository
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts)

And the scripts are:

## Defining project strategy 

### [08/06/2020]{.underline}

Questions resolved with Julia:

\- Images are too big and we don\'t have enough disk space. Should we
ignore some channels for the pixel probmap?

Yes

\- CD15 (channel 30) is a bit messy. What should we do in that case?

Remove it if it's something in all cores. Take note if it's only in one.

\- How to define background? Check all channels if there is no signal?

Use background channels. BG488, BG555, BG647.

\- Sometimes the nuclei move, should we use only one nuclei channel for
nuclei pixel probmap?

Yes. But we should detect which one could be the best nuclei to use

k all channels if there is no signal?

Use background channels. BG488, BG555, BG647.

\- Sometimes the nuclei move, should we use only one nuclei channel for
nuclei pixel probmap?

Yes. But we should detect which one could be the best nuclei to use

\- Nuclei pixel probability in another task?

Yes. We will create one Ilastik project for no-membrane and Membrane.
And another for nuclei.

[Additional point to note:]{.underline}

We can write rentangles in OMERO to identify where artifacts are like
bubbles, hairs and overexposure. Then we can download those annotations.
And remove those cells with artifacts.

#### Identifying quality of channels

I have created a document "Notes about the quality of cores" in which I
put notes about the quality of the cores and enlist issues like hairs,
bubbles and other issues.

By default we will use DNA chanel 1 to get the pixel probability maps
except if other thing is indicated in the document "Notes about the
quality of cores"

### [16/06/2020]{.underline}

#### We will use nuclei segmentation in the project

In reunion with Annina and Ella, we agreed to use the common approach
for detecting cells, that means generating pixel probabilities for
nuclei and segmenting nuclei.

#### [No success allowing YAPIC to use CUDA in Linux]{.underline}

In the Linux part of our windows workstations it is not possible to use
CUDA.

Xming can allow you to open some programs using the graphical interface.
But the Linux partition can not load the CUDA.

### [03/07/2020]{.underline}

#### [Ella finished labelling]{.underline}

Ella has been labelling using nuclei and taking in account LaminB.

We did three approaches to measured which were the best pixel
probabilities:

- Using DAPI

- Using DAPI and LaminB

- Denoising DAPI using OpenCV and LamindB (using the script
 > *Denoising_smoothing.py*)

sing nuclei and taking in account LaminB.

We did three approaches to measured which were the best pixel
probabilities:

- Using DAPI

- Using DAPI and LaminB

- Denoising DAPI using OpenCV and LamindB (using the script
 > *Denoising_smoothing.py*)

We noticed that the denoising was not perfect and was producing some
"waves" close to the nucleus, those were affecting the pixel probability
maps. The best approach detected was "Using DAPI and LaminB".

Ella also used some labels from the ilastik-thon and "fixed" them to
make them more similar to their labels. Their labels and ilastik project
are in Datacloud in "Projects/NKI_arrays/Ella_final"

#### [Starting training with UNET]{.underline}

I used the ilastik project generated by Ella to train UNET, using YAPIC.
For this, I created in my Workstation in the Ubuntu_18.04 environment
the folder /home/fperez/yapic_Ella-training

I will run 500 epochs of training. The script used for running it is
\`running.sh\` and it's inside the folder. Yapic will not be able to use
the CUDA, the programmers recommend to run it using 5000 epochs
([[https://yapic.github.io/yapic/]{.underline}](https://yapic.github.io/yapic/)),
but that would take 2months using CPUs.

### [07/07/2020]{.underline}

#### [Worry about UNET log]{.underline}

YAPIC has been running for several days. By now it has run almost half
of the iterations, but the log says that the accuracy of the model
created is around 10 percent. That's bad, previously in ilastik-thon the
training was around 0.9 percent, and in my first approach it was 60
percent. Most probably the model created will not be good. So, we should
start considering an alternative.

Alternatives:

1) Use CSC computers with GPUs and run Yapic with at least 5000 epochs.

2) Run several ilastik test's

#### [Ilastik tests]{.underline}

Running ilastik in batch-mode with the project created by Ella, produced
many blurry pixel classifications in the validation sets:

IMAGE

So, I'm creating a project using DAPI in high resolution (32bits) and
importing her labels, and running it again in batch mode. Although this
results are better than with LaminB, those are still blurry and some
nuclei lost resulution:

IMAGE

I think the model could require more labeling after all, and maybe
remove some labels which were dependent on LaminB or label more nuclei,
which in the interior have a lot of LaminB.

#### [Creating VM in CSC]{.underline}

After some hours of learning and creating and deleting VMs. Manage to:

1. Create a .pem key.

2. Install YAPIC using the right CUDA version and Tensorflow version

For that first:

1. Create VM using flavor "gpu1.1gpu", use the mirror Ubuntu16.04-cuda

2. Install CUDA toolkit v10.0 using the next commands:

sudo apt-get install cuda-10-0

sudo reboot 0

cd /usr/local/cuda/samples

sudo make -k

3. Then install Anaconda

wget
[[https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/Anaconda2-2019.10-Linux-x86_64.sh]{.underline}](https://repo.anaconda.com/archive/Anaconda2-2019.10-Linux-x86_64.sh)

conda init bash

sudo reboot 0

4. Then install YAPIC using anaconda

conda create -n yapic tensorflow-gpu python=3.6

conda activate yapic

pip install yapic

I created two VM with GPUs and transferred the images and ilastik
project to them. In one of the VM I runned Yapic for images with only
Dapi. In the other VM I runned Yapic with images that has laminB and
Dapi.

## Matching clinical information to the cores

### [09/11/2020]{.underline}

Previously I was using just the CoresIDs without any information about
which one of those corresponds to the same patients. Since september 25
we started to merge the clinical info with the Cycif cores IDs working
with Lilian

First I generated for each slide CSV tables with the core IDs ordered by
row and columns. For this I made the script
"CreateCSV_cores_in_matrix.R" and then I adjusted the results by hand.
The results of this are in the folder "RegistrationMaps" in the
Datacloud folder:
[[https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/s/noN3DfQERoWjZXN]{.underline}](https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/s/noN3DfQERoWjZXN)

Lilian gave me access to a portal called slidescore
[[https://rhpc.nki.nl/slidescore/Studies/Details/75]{.underline}](https://rhpc.nki.nl/slidescore/Studies/Details/75)
In this portal researchers from NKI manage all the samples of their
projects. In this portal we can see the slides, sample names (after
clicking the cores) and export and excel files that organize by row and
column the sample names corresponding for each of those cores.

![](media/image3.png){width="3.0989588801399823in"
height="1.889232283464567in"}

Then with Lilian we proceeded to match the excels for each Cycif slide
with the sample names generated by slidescore. We noticed that some
Cycif cores were absent (we named NDs, not detected) and others were
normal samples (OC livers). There are some notes about the absent cores
in the file "CoreNotes.txt" in
D:\\users\\fperez\\NKI_TMAs_AF\\Clinical_intersection . The final file
with Lilian comments is: "Kopie van TMA_Cycif_ids.xlsx", use this to
match with the file "TMA CFMPB 297 all maps.xlsx" .

es about the absent cores
in the file "CoreNotes.txt" in
D:\\users\\fperez\\NKI_TMAs_AF\\Clinical_intersection . The final file
with Lilian comments is: "Kopie van TMA_Cycif_ids.xlsx", use this to
match with the file "TMA CFMPB 297 all maps.xlsx" .

The matching of files was done with the script: "match_samplesIDs.R",
inside D:\\users\\fperez\\NKI_TMAs_AF\\Clinical_intersection\\. For each
of the slides the script produced the files: "\_cycif2samples.txt".

## Ligandomics

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we assessed the activity of
MHCII signature epithelial/cancer cells by computing the normalized
Ucell score using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) v1.3.1 in single
cell RNA sequencing cohort (done by Matias). The MHCII signature
comprised of the following genes: *HLA-DMA*, *HLA-DMB*, *HLA-DOA*,
*HLA-DOB*, *HLA-DPA1*, *HLA-DPB1*, *HLA-DQA1*, *HLA-DQA2*, *HLA-DQB1*,
*HLA-DRA*, *HLA-DRB1*, *HLA-DRB5*, *CD74.* Based on the MHCII Ucell
scores, the samples were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no
expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). MultiNicheNetR
([[https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git]{.underline}](https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high

b.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high
and low MHCII UCell expression samples (done by Aleksandra and Gayani).
Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate
representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples
per group. Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a
minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction
cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis
was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types,
applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤
0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed
down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for
immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using the
GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and
receptor were present. We correlated using Pearson correlation the
previously calculated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand
expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand
expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Correlation p-values
were adjusted for multiple testing. The corresponding scripts can be
found in github:
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts/tree/main/10_LR_analysis]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts/tree/main/10_LR_analysis)

## Immuno peptidomics

*01/2025* Cerrulo's laboratory (Jacopo and Salvatore) performed
immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from
iCAN study. The selected sample info is located on G-drive:
[[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1_Umqw5b8cDr_h-ZkfTCM0R400uD7lt2rU8uoS4yViMs/edit?gid=0#gid=0]{.underline}](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1_Umqw5b8cDr_h-ZkfTCM0R400uD7lt2rU8uoS4yViMs/edit?gid=0#gid=0).
Anastasia prepared around 1g of material for each sample.

MHC complex purification was performed as previously described
[(](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7Lf2IK)[*Chiaro J, Antignani G,
Feola S, Feodoroff M, Martins B, Cojoc H, et al. Development of
Mesothelioma-Specific Oncolytic Immunotherapy Enabled by
Immunopeptidomics of Murine and Human Mesothelioma Tumors. Nat Commun.
Nature Publishing Group;
2023;14:7056*](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Jg93Bh)[)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7Lf2IK)
with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were
slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell
suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a
70 μm cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then
subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis
buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease
inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal
(Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via
centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was
subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The
clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column

resulting lysate was initially cleared via
centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was
subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The
clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column
(Thermo Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled
to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.)
using the manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To
enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice
in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column
volumes of the following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM
Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM
Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes
bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using
0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted
using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were
preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA),
followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of
30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene
screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum
centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway
dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto
an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1

e CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1
min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),
with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs
cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired
with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the
minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at \< 700 m/z
or 3 Th \>800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000,
considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was
lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59
eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard
polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to
exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using
a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard
(ThermoFisher Scientific).

Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational
suite (RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.

ine}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.
MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC
peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications:
enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and 'null', allowing up to
two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and
30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da, and charge states from +1
to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a
variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches
and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher
(https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher), applying a false discovery
rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and
peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant
(https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run
normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with
default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1,
while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides
confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis.
Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting
from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286,
[https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant]{.underline}) with
default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. The

## Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data

*11/2025 (by Ziqi)* RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples
used for immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with
the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more
than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing

was performed on the 12 HGSC samples
used for immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with
the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more
than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing
data were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the
iCAN study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM
(RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale
factors, and cell-type--specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor,
stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for
PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell
RNA-seq dataset from [(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?s0IsHW),
which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29
patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the
tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample.

*11/2025 (by Aleksandra)* After decomposition, immune and stromal
components were combined to generate an immune--stromal profile. Scaled
expression values for each gene and each compartment (tumor and
immune--stromal) were computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors,
which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and
library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative
contribution of each compartment within a given sample. Scaled
expression therefore represents the expression level expected for each
cell type independent of its abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled
expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from
immunopeptidomics. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII
cancer groups based on matched t-CycIF imaging (as described in the
Immunopeptidomics section).

bulk sample. Scaled
expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from
immunopeptidomics. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII
cancer groups based on matched t-CycIF imaging (as described in the
Immunopeptidomics section). For each gene, the median scaled expression
and median z-score of scaled expression were calculated within each
patient group. Tumor specificity of gene expression in the MHCII-high
group was then assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the
tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the
tumor and immune--stromal compartments combined. This yields a value
between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression
predominantly in tumor fraction. Finally, fold change was calculated as
the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of
MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions
of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples. The used scripts can be found on
github:
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts/tree/main/12_Tumor_antigens/Peptides_presence]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts/tree/main/12_Tumor_antigens/Peptides_presence) -

04_PRISM_Deconv_analysis_of_antigen_gene_exp.Rmd

**iPDCs flow cytometry analysis & Statistical analyses for blocking
experiment**

Frozen tumor-derived samples from patients with high MHCII expression
were selected from the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective HGSC cohort. The tumor
tissue had been enzymatically dissociated prior to cryopreservation. For
iPDC generation, thawed cells were embedded as droplets in an
omentum-derived extracellular matrix. Cultures were treated with
Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination treatment, anti-HLA
antibody (2 µg/mL) was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL)
subsequently added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC
droplets were harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell
suspensions. Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for
lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam) and effector function (Granzyme B,
IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and

issociation to obtain single-cell
suspensions. Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for
lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam) and effector function (Granzyme B,
IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and
doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating. Data were
acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT
voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in
FlowJo v10.10.0, applying a consistent gating strategy across all
samples. Following gating in FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported
as CSV files and subsequently analyzed in R.

Statistical analyses were performed in RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build
418) using R (version 4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from
FlowJo were processed for each cell type to extract marker expression
(IFNg, GrzB) along with associated metadata, such as patient id and
condition. Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical
comparisons between conditions. Results are presented as a heatmap
showing mean log2 fold change of marker expression across pooled
patients, with statistically significant differences indicated
(*p* \< 0.05).

Protocols and other relevant documents, raw data, R scripts to generate
the plots, and generated plots can be found on the P Drive:

## Slide 4
Detection at advanced stage
High grade serous ovarian cancers (HGSC) patients have a poor clinical prognosis
TCGA; 
Tumor stage
at diagnosis
Lack of treatment options
weillcornell.org
The standard of care is: Surgery + platinum chemotherapy (or PARP inhibitors)

5-year survival rate is less than 40%
‹#›

## Slide 5
HGSC is characterized by common molecular alterations clinically relevant
Around 20% of samples have a BRCA1/2 alteration conferring a DNA-repair Homologous  recombination deficiency (HRD) 

Around 30% of samples have HRD alterations beyond BRCA1/2
Good responders to PARPi. 
Immune-inflamed tumors.
All these tumors have a TP53 mutation/alteration and harbour genomic lesions.
Poor responders to PARPi. 
Low (cold) immuned tumors.
CCNE1amp amplification in ~15%

The rest are HR proficient (HRP)
‹#›

## Slide 6
Despite the high immune infiltration in HGSC, the response to 
immune checkpoint inhibitors has been very modest
E. Kandalaft, et al, 2022

## **Abstract**

## The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant--host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.

## **INTRODUCTION**

The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions
[^1^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GKAflH). The continuous
crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is

cosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions
[^1^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GKAflH). The continuous
crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is
fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression,
and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC)
is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer
[^2,3^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?J8T2j5), characterized by
high genomic instability
[^4,5^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UZHLvb) and significant
tumor heterogeneity [^6^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?TW0MVf).
This heterogeneity is evident in various aspects, such as
histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME cellular
composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy
[^7^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?cy7HVb). The genetic diversity
of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors
with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as
those with *BRCA1/2* mutations, have been suggested to be more
immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration
[^8,9^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9ZXefK). Emerging evidence
also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) tumors possess distinct immune
escape mechanisms [^10^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ZOsCFd) and
unique responses to combinations with immunotherapies
[^11,12^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Kkdr2C). Moreover, we and
others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate
interactions within the immune compartment
[^13--15^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?E8yfUf), resulting in

r, we and
others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate
interactions within the immune compartment
[^13--15^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?E8yfUf), resulting in
spatially confined T-cell exhaustion
[^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?PeYuGV). Therefore, it is
crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and
molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve
patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for
patients with HGSC.

We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1
million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with
comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging
DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a
tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the
single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that
associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning
tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to
uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of
cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at
the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of
immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate
immune hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis
independently of clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the
interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms
linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in
HGSC.

## **RESULTS**

### **Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors**

To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography,
clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic
profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing,
gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex
imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (**Fig. 1a**).
The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from
treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after
platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS
n=158). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven patients.
Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were
collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months)
(**S. Table 1**). Most patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and
paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free
survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months).

We conducted a hierarchical multidimensional analysis to assign the
tumors' molecular profiles. Firstly, *BRCA1/2* germline or somatic
mutation status was assessed using targeted high-throughput sequencing,
and *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation was evaluated using a targeted DNA
methylation assay. Tumors without *BRCA*loss (*BRCA1/2* somatic or
germline mutation, or *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation) were tested for
Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and *CCNE1*-amplification

lation was evaluated using a targeted DNA
methylation assay. Tumors without *BRCA*loss (*BRCA1/2* somatic or
germline mutation, or *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation) were tested for
Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and *CCNE1*-amplification
using shallow whole genome sequencing and dedicated algorithms (see
Methods). We categorized 174 tumors (**S. Fig. 1a)** into four
clinically and molecularly relevant groups (*BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous
Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification*)
[^17^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JzylKC). The rest (n=91) of
the tumors were left "unassigned" as they could not be reliably
categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see Methods).

To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used
hyperplexed imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)
targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing tissue

cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping scheme
(**S. Fig. 1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell phenotyping

To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells
[^21^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QFXbxo), we clustered the
cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell phenotypic markers,
mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell morphology features. Using
unsupervised consensus clustering (see Methods), we uncovered four
distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations (**Fig. 1k to m**). These
populations exhibited distinct molecular and morphological
characteristics. The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high
expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the
Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell
area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle
cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers
(**Fig. 1k, l; Ext. material - Fig. 1, 2**).

We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the
defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all
molecular groups did not reveal statistically significant differences
after multiple hypothesis testing (**S. Fig. 2a, S. Table 3**). However,

consistent with previous reports
[^8,22^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UYqibr), we observed that
*BRCA*loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and CD11c.MY,
specifically after chemotherapy exposure (**Fig. 2a**). Notably, a
higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed exclusively in
*BRCA1*loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples (**Fig. 2a**). When
comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found that CD8+ T cells,
effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages were
enriched in IDS samples (**S. Fig. 2b**). Interestingly, all lymphocyte
populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated significant
co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (**Fig. 2b, S. Table 4,
5**). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages, did not
exhibit this co-infiltration (**Fig. 2b**). These results imply that
lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated process
potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms.

We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with
clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the
abundance of lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival
(OS) (**Fig. 2c**, **S. Fig. 2c**) and progression-free survival (PFS)
(**S. Fig. 2d**). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells
also showed association with longer PFS (**S. Fig. 2d**). Notably, in
IDS tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic
significance, suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to

). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells
also showed association with longer PFS (**S. Fig. 2d**). Notably, in
IDS tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic
significance, suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to
chemotherapy exposure beyond immune cell abundance are relevant for
prognosis, as previously suggested
[^23,24^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dYxZqE). To note, the
highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the
interpretability of individual and multivariable hazard ratios.

For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant
differences across the molecular profiles (**S. Fig. 2e**). However, the
EMT phenotype was enriched in BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS
samples (**S. Fig. 2e**). The relative abundance of these cancer cell
phenotypes was associated with OS (**Fig. 2d**) and survival PFS (**S.
Fig. 2f**), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For
example, a higher abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was
associated with shorter OS in IDS tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT
cluster was linked to improved OS in PDS but not in IDS (**Fig. 2d**).
We next performed multivariable Cox models using stricter inclusion
criteria for patients in the analyses (minimum of two tumor cores and
more than 5% of cancer cells) (**S. Fig. 1b**) further confirming
differential associations with clinical outcomes (**S. Fig. 2g**).
Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship between immune
cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the
clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor\'s
exposure to chemotherapy.

**Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
chemotherapy exposure**

To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells
based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We
uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter
referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN) (**Fig. 2e to
g**). Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal
(RCN01 to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells
adjacent to cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09
and RCN10) and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal
spatial domains included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and
an endothelial cell-rich domain (RCN03) (**Fig. 2e to g**). The cancer
spatial domains included the phenotypically most heterogeneous
neighbourhood (RCN04), and four neighbourhoods were predominated by
distinct tumor cell functional states (**Fig. 2e to g**). The immune
spatial domains included an immune B cell neighborhood (RCN13),
tentatively consisting of tertiary lymphoid structures (**S. Fig. 2h**),
and neighborhood of myeloid cells (RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11) (**Fig. 2e
to g**). These spatial domains were validated with visual inspection of
the t-CycIF images (**Fig. 2f, g; S. Fig. 2h**).\
\
Spatial composition analysis revealed that several of the domains were
intercorrelated, suggesting spatially coordinated TME domains (**Fig.
2h, S. Table 6, 7**). For example, two immune domains (RCN11 - T cells,
RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation with each other, as well

led that several of the domains were
intercorrelated, suggesting spatially coordinated TME domains (**Fig.
2h, S. Table 6, 7**). For example, two immune domains (RCN11 - T cells,
RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation with each other, as well
as with the immune-infiltrated stroma (RCN02). In contrast, the
immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation with these immune
domains (**Fig. 2h**). The cancer cell domains also revealed significant
correlations with other spatial domains. For instance, the neighborhood
of cancer idle phenotype (RCN01) was positively correlated with the
immune-cold tumor-stromal interface (TSI), whereas the neighborhood of
cancer cells displaying immunogenic phenotypes was negatively correlated
with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01) (**Fig. 2h**). These findings
suggest that the spatial domains of the TME are spatially coordinated in
HGSC.

We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the
clinicomolecular profiles. The overall comparisons did not reveal
significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple
hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most
heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in
*BRCA*loss tumors (**Fig. 2i, j**). Interestingly, comparison of the
spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of
stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01 and RCN02), and
immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and
proliferative tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples (**Fig.
2k**). Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less
abundant in the IDS samples (**Fig. 2k**) suggesting phenotypic
convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy,
consistent with our previous observation

**Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical
outcomes**

Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the
highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, **Fig. 2e**),
exhibiting the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs.
Statistical comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that
immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of T
cells, myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence
of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI (**Fig. 2l**). Consistent
with our previous observations
[^8^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KJYDUG), the *BRCA*loss tumors
contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated TSI (**Fig. 2j**).
By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was more abundant in the
IDS samples compared to PDS (**Fig. 2k**), was significantly enriched in
fibroblast stromal cells (**Fig. 2l**). Additionally, the immune-cold
TSI showed a higher prevalence of \'Idle\'-type cancer cells lacking
activity-linked phenotypic marker expressions, indicative of an
\'immune-evasive\' TSI where cancer cells could evade the recognition of
the immune system (**Fig. 2l**). Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI
(RCN10) had significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched

ker expressions, indicative of an
\'immune-evasive\' TSI where cancer cells could evade the recognition of
the immune system (**Fig. 2l**). Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI
(RCN10) had significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched
proliferative, immunogenic and EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive
"immune escape" mechanisms at the TSI
[^25^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kyN7aO).

Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical
outcomes. Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy
sequence (PDS/IDS), we identified three RCNs (RCN03, RCN09, RCN10)
significantly associated with both PFS and OS (**S. Fig. 2i, j**).
Notably, the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition
emerged as a key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across
the entire patient cohort (**Fig. 2m**). Specifically, a high abundance
of the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved
prognosis, whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated
with poorer outcomes (**Fig. 2m**).

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial
domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes
within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse
spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The
intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial
organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a
coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the
necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features
associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.

### **CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features**

To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune
infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature
Importance Identification by RAndom forest), a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) framework (**Fig. 3a**). The primary objective of
CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within
high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample
groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and
the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter
tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training
and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent
overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a
proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3)
Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen
data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to
minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features
using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the
previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve
a consensus estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source
software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional data from each sample,
such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles
(**Fig. 3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig.

ach sample,
such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles
(**Fig. 3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig. 1e, i; S. Fig. 1g**), morphological features and
spatial neighborhoods (**Fig. 1j**), resulting in 43 feature
representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover
multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we
stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium,
and short OS (**S. Fig. 3a,b**). We generated CEFIIRA models for either
cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like
(*BRCA*loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, *CCNE1*amp), and in PDS or IDS
tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential
confounders. As expected, the patient\'s age and tumor stage were the
top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (**S. Fig.
3c,d**). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited better
classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in both
PDS (**Fig. 3b, S. Table 8**) and IDS (**Fig. 3c, S. Table 8**),
indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential
prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell
spatial data.

### **Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with of overall survival** 

Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as
one of the key features for long or short OS prediction in both PDS and
IDS samples (**S. Fig. 3c,d**). Notably, of the key features, cancer
cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment
in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients
(**Fig.

hort OS prediction in both PDS and
IDS samples (**S. Fig. 3c,d**). Notably, of the key features, cancer
cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment
in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients
(**Fig. 3d, e; S. Table 8**). By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin,
α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples
were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression
was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (**Fig. 3d**). The
prevalence of stromal and immune markers in cancer cell models suggested
the relevance of signals from neighboring cells at the TSI. Indeed,
visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed MHCII expression by
cancer cells, and that the Vimentin, CD3d, and PD-1 signals were
primarily signals originating from distinct neighboring cells adjacent
to the cancer cells (**Fig. 3f; S. Fig. 3e**). In addition, MHCII was
also the top predictive marker in the immune-stromal cells in IDS
samples (**Fig. 3e)**. Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII
and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial
proximity, are relevant for OS prediction regardless of therapy
sequence.

Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular
profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of
individual cells. We observed that PDS samples demonstrated superior
classification performance than IDS samples (**Fig. 3g**, **S. Fig.
3f**). In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher
expression in the HRD-like tumors (**Fig. 3h, S. Fig. 3g**). The CEFIIRA
model to classify *BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest
predictive performance (Median F1=0.77, **Fig. 3g**), with MHCII being

odels, MHCII was a key feature with higher
expression in the HRD-like tumors (**Fig. 3h, S. Fig. 3g**). The CEFIIRA
model to classify *BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest
predictive performance (Median F1=0.77, **Fig. 3g**), with MHCII being
the most differential feature displaying higher expression in *BRCA*loss
cancer cells (**S. Fig. 3g, h**), also confirmed from the t-CycIF images
(**Fig. 3i**). These results indicate that cancer-intrinsic MHCII
expression is enriched in the HRD tumors.

We next aimed to characterize the heterogeneity of MHCII expression both
across and within tumors. We observed that 17% of cancer cells exhibited
MHCII expression levels comparable to or exceeding those of CD11c+MY
cells (**S. Fig. 3i**). Tumors showed variable proportions of MHCII+
cancer cells (**Fig. 3j**), with these proportions positively correlated
with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer cells (R = 0.45, p = 1.7e-13; **S.
Fig. 3j**). However, several tumors displayed high MHCII expression
coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant pattern, as
confirmed through t-CycIF image analysis (**S. Fig. 3k, l**). This
suggests that MHCII expression in cancer cells can occur independently
of MHCI expression.

To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted
Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not
assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA
analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells consistently predicted longer OS (**Fig. 3k**), further
strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and
prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy
sequence.

(TCGA)[^5^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ooK6Nh), by inferring

MHCII expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of improved
clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct tumor-immune
interactions within the TME.

### **Cancer MHCII expression associates with immune infiltration** 

To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune
populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor.
This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells
associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and
M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (**Fig. 4a**). To corroborate these

to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor.
This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells
associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and
M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (**Fig. 4a**). To corroborate these
findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer
cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted
data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found that tumors with high
MHCII expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T
cells and M1-like macrophages (**S. Fig. 4a**). In contrast, tumors with
high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like
macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for
anti-tumor responses (**S. Fig. 4a**). Overall, these results
demonstrate that HGSC tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer
cells have more immune infiltration in the TME.

To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial

revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (**S. Fig. 4f**),
abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers,
including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (**Fig. 1l**). This indicates that
MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the

CII expression within RCN05 (**S. Fig. 4f**),
abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers,
including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (**Fig. 1l**). This indicates that
MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the
cancer cell spatial compartments.

We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII
expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two
TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII
in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to
the immune-cold TSI (RCN9) (**S. Fig. 4f**). Consistently, analysis of
the cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells
resided more often in close proximity to immune cells as compared to
MHCII- cancer cells across the diverse clinicomolecular profiles (**Fig.
4f, S. Fig. 4g**). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly
neighboring stromal cells. Visual inspection of the tumor tissues
confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune
cells, forming immune 'hotspots', while MHCII- cells were embedded in
immune 'cold' tumor niches (**Fig. 4g**).

To expand our analyses to whole tissue sections, we generated

samples (**Fig. 4h**) from an independent cohort in the Oncosys-OVA

cells using the TRIBUS (**Fig. 4i,** **S. Fig. 4h**). In line with

expression within and across the tumors (**S. Fig. 4i,j**).
Consistently, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells associated with
longer progression-free survival (PFS) (**Fig. 4j**). To note, CD8+

To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer
cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the matched tumor and adjacent

deconvolution, we observed that tumor MHCII expression was associated
with a significantly higher inferred proportion of particularly
dendritic cells and T cells in the adjacent stroma of the TSI (**Fig.
4m,n**). Altogether, these findings demonstrate that MHCII+ cancer cells
colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains and associate with spatial
immune infiltration and antigen presentation at the TSI.

### **Cancer-intrinsic MHCII associates with single-cell and spatial immune activation**

Next, we set out to uncover transcriptional programs that underpin the
immune infiltration associated with the MHCII+ cancer cells.
Interestingly, tumors with a high MHCII expression exhibited upregulated
genes related to immune activation, including chemokines, cytokines,
immune checkpoints, granzymes, and immune receptors, compared to tumors
with low MHCII expression in the TCGA dataset
[^31^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Zj0zB2) (**S. Fig. 5a, b, S.
Table 9**). Notably, we observed a significantly augmented expression of
the immune checkpoint molecules TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+
tumors. Gene overrepresentation analysis
[^32^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Nb4L3T) revealed 14 enriched
pathways related to immune activation (**Fig. 5a**), including both PD-1
and chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways were enriched
within the under-expressed genes. Consistently, in DEG analysis of
cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (**Fig. 4b**), we found that MHCII
positivity was associated with upregulation of the tumor-suppressors
*RARRES3* and *CLU* associated with immune infiltration
[^33--35^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?CY52Q6) (**S. Fig. 5c**).

Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort.
For this, we utilized expression data of 335 genes profiled with the
Nanostring technology
[^36^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?aLNeNd). Consistently, a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of
chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (**S.
Fig. 5d, S. Table 10**). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11

[^37--39^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?C6OoZ9), were among the

To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ
