### **Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity and enhanced immunotherapy response *in vitro***

Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when
antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous
misfolded proteins [^46^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1PQcHJ).
To explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with
specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified
antigens detected via HLA ligandome analysis in HGSC
[^47^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LctNso) (**Fig. 6a**). We

lore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with
specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified
antigens detected via HLA ligandome analysis in HGSC
[^47^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LctNso) (**Fig. 6a**). We
found that the deconvoluted expression of MHCII in cancer cells is
indeed associated with the expression of most antigen-encoding genes in
the TCGA dataset (**Fig. 6b**). However, in our scRNA-seq dataset, only
four specific antigen genes (UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN) were expressed
in the cancer cells, and associated with tumor MHCII expression (**Fig.
6c**). In our GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also observed
positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the
expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at
the TSI (**Fig. 6d**).

To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in
HGSC, we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides in
12 HGSC samples alongside TCR repertoire analysis based on bulk RNA-seq
(**Fig. 6e**). We identified the presence of previously reported genes
from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets that showed
significant correlations and were consistently observed across the
patients (**Fig. 6f**). Moreover, the genes enriched in samples with a
high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells were significantly enriched in
cellular components related to junctions, adhesion, the extracellular
matrix, and cell--cell contact zones, as well as in biological processes
involved in protein localization and regulation (**Fig. 6g,h**). To
explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen
presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We

contact zones, as well as in biological processes
involved in protein localization and regulation (**Fig. 6g,h**). To
explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen
presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We
observed a significantly increased number of T cell receptor
clones---specifically TRAD and TRB ---in samples with a high MHCII
cancer proportion (**Fig. 6i**).

Given this association with enhanced T cell presence, we next
investigated whether tumor MHCII status influences CD8+ T-cell responses
to immunotherapy using our previously established immuno-oncology
platform of three-dimensional patient-derived immune-competent cultures
(iPDCs) ^16^. For this, we analysed flow cytometry data from drug
perturbation assays of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five
chemo-treated tumor samples (**Fig. 6j**). The iPDCs were stratified
into MHCII-high and MHCII-low groups based on baseline HLA-DR expression
in EpCAM+ cancer cells compared to immune and myeloid populations (see
**Methods** and **S. Fig. 6a,b**). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited
significantly greater CD8+ T-cell cytotoxic responses to pembrolizumab
(**Fig. 6k, S. Table 11**). Sample-by-sample analysis revealed a
statistically significant cytotoxicity gain (p \< 0.05) between baseline
and pembrolizumab-treated conditions in the MHCII-high group (**Fig.
6l**).

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen
presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of
distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to
T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore
contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context
of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.

## **DISCUSSION**

We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem
using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280
real-world clinical samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified
distinct spatial domains with varying phenotypic compositions. These
intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor cell phenotypic
profiles and clinicomolecular groups, underscoring the role of molecular
features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the
two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated
with cancer cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while
the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to
poorer outcomes.

Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated
high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key
prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of \~15.1 million
cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells
within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate immune
hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity
with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly
with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells
have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and
breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
[^48,49^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WgCdGj), and the spatial
contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells was reported

been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and
breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
[^48,49^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WgCdGj), and the spatial
contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells was reported
recently in triple-negative breast cancer
[^50^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NltuKr). While IFN expression
in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent
evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
[^51^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?aKDvHz). Through a robust
spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially
cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and
the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII
correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory
T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present
antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that
MHCII and *IFN-γ* expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated
that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of
neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at
the TSI. These findings, summarized in **Fig. 7**, support the
hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and
CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at
the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC.

MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under
evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in
antigen presentation [^52^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?vso6Hh).
In HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs

n reported previously to be under
evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in
antigen presentation [^52^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?vso6Hh).
In HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs
undergoing whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with
poor prognosis [^53^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SbNXa0).
Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an
improved response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung
cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer
[^41,50,54,55^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7zpUhp). However,
clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected
patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated only modest
benefits [^56^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?I8lvh7). In
contrast, our *in vitro* analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs
suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might
enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic
activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our
study revealed that other immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT
and CTLA4, were overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression,
further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune
checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC
[^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?mvREEE). Moreover, we showed
that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors,
and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality,
supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While
tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity

ptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors,
and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality,
supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While
tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity
cycle via active antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches
should be developed for patients with tumors lacking MHCII expression.
In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential
mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the
clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future
therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.

**Study limitations**

We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the
opportunities for future refinement and exploration. The retrospective
design and reliance on archival tissues may introduce biases related to
sample preservation and heterogeneity, limiting some of the statistical
comparisons between patients. Our protein marker panel, while
comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential
cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in
independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of
deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic
and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating
robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve
predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable
hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger
prospective patient cohorts.

## **MATERIALS & METHODS**

### ***Cohort description***

We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV
tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek
Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and
Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and
December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive
surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval
debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based
chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or
PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from
the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained
from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA)
^57^.

Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking
surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by
three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were
extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE
blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with
one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from
the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
previously described [^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gaaOUU)

eas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
previously described [^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gaaOUU)
and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration
detected with t-CycIF.

### ***Whole slide cohort description***

We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the
ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from
patients during interval debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection
criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status, a tumor purity
greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS)
of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R \>
0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that
underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova
clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to
define long-PFS (\>=12 months) and short-PFS (\<12 months) in the
survival analysis.

### ***Molecular profile assignment***

For the TMA cohort, we first identified *BRCA1/2* mutation and *BRCA1*
promoter methylation status of each sample as previously described
[^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SdxgQX). In brief, *BRCA1/2*
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the
Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a
*BRCA1/2* germline mutation, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and

on, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and
Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent
probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation.
Details of the number of tumors with *BRCA1* or *BRCA2* mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter methylation are reported in **S. Table 1**.

Next, tumors without a *BRCA1/2* mutation or *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using
Illumina HiSeq2500 to determine a *BRCA*-like CNV profile
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qGUBcI) and
*CCNE1*-amplification status as described previously
[^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y06swk). In addition, using
the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software
shallowHRD [^59^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6XQwP9). Finally,
we categorized the tumors as *BRCAloss*, HRD, HRP, and *CCNE1*amp using
a hierarchical approach described in **S. Fig. 1a**. Samples that failed
QC for *BRCA1/2* targeted DNA-sequencing, *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV analysis, were labeled as "Unassigned"
(or Other). In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between
shallowHRD and the *BRCA-like* CNV profile
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y35pzZ) were labeled as
"Unassigned".

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
(RRID:SCR_027138) [^60^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?njGwIx).
For all analyses, only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological
grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
included.

RD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
(RRID:SCR_027138) [^60^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?njGwIx).
For all analyses, only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological
grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
included. For the whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically
validated HRD classification performed at the Helsinki University
Hospital, Finland, which uses ovaHRDscar, and the detection of somatic
and germline mutations and gene deletions in *BRCA1/2* genes.

### ***Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing***

To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor
tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the
whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and
the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots.
The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (**S. Table
2**) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the
t-CycIF protocol [^61^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?FYaR9w). The
panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main
cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images' illumination was
corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371)

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were
clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers
(Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the
R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and
cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the
0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel\'s signal
was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by
marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized
map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM
node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S. Fig. 1c**) by
using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell

bal cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using
unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367)
[^18^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ohnqa1). Outliers were
removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity,
normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5
[^68^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Td9ajN), and scaled by
Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S. Table 13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are
images S100_iOme and S110_iOme, which were manually gated with scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) [^69^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?0eresq) due
to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our
annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by
expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)
staining, showing agreement across samples (**S. Fig. 4l**). The MHCII+
cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the
annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### ***Cancer cell phenotype clustering***

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed
outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method
section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based
on the expression levels of the markers: \"Ki67\", \"Vimentin\",
\"pSTAT1\", \"MHC-I\", \"ECadherin\", \"yH2AX\", \"pTBK1\", \"cPARP1\",
\"CK7\", \"PDL1_488\", and morphological features: \"Area\",
\"Eccentricity\", and \"Roundness\" into a 2500-node self-organized map
(SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were
clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954).
For this, the number of clusters (*k*) was assessed from 2 to 12. For
each value of *k* assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of
the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and
morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm
"partitioning around medoids" (pam) was used to cluster the selected
dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best
number of cancer meta-clusters, *k*, by assessing the resultant
consensus-clustering matrix for each value of *k* and its cumulative
density distribution (**Ext. material - Fig. 3**). The cancer
meta-clusters were named according to the expression of relevant markers
in each one (**Ext. material - Fig. 2**).

### ***Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis***

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality
control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius
of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap

lar neighborhoods and spatial analysis***

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality
control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius
of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) [^69^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5Mos0I). We
clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate
recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k**=**40
employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package scimap. Every
cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value of k, we
used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick
[^70^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?B2IjP9) (**Ext. material -
Fig. 3**). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods
of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis
using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the
robustness of our approach (**Ext. material - Fig. 3**). The proportion
of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of
cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.

### ***Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis***

To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity
of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial
transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different
responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data,
we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341)
[^71^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JOgS7e). For the QC, we
excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected,

and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341)
[^71^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JOgS7e). For the QC, we
excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected,
more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin
genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat\'s
SCTransform function.

Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an
expert pathologist as 'Cancer', 'TSI' (tumor-stroma interface with
partial tumor and stromal morphology), 'Immune-Stromal' and 'other'.
Spots annotated as 'other' have been excluded from the analysis. Within
'Immune-Stromal' spots, we have distinguished an additional type -
'TSI2', defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to
either 'Cancer' or 'TSI' spots. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) ^44^. The MHCII
signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA,
HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA,
HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed
MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the
median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to
chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor
response was adopted from Böhm et al, 2015
[^72^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JpjFmE). An IFN-γ signature,
obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene
set collection (RRID:SCR_016863)
[^73^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v1TFTd), was also quantified
per spot.

https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JpjFmE). An IFN-γ signature,
obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene
set collection (RRID:SCR_016863)
[^73^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v1TFTd), was also quantified

### ***Machine learning supervised classification***

The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had
the following pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein
marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score);
(2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness),
scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the proportion of
neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type;
additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like
short- or long-OS the model included patients' clinical information --
FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker
and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range
were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than
100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent
machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling
was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20%
into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.

To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm
for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the
PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695,
[[https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret]{.underline}](https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret)).
We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of:

into molecular profiles, we used the
PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695,
[[https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret]{.underline}](https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret)).
We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of:
BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected
Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance
(weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (**S. Table
14**). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to determine the
number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S. Table 15**).

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python
scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577)
[^74^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XlLOJH) for binary
classification of cells into clinicomolecular groups
([https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA]{.underline}). The models\'
training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop
iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using
the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 30 data
partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model
robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve
similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based
on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (**S. Fig.
3n**). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a
Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model

fying long vs. short overall survival based
on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (**S. Fig.
3n**). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a
Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model
(*min_samples_split*, *min_samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of
hyperparameters' values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean
weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each
iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three
independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a
cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on
classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting
the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target
variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we
evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set
partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell
level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard
deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported.
Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells,
Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified
the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of
overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below
the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and

cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with PRISM and adopted from

[^75^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?C7Mdte).

### ***RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment***

For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read
counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463)
[^76^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RgUjj2) from the Genomics
Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also
obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository
[^75^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?I1JWZK). We stratified the
tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1
in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We
assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group
with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33)
expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with
less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used
the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) ^31^ for count normalization and
differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change
\>=2, FDR \< 1\^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or

age (RRID:SCR_015687) ^31^ for count normalization and
differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change
\>=2, FDR \< 1\^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or
sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or
sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal PantherDB
v19.0 ^32^, we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the
Reactome database (RRID:SCR_003485).

For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=202), we
first stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups according to
the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression
analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed
groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the
difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest
and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold
change \>=1 or Log2-fold change \<=1 and FDR\<1\^-3. Overexpressed genes
are reported in **S. Table 10**.

### **GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis** 

We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors
obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024 ^16^, and is
publicly available at
[[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672).
The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the
tumor--stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and
stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were
initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and
IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the
TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data
had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed
normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) ^31^,
followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were
corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206).

Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic
deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene
expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
([[https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism]{.underline}](https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism)),

ar composition of each AOI, transcriptomic
deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene
expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
([[https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism]{.underline}](https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism)),
a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial
transcriptomics datasets
[^78^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?pjFFQe). This method not only
estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles
for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw
scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577,
along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we
downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final
dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in
resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were
grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T
cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B
cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90
endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late
erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient.
Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with
low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both
'cell types' and 'cell states'.

We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA)
scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific
deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess

(HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific
deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess
the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we
correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from
BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y \~
exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating 'Patient' as a covariate (y
\~ exp(x) \| Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the
same tumor.

### ***Ligand-receptor analysis***

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing
them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq
analysis). MultiNicheNetR
[^79^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?MdBFlQ)
([[https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git]{.underline}](https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high
and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the
analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of
five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were
required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion
of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates
were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log

olds, with a minimum sample proportion
of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates
were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log
fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized
ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions
enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These
candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics
dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We
correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor
deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma
segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

### ***Immunopeptidomics***

We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC
samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to
the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the
interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more
information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was
performed as previously described
[^80^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YdoerY) with minor
modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed
on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by
mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected
to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer
composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors

owed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected
to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer
composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors
(Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich,
I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at
500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at
25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was
applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher),
pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of
anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.
The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the
following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM
NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were
dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges
(Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in
0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were
eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1%
TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The
eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using
the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
into a single sample.

in 0.1%
TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The
eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using
the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
into a single sample.

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto
an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1
min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),
with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs
cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired
with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the
minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at \< 700 m/z
or 3 Th \>800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000,
considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was
lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59
eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard
polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to
exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using
a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard
(ThermoFischer Scientific).

Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational
suite (RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.
MSFragger [^81^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?06hrYE) was
employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a
few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and
'null', allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained
to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da,
and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da)
was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation
of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher
[^82^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?B9ESPF), applying a false
discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match
(PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out
using IonQuant [^83^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?bU4Soz),
enabling between-run normalization while disabling the
match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434)
[^84^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?BLFQMa) with default
settings.

match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434)
[^84^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?BLFQMa) with default
settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while
the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides
confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis.
Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting
from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286,
[[https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant]{.underline}](https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant))
[^85^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?w0V9Ed) with default settings
and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis,
peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor
antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets
(UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides
in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion
based on matching t-CyCIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low
(patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes
unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped
genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between
the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the
high group was performed using Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575)
[^86^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?wyaEfK) (GO Biological
Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms
were recorded.

### ***TCR repertoire analysis***

We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC
samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples
(total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study. Tumor samples underwent
RNA sequencing with Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using the Illumina Stranded
Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero plus), aiming at \>100 million read pairs
at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data has been processed with
Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes
2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were
extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725)
[^87^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9WV0sl), following the
RNA-seq workflow
([[https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/]{.underline}](https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/))
with default settings. We used 'mixcr analyze rnaseq', followed by
'mixcr exportQc align' and 'mixcr exportQc chainUsage' for quality
control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq
(non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully
aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones,
with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in
7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group).
Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly
represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal
diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R
package (RRID:SCR_023089)
[^88^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?A4XHGI). We employed the

re also highly
represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal
diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R
package (RRID:SCR_023089)
[^88^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?A4XHGI). We employed the
'repExplore' function with the \"volume\" method to compare TCR clone
counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to
the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): (1) High MHCII group: 6
samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6
samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (3) Medium (the rest).
Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were
evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for
multiple comparisons using the Holm method.

### ***iPDCs flow cytometry analysis***

The dataset used for this analysis was generated in Launonen et al.,
2024 [^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8SpPPv). Briefly, tumor
tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was
dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an
omentum-derived matrix
[^89^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v8otat). Cultures were
treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was
performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting
dataset was made publicly available at
[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}.

In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells
(single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low
groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all
immune (CD45⁺) and myeloid cells (CD11c⁺ and CD14⁺) (**S. Fig. 6**).
MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the

y 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low
groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all
immune (CD45⁺) and myeloid cells (CD11c⁺ and CD14⁺) (**S. Fig. 6**).
MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the
bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune
and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was
quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single
cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between
MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was
assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed
using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and
custom R scripts.

### ***Survival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival
(RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by
calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total
cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more
than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, **S. Fig. 1b**) were selected
for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell
phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune
populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core
post-preprocessing (n=244, **S. Fig. 1b**). Patient stratification based
on cell population used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above
median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split
into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66,

. 1b**). Patient stratification based
on cell population used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above
median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split
into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66,
and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same
approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular
profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only
high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.

### ***Statistical analyses***

We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The difference
in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided
Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The
difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a
two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were
assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted
P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted
P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

### **Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.**

**a** Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics
tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC
profiles: *BRCA*loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-*BRCA*wt),
homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification
(*CCNE1amp*), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).
High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract

ion assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).
High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract
single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline
(CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical
covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and
molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical
methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation,
both incorporating external cohorts. **b** Antibody panel employed for
t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. **c** A bar plot displaying the
composition of cancer cells, stromal cells, myeloid cells, lymphocytes,
and unassigned cells per tumor. The bar plot is sorted by cancer cell
abundance. **d** UMAP projection of the analyzed cells, the colors
correspond to their main cell type group assignment. **e** Example of
t-CycIF image. **f** Cell type annotation of the image in panel d**.**
**g** Bar plot illustrating the composition of immune cells per tumor.
The bar plot is sorted according to the CD11c+ myeloid cell proportion.
**h** UMAP projection of immune cells coloured by their annotation as in
panel f. **i** t-CycIF image displaying the functional marker Ki67; this
image corresponds to the one in panel e. **j** Cells labeled according
to their proportion of CD8.T cells neighbors, areas with higher CD8.T
cell neighbors are shown in red, this image corresponds to the one in
panel e. **k** UMAP projection of cancer cells, coloured by their
cluster assignment (at the left) and by the expression of relevant
proteins (smaller panels on the right). **l** Heatmap displaying the

wn in red, this image corresponds to the one in
panel e. **k** UMAP projection of cancer cells, coloured by their
cluster assignment (at the left) and by the expression of relevant
proteins (smaller panels on the right). **l** Heatmap displaying the
median expression of markers per cancer cell cluster. All cancer cells
detected in the study were included in the heatmap (number of tumors =
253). The listed markers were used for cancer cell clustering.
Hierarchical clustering of the markers, based on their median expression
per cluster, is shown in the accompanying dendrograms. **m** t-CycIF
image of the indicated markers, the right panel displays a magnification

### **Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC** 

**a** A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline 'CEFIIRA'.
Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example,
predicting if cancer cells belong to either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors.
Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target
(for example, *BRCA*loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation
iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their
corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the
remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to

ocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to
predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to
either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are
calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested
cross-validations, the model's median accuracy and consensus critical
features are identified. **b-c** Heatmap displaying the median accuracy
(F1 score) of CEFIIRA models in predicting whether cells belong to
tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated
molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations (columns). The 95%

results corresponding to PDS samples are in panel **b,** and in panel

prediction (F1 score) of the indicated targets (rows) when using
different cellular populations (columns). The corresponding models were

ages of independent samples, displaying the

nd, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, 38 Finland. 39 21 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology Clinical Trials Unit, Helsinki University 40 Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 41 22 Lead contact. 42 Abstract 43 The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, 44 yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly 45 understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC 46 from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. 47 Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically 48 heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the 49 tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we 50 identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. 51 Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic 52 datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune 53 activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived 54 immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with 55 increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers 56 novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification 57 in HGSC. 58 INTRODUCTION 59 The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, 60 immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 1.

, paving the way for improved patient stratification 57 in HGSC. 58 INTRODUCTION 59 The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, 60 immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 1. The 61 continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is 62 fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and 63 therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most 64 prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 2,3, characterized by high genomic 65 instability 4,5 and significant tumor heterogeneity 6. This heterogeneity is evident in 66 various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME 67 cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 7. The genetic 68 diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with 69 a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 70 BRCA1/2 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher 71 

## Page 3
 
3 lymphocyte infiltration 8,9. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) 72 tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 10 and unique responses to 73 combinations with immunotherapies 11,12. Moreover, we and others have shown that 74 exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by 75 enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 13–15, resulting in 76 spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 16.

r, we and others have shown that 74 exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by 75 enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 13–15, resulting in 76 spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 16. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex 77 relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor 78 ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment 79 strategies for patients with HGSC. 80 We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single 81 cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and 82 multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and 83 mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial 84 TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that 85 associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, 86 CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate 87 single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. 88 We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression 89 specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of 90 immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune 91 hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 92 clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune 93 cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for 94 targeted interventions in HGSC.

This emphasizes the necessity of 281 integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed 282 spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME. 283 CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor 284 ecosystem features 285 To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and 286 prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom 287 forest), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (Fig. 3a).

reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and 286 prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom 287 forest), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (Fig. 3a). The 288 primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-289 dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA 290 robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical 291 features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation 292 

## Page 10
 
10 in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent 293 overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and 294 balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a 295 prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a 296 sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical 297 features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the 298 previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus 299 estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate 300 multidimensional data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient 301 and molecular profiles (Fig. 3a). 302 We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the 4,644,485 single 303 cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (Fig. 1e, i; S. Fig. 304 1g), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (Fig. 1j), resulting in 43 feature 305 representations per cell (Materials and Methods).

To further corroborate the 361 prognostic role of MHCII expression by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended 362 our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)5, by inferring the cancer-cell-363 specific MHCII expression from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM 26. Fig. 3l). 366 However, its expression was higher in BRCAloss tumors compared to HRP and 367 CCNE1amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results (S. Fig. 3m). associates with immune infiltration 374 To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on overall immune 375 infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune populations according to the 376 fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor. This analysis revealed a higher fraction of 377 MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, 378 and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (Fig. 4a). To corroborate these findings, we 379 tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated 380 proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see 381 Methods). In contrast, 383 tumors with high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-384 like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-385 tumor responses (S. Tumor-intrinsic 559 MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the 560 context of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade. 561 DISCUSSION 562 We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026 563 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical 564 samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying 565 phenotypic compositions. These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor 566 cell phenotypic profiles and clinicomolecular groups, underscoring the role of 567 molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the 568 two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer 569 cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI 570 displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 571 Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional 572 single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC.

le the immune-cold TSI 570 displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 571 Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional 572 single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through 573 spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that 574 MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate 575 immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with 576 enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and 577 antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize 578 MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune 579 surveillance 48,49, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T 580 

## Page 19
 
19 cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 50. While IFN expression in 581 tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests 582 it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 51. Through a robust spatial 583 TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within 584 immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. 585 Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced 586 CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively 587 present antigens and activate the local TME.

I with augmented IFN activity. 585 Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced 586 CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively 587 present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII 588 and IFN-γ expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic 589 MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and 590 antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in Fig. 7, 591 support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ 592 T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated 593 with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 594 MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary 595 pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 52. In 596 HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs undergoing 597 whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis 53. 598 Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved 599 response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-600 negative breast cancer 41,50,54,55. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-601 L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated 602 only modest benefits 56. In contrast, our in vitro analysis of immunotherapy responses 603 in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might 604 enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of 605 CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples.

In addition, our study revealed that other 606 immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in 607 tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical 608 exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients 609 with HGSC 16. Moreover, we showed that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in 610 MHCII-expressing tumors, and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR 611 clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While tumor-612 intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity cycle via active 613

20 antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches should be developed for patients 614 with tumors lacking MHCII expression. In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas 615 uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the 616 clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic 617 approaches and patient stratification. 618 Study limitations 619 We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for 620 future refinement and exploration. The retrospective design and reliance on archival 621 tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity, 622 limiting some of the statistical comparisons between patients. Our protein marker 623 panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell 624 types.

The samples were obtained from patients during interval 736 debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection criteria for these samples included HR-737 proficient status, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-738 free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease 739 (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent 740 IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based 741 on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-742 PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis. 743 Molecular profile assignment 744 For the TMA cohort, we first identified BRCA1/2 mutation and BRCA1 promoter 745 methylation status of each sample as previously described 57. For patients without a BRCA1/2 germline mutation, both 748 BRCA1/2 somatic mutations and BRCA1 promoter hypermethylation were assessed 749 using DNA extracted from FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was 750 performed using BRCA MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, 751 USA) and Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent probe 752 amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. The models' training process employs 929 nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training 930 and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 931 30 data partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model 932 robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results 933 when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive 934 (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (S. Fig. 3n). For each nested cross-validation, using the 935 training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the 936 model (min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, n_estimators). The Bayesian 937 optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ 938 values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine 939 cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-940 folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-941 validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This 942 step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training 943 set.

dent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-941 validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was 1090 passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially 1091 washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 1092 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-1093 HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the 1094 resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, 1095 along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, 1096 WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic 1097 acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL 1098 of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials 1099 (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) 1100 at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled 1101 into a single sample. 1102 Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical 1103 column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18; 1104 IonOpticks).

Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow 1105 rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent 1106 B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 1107 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient 1108 was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 1109 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS 1110 acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1111 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF 1112 mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 1113 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th 1114 at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, 1115 considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered 1116 linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 1117 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested 1118 proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. 36 Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational suite 1122 (RRID:SCR_022864, https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe). Searches were 1123 conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive 1124 of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences 1125 for validation purposes. MSFragger 81 was employed for MHC peptide identification 1126 under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-1127 specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to 1128 lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge 1129 states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a 1130 variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM 1131 filtering was performed with Philosopher 82, applying a false discovery rate (FDR) 1132 cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-1133 free quantification was carried out using IonQuant 83, enabling between-run 1134 normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option. 1135 Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the 1136 online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) 84 with default settings. The 1137 threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder 1138 (% Rank) was set to 5.
