vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
hival samples
and anonymization of the material.

Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking
surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by
three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were
extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE
blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with
one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from
the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
previously described [^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gaaOUU)
and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration
detected with t-CycIF.

### ***Whole slide cohort description***

We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the
ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from
patients during interval debulking surgeries (IDS) conducted at Helsinki
University Hospital, Finland in accordance with the ethical standards
from the 1975 Declaration of Helsinki. Each patient gave their written
informed consent, and the use of samples and clinical data was approved
by the local Ethics Committee (HUS334/2021). The selection criteria for
these samples included HR-proficient status, a tumor purity greater than
10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than
6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R \> 0cm) after
surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS
therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical
trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0040
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
macroscopic residual disease (R \> 0cm) after
surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS
therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical
trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define
long-PFS (\>=12 months) and short-PFS (\<12 months) in the survival
analysis.

### ***Molecular profile assignment***

For the TMA cohort, we first identified *BRCA1/2* mutation and *BRCA1*
promoter methylation status of each sample as previously described
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SdxgQX). In brief, *BRCA1/2*
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the
Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a
*BRCA1/2* germline mutation, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and
Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent
probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation.
Details of the number of tumors with *BRCA1* or *BRCA2* mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter methylation are reported in **S. Table 1**.

Next, tumors without a *BRCA1/2* mutation or *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using
Illumina HiSeq2500 to determine a *BRCA*-like CNV profile
[^59^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qGUBcI) and
*CCNE1*-amplification status as described previously
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y06swk). In addition, using
the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software
shallowHRD [^60^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6XQwP9). Finally,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0041
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
plification status as described previously
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y06swk). In addition, using
the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software
shallowHRD [^60^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6XQwP9). Finally,
we categorized the tumors as *BRCAloss*, HRD, HRP, and *CCNE1*amp using
a hierarchical approach described in **S. Fig. 1a**. Samples that failed
QC for *BRCA1/2* targeted DNA-sequencing, *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV analysis, were labeled as "Unassigned"
(or Other). In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between
shallowHRD and the *BRCA-like* CNV
profile[^59^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y35pzZ) were labeled
as \'Unassigned.

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
[^61^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?njGwIx). For all analyses,
only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological grades G3 and G4, and
those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were included. For the
whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically validated HRD
classification performed at the Helsinki University Hospital, Finland,
which uses ovaHRDscar
[^61^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jEEHLd), and the detection of
somatic and germline mutations and gene deletions in *BRCA1/2* genes.

### ***Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing***

To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor
tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the
whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and
the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots.
The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (**S. Table
2**) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0042
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
pled two cores from the tumor center and
the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots.
The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (**S. Table
2**) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the
t-CycIF protocol [^62^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?FYaR9w). The
panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main
cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images' illumination was
corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371)
[^63^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?wLnfGG). The per-image tiles
were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266,
[[https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar]{.underline}](https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar)).
Individual TMA's cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox
on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts,
United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
(RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2
[^64^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fuk3P2) and Cellprofiler
v3.1.8 [^65^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zuypdM) using 2-pixel
nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and
morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter,
and Roundness were quantified using MATLAB. Quality filtering was
applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity across cycles
([[https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite]{.underline}](https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite)),
image regions with artifacts, and TMA's cores (n=64) with less than 1000
high-quality cells.

For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections
were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex
validated antibody panel (**S.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0043
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
h artifacts, and TMA's cores (n=64) with less than 1000
high-quality cells.

For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections
were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex
validated antibody panel (**S. Table 12**) and scanned with a RareCyte
CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol. The image tiles were
stitched with ASHLAR. For WSI, we developed a more scalable analysis
pipeline
([[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)).
Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei
segmentation in StarDist
[^66^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gOhXek), implementing the
pre-trained model '*2D_versatile_flu*o\'\'. Then, the mean intensity of
each channel was quantified by our in-house script
(https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main) based on
skimage package [^67^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RBeQSO).
Image quality control was performed by CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157)
[^68^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6NA1Xm).

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were
clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers
(Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the
R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and
cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0044
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
r were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the
0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel\'s signal
was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by
marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized
map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM
node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S. Fig. 1c**) by
using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using
unsupervised clustering with the software Tribus
[^69^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dApoW5). Outliers were
removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity,
normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5
[^70^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Td9ajN), and scaled by
Z-score. Tribus was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0045
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
ribus was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S. Table 13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are
images S100_iOme and S110_iOme, which were manually gated with scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) due to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells.
We corroborated our annotations by comparing them to cancer cell
proportions determined by expert pathologist annotations using
hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, showing agreement across samples
(**S. Fig. 4l**). The MHCII+ cancer cells were identified using manual
thresholds and comparing the annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### **Cancer cell phenotype clustering**

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed
outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method
section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based
on the expression levels of the markers: \"Ki67\", \"Vimentin\",
\"pSTAT1\", \"MHC-I\", \"ECadherin\", \"yH2AX\", \"pTBK1\", \"cPARP1\",
\"CK7\", \"PDL1_488\", and morphological features: \"Area\",
\"Eccentricity\", and \"Roundness\" into a 2500-node self-organized map
(SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were
clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954)
[^71^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?TTvrXT). For this, the number
of clusters (*k*) was assessed from 2 to 12. For each value of *k*
assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of the 80% of the

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0046
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
age ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954)
[^71^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?TTvrXT). For this, the number
of clusters (*k*) was assessed from 2 to 12. For each value of *k*
assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of the 80% of the
SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and morphological features)
were randomly selected. The algorithm "partitioning around medoids"
(pam) was used to cluster the selected dataset per iteration. After the
iterations, we determined the best number of cancer meta-clusters, *k*,
by assessing the resultant consensus-clustering matrix for each value of
*k* and its cumulative density distribution (**Ext. material - Fig.
3**). The cancer meta-clusters were named according to the expression of
relevant markers in each one (**Ext. material - Fig. 2**).

### ***Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis***

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality
control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius
of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package Scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) [^72^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5Mos0I). We
clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate
recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k**=**40
employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package Scimap. Every
cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value of k, we
used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick
(**Ext. material - Fig. 3**). We used a radius of 30µm to identify
recurrent neighborhoods of cells that are in physical contact. The
replication of our analysis using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to
similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach (**Ext.
material - Fig. 3**).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0047
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
adius of 30µm to identify
recurrent neighborhoods of cells that are in physical contact. The
replication of our analysis using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to
similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach (**Ext.
material - Fig. 3**). The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was
calculated as the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total
number of cells in the tumor.

### ***Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis***

To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity
of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial
transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different
responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data,
we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package
[^73^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JOgS7e). For the QC, we
excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected,
more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin
genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat\'s
SCTransform function.

Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an
expert pathologist as 'Cancer', 'TSI' (tumor-stroma interface with
partial tumor and stromal morphology), 'Immune-Stromal' and 'other'.
Spots annotated as 'other' have been excluded from the analysis. Within
'Immune-Stromal' spots, we have distinguished an additional type -
'TSI2', defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to
either 'Cancer' or 'TSI' spots. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109)

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0048
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
efined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to
either 'Cancer' or 'TSI' spots. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109)
[^[44]{.underline}^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GURNkO). The
MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB,
HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1,
HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the
computed MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and
by the median expression across spots in the samples regarding the
response to chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial
or poor response was adopted from Böhm et al, 2015
[^74^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JpjFmE). An IFN-γ signature,
obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene
set collection (RRID:SCR_016863)
[^75^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v1TFTd), was also quantified
per spot. Statistical significance has been computed using a two-sided
Wilcoxon Rank Sum test.

### ***Machine learning supervised classification***

The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had
the following pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein
marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score);
(2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness),
scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the proportion of
neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type;
additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like
short- or long-OS the model included patients' clinical information --
FIGO stage and age at diagnosis.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0049
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
portion of
neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type;
additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like
short- or long-OS the model included patients' clinical information --
FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker
and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range
were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than
100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent
machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling
was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20%
into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.

To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm
for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the
PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695,
[[https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret]{.underline}](https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret)).
We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of:
BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected
Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance
(weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (**S. Table
14**). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to determine the
number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S. Table 15**).

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python
scikit-learn library [^76^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XlLOJH)

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0050
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S. Table 15**).

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python
scikit-learn library [^76^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XlLOJH)
for binary classification of cells into clinicomolecular groups
(https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA). The models\' training process
employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively
partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using the
stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 30 data partitions
based on a balance between computational efficiency and model
robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve
similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based
on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (**S. Fig.
3n**). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a
Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model
(*min_samples_split*, *min_samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of
hyperparameters' values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean
weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each
iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three
independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a
cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on
classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting
the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target
variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we
evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set

vector_chunks.text:
ve features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target
variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we
evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set
partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell
level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard
deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported.
Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells,
Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified
the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of
overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below
the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

### ***Deconvoluted RNA-expression and CIBERT sort profile***

For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and
MHCI), we used publicly available RNA-expression data from The Cancer
Genome Atlas (TCGA) (RRID:SCR_003193)
[^[5]{.underline}^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WXlp1J). The
deconvoluted cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with PRISM and
adopted from the previous work
[^[26]{.underline}^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?TjHdBf). The
inferred cell proportions by CIBERSORT were obtained from the
GDC-PanCanAtlas repository
[^77^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?C7Mdte).

### ***RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment***

For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read
counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463)
[^78^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RgUjj2), from the Genomics
Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also

vector_chunks.text:
s of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read
counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463)
[^78^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RgUjj2), from the Genomics
Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also
obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository
[^77^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?I1JWZK). We stratified the
tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1
in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We
assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group
with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33)
expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with
less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used
the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687)
[^[31]{.underline}^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9AxknJ) for
count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were
considered with differential expression according to the next criteria:
Log2-fold change \>=2, FDR \< 1\^-3. To identify over-represented
pathways or sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of
over-expressed or sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web
portal PantherDB v19.0
[^[32]{.underline}^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?S91n8S), we
performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the Reactome database
(RRID:SCR_003485).

For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=202). We
first stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups according to
the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)

vector_chunks.text:
nto three equal-sized groups according to
the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression
analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed
groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the
difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest
and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold
change \>=1 or Log2-fold change \<=1 and FDR\<1\^-3. Overexpressed genes
are reported in **S. Table 10**.

### ***scRNA-seq analysis***

We retrieved scRNA-seq gene count matrices from 51 HGSC samples,
pre-processed and annotated
[^23^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6j34Lb), from the Gene
Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples
containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis,
resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from
27 patients. We assessed the activity of MHCII signature across cell
types by computing the normalized Ucell score using the R UCell package
(v1.3.1) (RRID:SCR_027109)
[^44^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?HPzMBY). The MHCII signature
comprised of the following genes: *HLA-DMA*, *HLA-DMB*, *HLA-DOA*,
*HLA-DOB*, *HLA-DPA1*, *HLA-DPB1*, *HLA-DQA1*, *HLA-DQA2*, *HLA-DQB1*,
*HLA-DRA*, *HLA-DRB1*, *HLA-DRB5*, *CD74.* Based on the MHCII Ucell
scores, the cells were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no
expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). For each sample

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0054
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
PB1*, *HLA-DQA1*, *HLA-DQA2*, *HLA-DQB1*,
*HLA-DRA*, *HLA-DRB1*, *HLA-DRB5*, *CD74.* Based on the MHCII Ucell
scores, the cells were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no
expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). For each sample
and cell type, pseudo-bulk gene expression profiles were generated using
the function '*AggregateExpression'* from the Seurat package
(v4.3.0)[^79^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?AWcfq3). According to
the pseudo-bulk MHCII Ucell signature in cancer cells, the samples were
stratified into three equal-sized groups: high, medium, and low,
according to tertile distribution (**S. Fig. 4c**). To assess the
correlation between cancer-specific MHCII expression and IFN-γ in immune
populations, pseudo-bulk expression of the *IFNG* gene was computed for
each cell type per sample. Only immune cells were included in the
correlation analysis, as they exhibited higher *IFNG* expression
compared to fibroblasts or epithelial/cancer cells (**S. Fig. 5l**).
Pearson's correlation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially
expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in
cancer cells were identified using pseudobulk sample expression and
analyzed with the limma package. Patient information was added to the
design matrix and regressed out from the DEG analysis.

### **GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis** 

We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors
obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024
[^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4Av0Uh), and is publicly
available at
[[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0055
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024
[^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4Av0Uh), and is publicly
available at
[[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672).
The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the
tumor--stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and
stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were
initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and
IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the
TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data
had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed
normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by
variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using
the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206).

Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic
deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene
expression profiles. We applied *BayesPrism*, a deconvolution tool
developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics
datasets. This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also
infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for
the deconvolution, we used raw scRNA-seq counts from the annotated
ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw GeoMx counts. To
prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we downsampled the dataset in a
patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and
33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune
populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0056
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
erence, we downsampled the dataset in a
patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and
33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune
populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories.
The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364
macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403
dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other
cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were
annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard
*BayesPrism* workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and
annotations provided for both 'cell types' and 'cell states'.

We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA)
scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific
deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess
the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we
correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from
BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y \~
exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating 'Patient' as a covariate (y
\~ exp(x) \| Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the
same tumor.

### ***Ligand-receptor analysis***

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing
them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq
analysis). MultiNicheNetR
[^80^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?MdBFlQ)

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0057
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
ons, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing
them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq
analysis). MultiNicheNetR
[^80^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?MdBFlQ)
([[https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git]{.underline}](https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high
and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the
analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of
five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were
required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion
of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates
were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log
fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized
ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions
enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These
candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics
dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We
correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor
deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma
segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

### ***Immunopeptidomics***

We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC

segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

### ***Immunopeptidomics***

We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC
samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to
the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the
interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more
information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was
performed as previously described
[^81^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YdoerY) with minor
modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed
on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by
mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected
to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer
composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors
(Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich,
I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at
500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at
25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was
applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher),
pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of
anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.
The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0059
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
VA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.
The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the
following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM
NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were
dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges
(Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in
0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were
eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1%
TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The
eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using
the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
into a single sample.

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto
an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1
min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0060
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),
with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs
cm^−2^ to 0.64 Vs cm^−2^ in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were
acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above
the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at \< 700
m/z or 3 Th \>800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of
20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy
(CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting
from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm^−2^ to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm^−2^.
Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was
used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was
evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest
standard (ThermoFischer Scientific).

Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational
suite (RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.
MSFragger [^82^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?06hrYE) was
employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a
few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and
'null', allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained
to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da,

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
fication under default settings, with a
few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and
'null', allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained
to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da,
and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da)
was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation
of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher
[^83^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?B9ESPF), applying a false
discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match
(PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out
using IonQuant [^84^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?bU4Soz),
enabling between-run normalization while disabling the
match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434)
[^85^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?BLFQMa) with default
settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while
the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides
confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis.
Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting
from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286,
[[https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant]{.underline}](https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant))
[^86^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?w0V9Ed) with default settings
and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis,
peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor
antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
oogle-docs/?w0V9Ed) with default settings
and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis,
peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor
antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets
(UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides
in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion
based on matching t-CyCIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12) and
low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) groups. Genes unique to the high group were
identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups
and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment
analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using
Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575)
[^87^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?wyaEfK) (GO Biological
Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms
were recorded.

### ***TCR repertoire analysis***

We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC
samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples
(total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study. Tumor samples underwent
RNA sequencing with Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using the Illumina Stranded
Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero plus), aiming at \>100 million read pairs
at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data has been processed with
Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes
2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were
extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725)
[^88^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9WV0sl), following the
RNA-seq workflow
([[https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/]{.underline}](https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/))
with default settings.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0063
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
es were
extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725)
[^88^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9WV0sl), following the
RNA-seq workflow
([[https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/]{.underline}](https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/))
with default settings. We used 'mixcr analyze rnaseq', followed by
'mixcr exportQc align' and 'mixcr exportQc chainUsage' for quality
control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq
(non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully
aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones,
with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in
7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group).
Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly
represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal
diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R
package (RRID:SCR_023089)
[^89^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?A4XHGI). We employed the
'repExplore' function with the \"volume\" method to compare TCR clone
counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to
the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): (1) High MHCII group: 6
samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6
samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB). Differences in clone numbers
between groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with
p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.

### ***iPDCs flow cytometry analysis***

The dataset used for this analysis was generated in Launonen et al.,
2024 [^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8SpPPv). Briefly, tumor
tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was

vector_chunks.text:
### ***iPDCs flow cytometry analysis***

The dataset used for this analysis was generated in Launonen et al.,
2024 [^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8SpPPv). Briefly, tumor
tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was
dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an
omentum-derived matrix
[^90^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v8otat). Cultures were
treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was
performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting
dataset was made publicly available at
[[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672).

In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells
(single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low
groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all
immune (CD45⁺) and myeloid cells (CD11c⁺ and CD14⁺) (**S. Fig. 6**).
MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the
bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune
and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was
quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single
cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between
MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was
assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed
using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and
custom R scripts.

### ***Survival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer and survival. For TMAs, cell
population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0065
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
cripts.

### ***Survival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer and survival. For TMAs, cell
population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance
per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor.
Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core
post-preprocessing (n=233, **S. Fig. 1b**) were selected for survival
analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and
clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or
RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing
(n=244, **S. Fig. 1b**). Patient stratification based on cell population
used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above median) and low (below
median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by
abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66).
Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models
were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when
applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage
HGSCs (III and IV) were included.

### ***Statistical analyses***

We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The difference
in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided
Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The
difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a
two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were
assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted
P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md
vector_chunks.text:
erent groups was calculated using a
two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were
assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted
P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted
P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

## **FIGURE LEGENDS**

### **Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.**

**a** Study schematics. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics
tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC
profiles: *BRCA*loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-*BRCA*wt),
homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification
(*CCNE1amp*), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).
High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract
single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline
(CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical
covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and
molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical
methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation,
both incorporating external cohorts. **b** Antibody panel employed for
t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. **c** A bar plot displaying the

regressions were adjusted by patient age, FIGO stage and molecular
profile and were performed separately for PDS and IDS samples. Analysis

### **Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC** 

**a** A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline 'CEFIIRA'.
Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example,
predicting if cancer cells belong to either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors.
Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target
(for example, *BRCA*loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation
iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their
corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the
remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to
predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to
either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are
calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested
cross-validations, the model's median accuracy and consensus critical
features are identified. **b-c** Heatmap displaying the median accuracy
(F1 score) of CEFIIRA models in predicting whether cells belong to
tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated

results corresponding to PDS samples are in panel **b,** and in panel

markers. The immune 'hot' and immune 'cold' areas were manually
annotated according to the abundance of immune cells observed in them.
**f** Bar plots displaying the Pearson's correlation of *IFNG* gene

expression in epithelial/cancer cells, inter samples (n=39) using

(p\<0.05).

in the GeoMx dataset (N=108 ROIs). The size of the dots reflects the

across 12 samples that passed quality control. **g and h** Top 10
overrepresented pathways based on the uniquely mapped genes to MHCII
peptidome on four MHCII tumor high samples. The pathways are categorised
by GO cellular components (**g**) and Biological Processes (**h**), with
reflected gene number overlap and adjusted by the Benjamini and Hochberg
method p-value of enrichment. **i** Bar plot of TCR clone counts (TRAD
and TRB) derived from bulk RNAseq data for tumor samples classified as
MHCII-high and MHCII-low. Each point represents an individual clone
count, with bars indicating the mean count for each group. The error
bars represent the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles. Statistical difference
between groups is evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with
p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method. **j**
Infographic illustrating the analysis of multicolor flow cytometry data
from iPDCs collected by Launonen et al., 2024, used to compare CD8+ T
cell responses in MHCII-high and MHCII-low cancer samples following drug

mean fluorescence intensity of CD8+ T cells between control and treated
samples for MHCII-high and MHCII-low groups. Treatments include
pembrolizumab (P10, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (T10, 10 µg/mL), or their
combination (P10T10). Statistical significance is assessed using the
Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with significant differences (p \< 0.05) marked
by an asterisk (\*). **l** Per-sample log2FC across mean fluorescence
intensity of GrzB response in CD8+ T cells between control and treated
conditions. Samples are grouped based on MHCII tumor status. Significant
changes (p-value \< 0.05, Wilcoxon rank-sum test) are highlighted with
circles.

**Figure 7. Schematics of the main findings.**

Illustration highlighting the main findings in our study.

### **Supplementary Figure 1**

**a** Assignment of the tumors into the four different molecular
profiles. **b** Flowchart of various quality control criteria used for
tumors in the multiomics cohort. Different quality control criteria were
applied for different analyses. For example, in the survival analysis of
immune cell infiltration, only tumors with matched clinical data and
more than one core per tumor (N=244) were included. Additionally, for
the survival analysis of cancer cell proportions, only tumors with more
than 5% cancer cells (N=233) were analyzed. **c** Flowchart representing
the strategy used to assign cells from t-CycIF images into different
populations according to the expression of indicated markers.

cancer cell proportions, only tumors with more
than 5% cancer cells (N=233) were analyzed. **c** Flowchart representing
the strategy used to assign cells from t-CycIF images into different
populations according to the expression of indicated markers. **d**
Heatmap of the median expression of cell lineage markers per labeled
cell phenotypes. Median values per cell phenotype from all tumors after
the first QC (N=258) are shown in the heatmap. **e,f** Box plots of the
proportion of immune cells in tumors detected with t-CycIF, grouped by
annotations made by an expert pathologist using immunohistochemistry
(IHC) on matching tumor sections. For each tumor, the pathologist\'s
annotation reflects the number of immune cells in the core with the
highest number of IHC-positive cells (out of four cores per tumor), for
example: \<20 indicates fewer than 20 cells in the core with the most
IHC-positive cells. Similarly, for t-CycIF, the proportion of immune
cells in the core with the highest abundance of the indicated immune
population is reflected. **e** The panel displays the proportions of
CD8+ T cells and panel **f** the proportion of CD20+ lymphocytes. The
differences between groups were calculated using a two-tailed Wilcoxon
rank-sum test. The black horizontal lines represent the median, the
boxes extend from the first to the third quartile, and the whiskers
indicate the values at 1.5 times the interquartile range. Individual
dots represent the values per tumor. **g** Heatmap of the median
expression of functional markers per labeled cell phenotypes. Median
values per cell phenotype from all tumors after the first QC (N=258) are
shown in the heatmap.

### **Supplementary Figure 2**

**a** Heatmaps displaying the median of the median proportions of each

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.md::chunk_0095

vector_chunks.text:
tional markers per labeled cell phenotypes. Median
values per cell phenotype from all tumors after the first QC (N=258) are

### **Supplementary Figure 2**

**a** Heatmaps displaying the median of the median proportions of each
immune population per tumor across molecular profiles. First, the median
proportion of each immune population was calculated per tumor (across
cores); then, for each molecular profile, the median of these
tumor-level medians was computed. Separate heatmaps are shown for PDS
and IDS samples. Tumors sampled with more than one core were included in
the analysis. Differences in median proportions across the four
molecular profiles were assessed using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test.
Median values per molecular profile and FDR of the test are reported in
S. Table 3. **b** Bar plots of the enrichment (log2 fold change) of RCNs
in IDS samples compared to PDS. Enrichment was calculated using a
two-tailed Wilcoxon rank-sum test. **c** Forest plot of the Cox
regression of progression-free survival (PFS) in relation to the median
abundance of the indicated immune cells per tumor. The regressions were
adjusted by patient age, FIGO stage and molecular profile and were
performed separately for PDS and IDS samples. For the analysis, we
included only tumors sampled with more than one core and with associated
clinical information (N=244). The small squares represent the mean
hazard ratio (HR), and the whiskers show the 95% confidence intervals.
**d** Forest plot of the Cox regression of overall survival (OS) in
relation to the median abundance of the indicated immune cells per
tumor. The regressions were adjusted by patient age, FIGO stage and
molecular profile and were performed separately for PDS and IDS samples.

ox regression of overall survival (OS) in

according to the number of cancer cells per core. Only tumors sampled
with more than 5% cancer cells and with at least two cores after QC were
included. In the box plots, black horizontal lines represent the median,
the boxes extend from the first to the third quartile, and the whiskers
indicate the values at 1.5 times the interquartile range. Outliers are
shown as dots. The differences in proportion were calculated using a
two-tailed Wilcoxon rank-sum test. The notation for unadjusted P-values

adjusted by patient age, FIGO stage and molecular profile and were
performed separately for PDS and IDS samples. Forest plot notations are
as in Figure 2c. **g** Forest plot of the multivariable Cox regression
of OS in relation to the median relative abundance of the indicated
cancer cell cluster per tumor and adjusted by the displayed variables.

Anonymized patient IDs correspond to the first part of the sample name
(EOCid) then, a unique sample ID is given subsequently after the
underscore symbol "\_", PDS samples are denoted with (p), while IDS
samples with (i). The anatomical origins of samples correspond to
Omentum (Ome), Peritoneum (Per), Primary tumor (Tum), Ovary (Ova) or
Mesothelium (Mes). **d** Bar plot of the abundance of the MHCII
signature per tumor in the scRNA-seq dataset, with tumor samples stacked
by tumor. **e** Box plots of the estimated proportions of immune

neum (Per), Primary tumor (Tum), Ovary (Ova) or

## **Abstract**

## **Abstract**

## The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant--host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.

## **INTRODUCTION**

The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions
[^1^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GKAflH). The continuous
crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
cosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions
[^1^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GKAflH). The continuous
crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is
fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression,
and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC)
is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer
[^2,3^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?J8T2j5), characterized by
high genomic instability
[^4,5^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UZHLvb) and significant
tumor heterogeneity [^6^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?TW0MVf).
This heterogeneity is evident in various aspects, such as
histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME cellular
composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy
[^7^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?cy7HVb). The genetic diversity
of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors
with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as
those with *BRCA1/2* mutations, have been suggested to be more
immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration
[^8,9^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9ZXefK). Emerging evidence
also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) tumors possess distinct immune
escape mechanisms [^10^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ZOsCFd) and
unique responses to combinations with immunotherapies
[^11,12^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Kkdr2C). Moreover, we and
others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate
interactions within the immune compartment
[^13--15^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?E8yfUf), resulting in

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
r, we and
others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate
interactions within the immune compartment
[^13--15^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?E8yfUf), resulting in
spatially confined T-cell exhaustion
[^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?PeYuGV). Therefore, it is
crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and
molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve
patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for
patients with HGSC.

We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1
million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with
comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging
DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a
tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the
single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that
associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning
tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to
uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of
cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at
the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of
immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate
immune hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis
independently of clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the
interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms
linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in
HGSC.

## **RESULTS**
