pacting prognosis
independently of clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the
interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms
linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in
HGSC.

## **RESULTS**

### **Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors**

To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography,
clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic
profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing,
gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex
imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (**Fig. 1a**).
The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from
treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after
platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS
n=158). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven patients.
Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were
collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months)
(**S. Table 1**). Most patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and
paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free
survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months).

We conducted a hierarchical multidimensional analysis to assign the
tumors' molecular profiles. Firstly, *BRCA1/2* germline or somatic
mutation status was assessed using targeted high-throughput sequencing,
and *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation was evaluated using a targeted DNA
methylation assay. Tumors without *BRCA*loss (*BRCA1/2* somatic or
germline mutation, or *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation) were tested for
Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and *CCNE1*-amplification

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0006
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
lation was evaluated using a targeted DNA
methylation assay. Tumors without *BRCA*loss (*BRCA1/2* somatic or
germline mutation, or *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation) were tested for
Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and *CCNE1*-amplification
using shallow whole genome sequencing and dedicated algorithms (see
Methods). We categorized 174 tumors (**S. Fig. 1a)** into four
clinically and molecularly relevant groups (*BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous
Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification*)
[^17^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JzylKC). The rest (n=91) of
the tumors were left "unassigned" as they could not be reliably
categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see Methods).

To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used
hyperplexed imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)
targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing tissue
microarrays (TMAs) (**Fig. 1b, S. Table 2**). Image processing and
quality filtering produced 929 single-cell maps of HGSC from 253
patients (**Fig. 1a, S. Fig. 1b**). In total, the dataset consists of
4,644,485 high-quality single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for
each tumor. The cell phenotyping was based on the multidimensional
protein expression using two sequential approaches: 1) Per-tumor
detection of cancer cells through unsupervised clustering based on
epithelial marker expression and, 2) Annotation of stromal and immune
populations using TRIBUS
[^18^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Ccs1a0) - a tool that
performs highly granular unsupervised clustering, followed by automated
cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping scheme
(**S. Fig. 1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell phenotyping
**(Fig.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ogle-docs/?Ccs1a0) - a tool that
performs highly granular unsupervised clustering, followed by automated
cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping scheme
(**S. Fig. 1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell phenotyping
**(Fig. 1c,d)** by visual assessment of the cell annotation masks
overlayed with the highly multiplexed images via customized Napari
plugin [^19^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KC8IZg) (**Fig.
1e,f**). The annotated cell phenotypes displayed marker expression
profiles corresponding to their respective cell lineages (**S. Fig.
1d**). The deep cell phenotypes annotation comprised two non-immune
stromal populations (annotated stromal) constituting 40% of the cells
(CD31+ endothelial cells and Vimentin+, α-SMA+ fibroblasts) and 15
immune populations corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells,
memory and regulatory T cells, and Myeloid lineages such as macrophages,
neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells (**Fig. 1g,h**). The per-sample
abundance of CD8+ T and CD20+ B cells aligned with the expert
pathologists' annotations based on immunohistochemistry (IHC) (**S. Fig.
1e,f**). When visualized based on their marker expression using uniform
manifold approximation and projection (UMAP), the cells formed distinct
visual clusters that aligned with our cell type annotations (**Fig.
1d,h**). Following cell phenotyping, we quantified functional cell
states, including Ki67 (**Fig. 1i, S. Fig. 1g**) and PDL1 expression
(**S. Fig. 1g**). This analysis enabled us to explore lineage-specific
phenotypes, such as the higher expression of the proliferative marker
Ki67 in immune cell populations compared to stromal cells, and increased
expression of the immune checkpoint inhibitor PDL1 in neutrophils and

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0008
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
abled us to explore lineage-specific
phenotypes, such as the higher expression of the proliferative marker
Ki67 in immune cell populations compared to stromal cells, and increased
expression of the immune checkpoint inhibitor PDL1 in neutrophils and
CD4+ T cells relative to stromal cells. Notably, some of these
phenotypic patterns have been associated with long-term survival in HGSC
[^20^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dbpuvG). Additionally, we
quantified the cell-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods (**Fig. 1j**).

To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells
[^21^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QFXbxo), we clustered the
cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell phenotypic markers,
mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell morphology features. Using
unsupervised consensus clustering (see Methods), we uncovered four
distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations (**Fig. 1k to m**). These
populations exhibited distinct molecular and morphological
characteristics. The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high
expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the
Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell
area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle
cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers
(**Fig. 1k, l; Ext. material - Fig. 1, 2**).

### **Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles** 

We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the
defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all
molecular groups did not reveal statistically significant differences
after multiple hypothesis testing (**S. Fig. 2a, S. Table 3**). However,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0009
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
patterns of immune infiltration across the
defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all
molecular groups did not reveal statistically significant differences
after multiple hypothesis testing (**S. Fig. 2a, S. Table 3**). However,
consistent with previous reports
[^8,22^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UYqibr), we observed that
*BRCA*loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and CD11c.MY,
specifically after chemotherapy exposure (**Fig. 2a**). Notably, a
higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed exclusively in
*BRCA1*loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples (**Fig. 2a**). When
comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found that CD8+ T cells,
effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages were
enriched in IDS samples (**S. Fig. 2b**). Interestingly, all lymphocyte
populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated significant
co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (**Fig. 2b, S. Table 4,
5**). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages, did not
exhibit this co-infiltration (**Fig. 2b**). These results imply that
lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated process
potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms.

We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with
clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the
abundance of lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival
(OS) (**Fig. 2c**, **S. Fig. 2c**) and progression-free survival (PFS)
(**S. Fig. 2d**). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells
also showed association with longer PFS (**S. Fig. 2d**). Notably, in
IDS tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic
significance, suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0010
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells
also showed association with longer PFS (**S. Fig. 2d**). Notably, in
IDS tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic
significance, suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to
chemotherapy exposure beyond immune cell abundance are relevant for
prognosis, as previously suggested
[^23,24^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dYxZqE). To note, the
highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the
interpretability of individual and multivariable hazard ratios.

For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant
differences across the molecular profiles (**S. Fig. 2e**). However, the
EMT phenotype was enriched in BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS
samples (**S. Fig. 2e**). The relative abundance of these cancer cell
phenotypes was associated with OS (**Fig. 2d**) and survival PFS (**S.
Fig. 2f**), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For
example, a higher abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was
associated with shorter OS in IDS tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT
cluster was linked to improved OS in PDS but not in IDS (**Fig. 2d**).
We next performed multivariable Cox models using stricter inclusion
criteria for patients in the analyses (minimum of two tumor cores and
more than 5% of cancer cells) (**S. Fig. 1b**) further confirming
differential associations with clinical outcomes (**S. Fig. 2g**).
Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship between immune
cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the
clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor\'s
exposure to chemotherapy.

**Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
chemotherapy exposure**

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0011
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
e
cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the
clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor\'s
exposure to chemotherapy.

**Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
chemotherapy exposure**

To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells
based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We
uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter
referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN) (**Fig. 2e to
g**). Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal
(RCN01 to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells
adjacent to cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09
and RCN10) and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal
spatial domains included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and
an endothelial cell-rich domain (RCN03) (**Fig. 2e to g**). The cancer
spatial domains included the phenotypically most heterogeneous
neighbourhood (RCN04), and four neighbourhoods were predominated by
distinct tumor cell functional states (**Fig. 2e to g**). The immune
spatial domains included an immune B cell neighborhood (RCN13),
tentatively consisting of tertiary lymphoid structures (**S. Fig. 2h**),
and neighborhood of myeloid cells (RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11) (**Fig. 2e
to g**). These spatial domains were validated with visual inspection of
the t-CycIF images (**Fig. 2f, g; S. Fig. 2h**).\
\
Spatial composition analysis revealed that several of the domains were
intercorrelated, suggesting spatially coordinated TME domains (**Fig.
2h, S. Table 6, 7**). For example, two immune domains (RCN11 - T cells,
RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation with each other, as well

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0012
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
led that several of the domains were
intercorrelated, suggesting spatially coordinated TME domains (**Fig.
2h, S. Table 6, 7**). For example, two immune domains (RCN11 - T cells,
RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation with each other, as well
as with the immune-infiltrated stroma (RCN02). In contrast, the
immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation with these immune
domains (**Fig. 2h**). The cancer cell domains also revealed significant
correlations with other spatial domains. For instance, the neighborhood
of cancer idle phenotype (RCN01) was positively correlated with the
immune-cold tumor-stromal interface (TSI), whereas the neighborhood of
cancer cells displaying immunogenic phenotypes was negatively correlated
with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01) (**Fig. 2h**). These findings
suggest that the spatial domains of the TME are spatially coordinated in
HGSC.

We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the
clinicomolecular profiles. The overall comparisons did not reveal
significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple
hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most
heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in
*BRCA*loss tumors (**Fig. 2i, j**). Interestingly, comparison of the
spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of
stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01 and RCN02), and
immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and
proliferative tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples (**Fig.
2k**). Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less
abundant in the IDS samples (**Fig. 2k**) suggesting phenotypic
convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy,
consistent with our previous observation

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0013
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
les (**Fig.
2k**). Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less
abundant in the IDS samples (**Fig. 2k**) suggesting phenotypic
convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy,
consistent with our previous observation
[^23^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?j0RoKe). These results
highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies associated with the
molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure.

**Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical
outcomes**

Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the
highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, **Fig. 2e**),
exhibiting the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs.
Statistical comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that
immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of T
cells, myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence
of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI (**Fig. 2l**). Consistent
with our previous observations
[^8^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KJYDUG), the *BRCA*loss tumors
contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated TSI (**Fig. 2j**).
By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was more abundant in the
IDS samples compared to PDS (**Fig. 2k**), was significantly enriched in
fibroblast stromal cells (**Fig. 2l**). Additionally, the immune-cold
TSI showed a higher prevalence of \'Idle\'-type cancer cells lacking
activity-linked phenotypic marker expressions, indicative of an
\'immune-evasive\' TSI where cancer cells could evade the recognition of
the immune system (**Fig. 2l**). Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI
(RCN10) had significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0014
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ker expressions, indicative of an
\'immune-evasive\' TSI where cancer cells could evade the recognition of
the immune system (**Fig. 2l**). Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI
(RCN10) had significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched
proliferative, immunogenic and EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive
"immune escape" mechanisms at the TSI
[^25^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kyN7aO).

Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical
outcomes. Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy
sequence (PDS/IDS), we identified three RCNs (RCN03, RCN09, RCN10)
significantly associated with both PFS and OS (**S. Fig. 2i, j**).
Notably, the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition
emerged as a key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across
the entire patient cohort (**Fig. 2m**). Specifically, a high abundance
of the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved
prognosis, whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated
with poorer outcomes (**Fig. 2m**).

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial
domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes
within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse
spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The
intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial
organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a
coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the
necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features
associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.

### **CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features**

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
izes the
necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features
associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.

### **CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features**

To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune
infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature
Importance Identification by RAndom forest), a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) framework (**Fig. 3a**). The primary objective of
CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within
high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample
groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and
the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter
tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training
and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent
overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a
proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3)
Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen
data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to
minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features
using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the
previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve
a consensus estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source
software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional data from each sample,
such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles
(**Fig. 3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0016
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ach sample,
such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles
(**Fig. 3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig. 1e, i; S. Fig. 1g**), morphological features and
spatial neighborhoods (**Fig. 1j**), resulting in 43 feature
representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover
multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we
stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium,
and short OS (**S. Fig. 3a,b**). We generated CEFIIRA models for either
cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like
(*BRCA*loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, *CCNE1*amp), and in PDS or IDS
tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential
confounders. As expected, the patient\'s age and tumor stage were the
top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (**S. Fig.
3c,d**). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited better
classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in both
PDS (**Fig. 3b, S. Table 8**) and IDS (**Fig. 3c, S. Table 8**),
indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential
prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell
spatial data.

### **Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with of overall survival** 

Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as
one of the key features for long or short OS prediction in both PDS and
IDS samples (**S. Fig. 3c,d**). Notably, of the key features, cancer
cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment
in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients
(**Fig.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0017
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
hort OS prediction in both PDS and
IDS samples (**S. Fig. 3c,d**). Notably, of the key features, cancer
cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment
in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients
(**Fig. 3d, e; S. Table 8**). By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin,
α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples
were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression
was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (**Fig. 3d**). The
prevalence of stromal and immune markers in cancer cell models suggested
the relevance of signals from neighboring cells at the TSI. Indeed,
visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed MHCII expression by
cancer cells, and that the Vimentin, CD3d, and PD-1 signals were
primarily signals originating from distinct neighboring cells adjacent
to the cancer cells (**Fig. 3f; S. Fig. 3e**). In addition, MHCII was
also the top predictive marker in the immune-stromal cells in IDS
samples (**Fig. 3e)**. Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII
and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial
proximity, are relevant for OS prediction regardless of therapy
sequence.

Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular
profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of
individual cells. We observed that PDS samples demonstrated superior
classification performance than IDS samples (**Fig. 3g**, **S. Fig.
3f**). In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher
expression in the HRD-like tumors (**Fig. 3h, S. Fig. 3g**). The CEFIIRA
model to classify *BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest
predictive performance (Median F1=0.77, **Fig. 3g**), with MHCII being

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0018
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
odels, MHCII was a key feature with higher
expression in the HRD-like tumors (**Fig. 3h, S. Fig. 3g**). The CEFIIRA
model to classify *BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest
predictive performance (Median F1=0.77, **Fig. 3g**), with MHCII being
the most differential feature displaying higher expression in *BRCA*loss
cancer cells (**S. Fig. 3g, h**), also confirmed from the t-CycIF images
(**Fig. 3i**). These results indicate that cancer-intrinsic MHCII
expression is enriched in the HRD tumors.

We next aimed to characterize the heterogeneity of MHCII expression both
across and within tumors. We observed that 17% of cancer cells exhibited
MHCII expression levels comparable to or exceeding those of CD11c+MY
cells (**S. Fig. 3i**). Tumors showed variable proportions of MHCII+
cancer cells (**Fig. 3j**), with these proportions positively correlated
with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer cells (R = 0.45, p = 1.7e-13; **S.
Fig. 3j**). However, several tumors displayed high MHCII expression
coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant pattern, as
confirmed through t-CycIF image analysis (**S. Fig. 3k, l**). This
suggests that MHCII expression in cancer cells can occur independently
of MHCI expression.

To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted
Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not
assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA
analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells consistently predicted longer OS (**Fig. 3k**), further
strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and
prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy
sequence.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0019
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
rt, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells consistently predicted longer OS (**Fig. 3k**), further
strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and
prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy
sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression
by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The
Cancer Genome Atlas
(TCGA)[^5^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ooK6Nh), by inferring
the cancer-cell-specific MHCII expression from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM
[^26^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?x7bdcN). Notably, the
observed MHCII expression was not associated with genomic alterations
(somatic mutations, loss of heterozygosity, or promoter
hypermethylation) in the MHCII gene (**S. Fig. 3l**). However, its
expression was higher in *BRCA*loss tumors compared to HRP and
*CCNE1*amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results (**S. Fig. 3m**).
Consistently using adjusted Cox models, we observed that high MHCII
expression by cancer cells predicted better OS in this independent
cohort at the mRNA level (**Fig. 3l, m**). These findings confirm that
MHCII expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of improved
clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct tumor-immune
interactions within the TME.

### **Cancer MHCII expression associates with immune infiltration** 

To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune
populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor.
This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells
associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and
M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (**Fig. 4a**). To corroborate these

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0020
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor.
This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells
associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and
M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (**Fig. 4a**). To corroborate these
findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer
cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted
data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found that tumors with high
MHCII expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T
cells and M1-like macrophages (**S. Fig. 4a**). In contrast, tumors with
high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like
macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for
anti-tumor responses (**S. Fig. 4a**). Overall, these results
demonstrate that HGSC tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer
cells have more immune infiltration in the TME.

To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and
underlying transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both
from PDS (N=29), and IDS (n=22), from 29 patients using single-cell RNA
sequencing[^23^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WIwitO) (scRNA-seq)
(**Fig. 4b**). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which included
25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal cells, and
97,409 immune cells, annotated using canonical phenotypic markers as
described previously [^23^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?AKE0ew)
(see Methods). Using gene signature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a
high MHCII expression level similar to that of canonical
antigen-presenting cells (**S. Fig. 4b**). The proportion of MHCII
positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable intra-patient heterogeneity
(**S. Fig.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0021
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
nature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a
high MHCII expression level similar to that of canonical
antigen-presenting cells (**S. Fig. 4b**). The proportion of MHCII
positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable intra-patient heterogeneity
(**S. Fig. 4c,d**), prompting us to conduct sample-based downstream
analyses. We observed that samples with a high proportion of MHCII+
cancer cells had increased proportions of type 17 proinflammatory helper
CD4+ T cells [^27^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NPNcoD), central
memory (CM) CD8+ T cells, related to activation after antigen exposure
[^28,29^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dOy1NH), and precursor
exhausted (PEX) CD8+ T cells, which have traits of both exhaustion and
memory [^30^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?igDOU3) (**Fig. 4c to
e**). Similar results were observed at the patient level taking only PDS
samples; however, for one of these immune populations (CM CD8+ T cells),
statistical significance was not reached, due to the smaller sample size
**(S. Fig. 4e**). Altogether, these findings suggest that MHCII+ cancer
cells influence immune infiltration by promoting antigen presentation
and driving proinflammatory responses.

### **Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity**

To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial
organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression
across the cancer cell spatial domains (RCN04 to RCN08). Our analysis
revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (**S. Fig. 4f**),
abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers,
including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (**Fig. 1l**). This indicates that
MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0022
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
CII expression within RCN05 (**S. Fig. 4f**),
abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers,
including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (**Fig. 1l**). This indicates that
MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the
cancer cell spatial compartments.

We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII
expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two
TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII
in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to
the immune-cold TSI (RCN9) (**S. Fig. 4f**). Consistently, analysis of
the cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells
resided more often in close proximity to immune cells as compared to
MHCII- cancer cells across the diverse clinicomolecular profiles (**Fig.
4f, S. Fig. 4g**). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly
neighboring stromal cells. Visual inspection of the tumor tissues
confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune
cells, forming immune 'hotspots', while MHCII- cells were embedded in
immune 'cold' tumor niches (**Fig. 4g**).

To expand our analyses to whole tissue sections, we generated
high-dimensional single-cell spatial data using t-CycIF on 15 HRP-IDS
samples (**Fig. 4h**) from an independent cohort in the Oncosys-OVA
prospective trial (NCT06117384). We captured and phenotyped 10.5 million
cells using the TRIBUS (**Fig. 4i,** **S. Fig. 4h**). In line with
previous findings, we observed heterogeneity of cancer-intrinsic MHCII
expression within and across the tumors (**S. Fig. 4i,j**).
Consistently, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells associated with
longer progression-free survival (PFS) (**Fig. 4j**). To note, CD8+

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0023
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
observed heterogeneity of cancer-intrinsic MHCII
expression within and across the tumors (**S. Fig. 4i,j**).
Consistently, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells associated with
longer progression-free survival (PFS) (**Fig. 4j**). To note, CD8+
T-cell infiltration did not associate with PFS (**S. Fig. 4k**), in
agreement with the observation in the multiomics cohort, underscoring
the presence of tumor-intrinsic factors influencing prognosis in IDS
samples. Importantly, we observed that the MHCII+ cancer cells (**S.
Fig. 4i**) had significantly more neighboring CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and CD11C+
antigen presenting Myeloid cells as compared to the MHCII- cells,
generating spatial immune hotspots (**Fig. 4k**). This additional
single-cell spatial analysis further highlights that MHCII+ cancer cells
colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains, enriched in T-cells and
antigen presenting cells.

To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer
cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the matched tumor and adjacent
stromal regions TSI in 11 HGSC-IDS tumors (**Fig. 4l**). Using
deconvolution, we observed that tumor MHCII expression was associated
with a significantly higher inferred proportion of particularly
dendritic cells and T cells in the adjacent stroma of the TSI (**Fig.
4m,n**). Altogether, these findings demonstrate that MHCII+ cancer cells
colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains and associate with spatial
immune infiltration and antigen presentation at the TSI.

### **Cancer-intrinsic MHCII associates with single-cell and spatial immune activation**

Next, we set out to uncover transcriptional programs that underpin the
immune infiltration associated with the MHCII+ cancer cells.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0024
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
n presentation at the TSI.

### **Cancer-intrinsic MHCII associates with single-cell and spatial immune activation**

Next, we set out to uncover transcriptional programs that underpin the
immune infiltration associated with the MHCII+ cancer cells.
Interestingly, tumors with a high MHCII expression exhibited upregulated
genes related to immune activation, including chemokines, cytokines,
immune checkpoints, granzymes, and immune receptors, compared to tumors
with low MHCII expression in the TCGA dataset
[^31^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Zj0zB2) (**S. Fig. 5a, b, S.
Table 9**). Notably, we observed a significantly augmented expression of
the immune checkpoint molecules TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+
tumors. Gene overrepresentation analysis
[^32^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Nb4L3T) revealed 14 enriched
pathways related to immune activation (**Fig. 5a**), including both PD-1
and chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways were enriched
within the under-expressed genes. Consistently, in DEG analysis of
cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (**Fig. 4b**), we found that MHCII
positivity was associated with upregulation of the tumor-suppressors
*RARRES3* and *CLU* associated with immune infiltration
[^33--35^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?CY52Q6) (**S. Fig. 5c**).

Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort.
For this, we utilized expression data of 335 genes profiled with the
Nanostring technology
[^36^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?aLNeNd). Consistently, a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of
chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (**S.
Fig. 5d, S. Table 10**). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0025
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
oogle-docs/?aLNeNd). Consistently, a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of
chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (**S.
Fig. 5d, S. Table 10**). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11
chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with interferon-gamma
(IFN-γ) activation
[^37--39^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?C6OoZ9), were among the
top overexpressed genes as in TCGA, suggesting that IFN activation plays
a key role in MHCII-associated immune activation, as previously
suggested also in other cancers
[^40--42^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jG9TyK).

To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ
signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously
available spatial transcriptomic dataset
[^43^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Qw6hIE) incorporating expert
pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses
(**Fig. 5b**, **S. Fig. 5e**). The annotated TME areas exhibited
distinct transcriptomic profiles visualized in differential clusters in
a UMAP projection (**S. Fig. 5f**). Using MHCII and IFN-γ signatures
[^44^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?eOgUDG) (see Methods), we
found that the MHCII expression was significantly correlated with IFN-γ
signature expression across distinct tumor areas, including cancer and
TSI regions (**Fig. 5c**). Moreover, a higher MHCII signature and
increased IFN-γ in both cancer and TSI spatial regions predicted
improved clinical responses to chemotherapy at spot level (**S. Fig.
5g**,**h**), and similar trends were observed at the patient level,
although statistical significance was not reached due to the smaller
sample size (**S. Fig. 5i**,**j**). Interestingly, in our t-CycIF

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
onses to chemotherapy at spot level (**S. Fig.
5g**,**h**), and similar trends were observed at the patient level,
although statistical significance was not reached due to the smaller
sample size (**S. Fig. 5i**,**j**). Interestingly, in our t-CycIF
spatial data (multiomics cohort), statistical analysis revealed
significantly higher expression of phospho-STAT1 (pSTAT1), indicative of
IFN activation in CD8+ and CD4+ T cells and antigen-presenting (CD11c+)
myeloid cells neighboring MHCII+ cancer cells at the TSI (**Fig. 5d**),
confirmed via visual inspection (**Fig. 5e, S. Fig. 5k**). These
findings suggest that cancer cell MHCII associates with spatially
coordinated activation of antigen presentation and T cells via IFN
signaling at the TSI. Further, using the scRNA-seq dataset, we observed
that the MHCII expression in cancer cells correlated with *IFNG* gene
expression specifically in CD4+ memory helper and CD8+ central memory
(CM) T cells, which are related to activation after antigen exposure
(**Fig. 5f**). Overall, these findings reinforce the link between
cancer-intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation and highlight the role of
cancer cell MHCII in spatial immune activation.

To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in
MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict
ligand--receptor pairs (**Fig. 5g, Ext. material - Fig.4**). We
identified the top seven ligand--receptor interactions across CD8⁺ T
cells, CD4⁺ T cells, Tregs, and DCs (**Fig. 5h**). Interestingly, IFN
signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in
CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and
APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0027
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
Cs (**Fig. 5h**). Interestingly, IFN
signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in
CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and
APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions
across the cell populations, which may indicate the induction of immune
tolerance [^45^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?FqCnnD). Similarly,
in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (**Fig. 5i**),
we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII
ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL,
IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (**Fig. 5j**). In addition, VEGFA, one of
the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative
correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell
interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at
the TSI (**Fig. 5j**). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells
elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the
surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with
activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.

### **Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity and enhanced immunotherapy response *in vitro***

Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when
antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous
misfolded proteins [^46^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1PQcHJ).
To explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with
specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified
antigens detected via HLA ligandome analysis in HGSC
[^47^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LctNso) (**Fig. 6a**). We

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0028
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
lore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with
specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified
antigens detected via HLA ligandome analysis in HGSC
[^47^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LctNso) (**Fig. 6a**). We
found that the deconvoluted expression of MHCII in cancer cells is
indeed associated with the expression of most antigen-encoding genes in
the TCGA dataset (**Fig. 6b**). However, in our scRNA-seq dataset, only
four specific antigen genes (UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN) were expressed
in the cancer cells, and associated with tumor MHCII expression (**Fig.
6c**). In our GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also observed
positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the
expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at
the TSI (**Fig. 6d**).

To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in
HGSC, we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides in
12 HGSC samples alongside TCR repertoire analysis based on bulk RNA-seq
(**Fig. 6e**). We identified the presence of previously reported genes
from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets that showed
significant correlations and were consistently observed across the
patients (**Fig. 6f**). Moreover, the genes enriched in samples with a
high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells were significantly enriched in
cellular components related to junctions, adhesion, the extracellular
matrix, and cell--cell contact zones, as well as in biological processes
involved in protein localization and regulation (**Fig. 6g,h**). To
explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen
presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0029
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
contact zones, as well as in biological processes
involved in protein localization and regulation (**Fig. 6g,h**). To
explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen
presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We
observed a significantly increased number of T cell receptor
clones---specifically TRAD and TRB ---in samples with a high MHCII
cancer proportion (**Fig. 6i**).

Given this association with enhanced T cell presence, we next
investigated whether tumor MHCII status influences CD8+ T-cell responses
to immunotherapy using our previously established immuno-oncology
platform of three-dimensional patient-derived immune-competent cultures
(iPDCs) ^16^. For this, we analysed flow cytometry data from drug
perturbation assays of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five
chemo-treated tumor samples (**Fig. 6j**). The iPDCs were stratified
into MHCII-high and MHCII-low groups based on baseline HLA-DR expression
in EpCAM+ cancer cells compared to immune and myeloid populations (see
**Methods** and **S. Fig. 6a,b**). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited
significantly greater CD8+ T-cell cytotoxic responses to pembrolizumab
(**Fig. 6k, S. Table 11**). Sample-by-sample analysis revealed a
statistically significant cytotoxicity gain (p \< 0.05) between baseline
and pembrolizumab-treated conditions in the MHCII-high group (**Fig.
6l**).

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen
presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of
distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to
T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore
contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context
of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.

## **DISCUSSION**

gens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to
T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore
contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context
of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.

## **DISCUSSION**

We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem
using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280
real-world clinical samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified
distinct spatial domains with varying phenotypic compositions. These
intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor cell phenotypic
profiles and clinicomolecular groups, underscoring the role of molecular
features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the
two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated
with cancer cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while
the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to
poorer outcomes.

Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated
high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key
prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of \~15.1 million
cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells
within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate immune
hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity
with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly
with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells
have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and
breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
[^48,49^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WgCdGj), and the spatial
contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells was reported

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0031
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and
breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
[^48,49^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?WgCdGj), and the spatial
contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells was reported
recently in triple-negative breast cancer
[^50^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NltuKr). While IFN expression
in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent
evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
[^51^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?aKDvHz). Through a robust
spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially
cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and
the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII
correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory
T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present
antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that
MHCII and *IFN-γ* expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated
that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of
neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at
the TSI. These findings, summarized in **Fig. 7**, support the
hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and
CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at
the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC.

MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under
evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in
antigen presentation [^52^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?vso6Hh).
In HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0032
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
n reported previously to be under
evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in
antigen presentation [^52^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?vso6Hh).
In HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs
undergoing whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with
poor prognosis [^53^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SbNXa0).
Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an
improved response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung
cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer
[^41,50,54,55^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7zpUhp). However,
clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected
patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated only modest
benefits [^56^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?I8lvh7). In
contrast, our *in vitro* analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs
suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might
enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic
activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our
study revealed that other immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT
and CTLA4, were overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression,
further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune
checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC
[^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?mvREEE). Moreover, we showed
that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors,
and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality,
supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While
tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0033
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors,
and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality,
supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While
tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity
cycle via active antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches
should be developed for patients with tumors lacking MHCII expression.
In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential
mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the
clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future
therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.

**Study limitations**

We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the
opportunities for future refinement and exploration. The retrospective
design and reliance on archival tissues may introduce biases related to
sample preservation and heterogeneity, limiting some of the statistical
comparisons between patients. Our protein marker panel, while
comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential
cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in
independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of
deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic
and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating
robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve
predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable
hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger
prospective patient cohorts.

## **MATERIALS & METHODS**

### ***Cohort description***

We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV
tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek
Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and
Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and
December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive
surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval
debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based
chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or
PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from
the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained
from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA)
^57^.

Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking
surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by
three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were
extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE
blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with
one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from
the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
previously described [^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gaaOUU)

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0039
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
eas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
previously described [^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gaaOUU)
and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration
detected with t-CycIF.

### ***Whole slide cohort description***

We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the
ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from
patients during interval debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection
criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status, a tumor purity
greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS)
of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R \>
0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that
underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova
clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to
define long-PFS (\>=12 months) and short-PFS (\<12 months) in the
survival analysis.

### ***Molecular profile assignment***

For the TMA cohort, we first identified *BRCA1/2* mutation and *BRCA1*
promoter methylation status of each sample as previously described
[^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SdxgQX). In brief, *BRCA1/2*
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the
Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a
*BRCA1/2* germline mutation, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0040
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
on, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and
Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent
probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation.
Details of the number of tumors with *BRCA1* or *BRCA2* mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter methylation are reported in **S. Table 1**.

Next, tumors without a *BRCA1/2* mutation or *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using
Illumina HiSeq2500 to determine a *BRCA*-like CNV profile
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qGUBcI) and
*CCNE1*-amplification status as described previously
[^57^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y06swk). In addition, using
the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software
shallowHRD [^59^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6XQwP9). Finally,
we categorized the tumors as *BRCAloss*, HRD, HRP, and *CCNE1*amp using
a hierarchical approach described in **S. Fig. 1a**. Samples that failed
QC for *BRCA1/2* targeted DNA-sequencing, *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV analysis, were labeled as "Unassigned"
(or Other). In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between
shallowHRD and the *BRCA-like* CNV profile
[^58^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Y35pzZ) were labeled as
"Unassigned".

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
(RRID:SCR_027138) [^60^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?njGwIx).
For all analyses, only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological
grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
included.

RD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
(RRID:SCR_027138) [^60^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?njGwIx).
For all analyses, only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological
grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
included. For the whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically
validated HRD classification performed at the Helsinki University
Hospital, Finland, which uses ovaHRDscar, and the detection of somatic
and germline mutations and gene deletions in *BRCA1/2* genes.

### ***Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing***

To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor
tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the
whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and
the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots.
The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (**S. Table
2**) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the
t-CycIF protocol [^61^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?FYaR9w). The
panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main
cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images' illumination was
corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371)
[^62^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?wLnfGG). The per-image tiles
were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266,
[[https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar]{.underline}](https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar)).
Individual TMA's cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox
on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts,
United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
(RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2
[^63^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fuk3P2) and Cellprofiler

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0042
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
Image Processing Toolbox
on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts,
United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
(RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2
[^63^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fuk3P2) and Cellprofiler
(RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8
[^64^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zuypdM) using 2-pixel nuclear
expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and morphological
features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter, and Roundness
were quantified using MATLAB. Quality filtering was applied based on
changes in the DNA channel intensity across cycles
([[https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite]{.underline}](https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite)),
image regions with artifacts, and TMA's cores (n=64) with less than 1000
high-quality cells.

For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections
were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex
validated antibody panel (**S. Table 12**) and scanned with a RareCyte
CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol. The image tiles were
stitched with ASHLAR. For WSI, we developed a more scalable analysis
pipeline
([[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)).
Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei
segmentation in StarDist
[^65^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gOhXek), implementing the
pre-trained model '*2D_versatile_flu*o\'\'. Then, the mean intensity of
each channel was quantified by our in-house script based on skimage
package [^66^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RBeQSO). Image
quality control was performed by CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157)
[^67^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6NA1Xm).

of
each channel was quantified by our in-house script based on skimage
package [^66^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RBeQSO). Image
quality control was performed by CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157)
[^67^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6NA1Xm).

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were
clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers
(Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the
R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and
cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the
0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel\'s signal
was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by
marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized
map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM
node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S. Fig. 1c**) by
using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0044
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
bal cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using
unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367)
[^18^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ohnqa1). Outliers were
removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity,
normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5
[^68^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Td9ajN), and scaled by
Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S. Table 13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are
images S100_iOme and S110_iOme, which were manually gated with scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) [^69^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?0eresq) due
to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our
annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by
expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)
staining, showing agreement across samples (**S. Fig. 4l**). The MHCII+
cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the
annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### **Cancer cell phenotype clustering**

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md::chunk_0045
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
es (**S. Fig. 4l**). The MHCII+
cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the
annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### **Cancer cell phenotype clustering**

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed
outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method
section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based
on the expression levels of the markers: \"Ki67\", \"Vimentin\",
\"pSTAT1\", \"MHC-I\", \"ECadherin\", \"yH2AX\", \"pTBK1\", \"cPARP1\",
\"CK7\", \"PDL1_488\", and morphological features: \"Area\",
\"Eccentricity\", and \"Roundness\" into a 2500-node self-organized map
(SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were
clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954).
For this, the number of clusters (*k*) was assessed from 2 to 12. For
each value of *k* assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of
the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and
morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm
"partitioning around medoids" (pam) was used to cluster the selected
dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best
number of cancer meta-clusters, *k*, by assessing the resultant
consensus-clustering matrix for each value of *k* and its cumulative
density distribution (**Ext. material - Fig. 3**). The cancer
meta-clusters were named according to the expression of relevant markers
in each one (**Ext. material - Fig. 2**).

### ***Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis***

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality
control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius
of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap
