## **Abstract**

The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant--host cell
interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian
carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive
single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients,
integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929
single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically
heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell
co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts
clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII
expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation
with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic
datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with
local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR
clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show
that tumor MHCII expression associates with increased CD8+ T cell
cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade, while blocking MHCII abrogates this
activation. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune
activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.

## **INTRODUCTION**

The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions
[(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nfS5Pn). The continuous
crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is
fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression,
and therapeutic resistance.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
interactions
[(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nfS5Pn). The continuous
crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is
fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression,
and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC)
is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer,
characterized by high genomic instability
[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UliKHh) and significant tumor
heterogeneity [(3)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1lvJx8). The
genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For
instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair
deficiency, such as those with *BRCA1/2* mutations, have been suggested
to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration
[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jcEirr) and better responses
to combinations with immunotherapies
[(5,6)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9vmQCT). Moreover, we and
others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate
interactions within the immune compartment
[(7--9)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yzi8KV), resulting in
spatially confined T-cell exhaustion
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?EjZUnX). Therefore, it is
crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and
molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve
patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for
patients with HGSC.

We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1
million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with
comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
ent strategies for
patients with HGSC.

We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1
million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with
comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging
DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a
tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the
single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that
associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning
tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to
uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of
cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at
the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of
immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate
immune hotspots marked by increased activation, and tumor reactivity
impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles.
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition
ex-vivo. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune
cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the
way for targeted interventions in HGSC.

## **RESULTS**

### **Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors**

To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography,
clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic
profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing,
gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex
imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (**Fig.1a**). The

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0005
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
ar profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic
profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing,
gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex
imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (**Fig.1a**). The
tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from
treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after
platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS
n=158). Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes,
were collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67
months) (**S.Table1**). Most patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and
paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free
survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). We conducted a hierarchical
multidimensional analysis to categorize the 174 tumors (**S.Fig.1a)**
into four clinically and molecularly relevant groups (*BRCAloss, HRD,
Homologous Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification*)
[(11)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dchEuz). The rest (n=91) of
the tumors were left "unassigned" as they could not be reliably
categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see Methods).

To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used
hyperplexed imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)
targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing tissue
microarrays (TMAs) (**Fig.1b, S.Table2**). Image processing and quality
filtering produced 929 single-cell maps of HGSC from 253 patients
(**Fig.1a, S.Fig.1b**). In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485
high-quality single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for each
tumor. The cell phenotyping was based on the multidimensional protein
expression (**S.Fig.1c**).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0006
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
from 253 patients
(**Fig.1a, S.Fig.1b**). In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485
high-quality single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for each
tumor. The cell phenotyping was based on the multidimensional protein
expression (**S.Fig.1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell
phenotypes **(Fig.1c,d)** with visual assessment of the cell annotation
masks overlayed with the highly multiplexed images using Napari
(RRID:SCR_022765) (**Fig.1e,f**). The annotated cell phenotypes
displayed marker expression profiles corresponding to their respective
cell lineages (**S.Fig.1d**). The deep cell phenotype annotation
comprised two non-immune stromal populations (annotated stromal)
constituting 40% of the cells (CD31+ endothelial cells and Vimentin+,
α-SMA+ fibroblasts) and 15 immune populations corresponding to 17% of
the cells, including B cells, memory and regulatory T cells, and Myeloid
lineages such as macrophages, neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells
(**Fig.1g,h**). The per-sample abundance of CD8+ T and CD20+ B cells
aligned with the expert pathologists' annotations based on
immunohistochemistry (IHC) (**S.Fig.1e,f**). The cells formed distinct
visual clusters that aligned with our cell type annotations
(**Fig.1d,h**). Following cell phenotyping, we quantified functional
cell states, including Ki67 (**Fig.1i, S.Fig.1g**) and PDL1 expression
(**S.Fig.1g**), some of which have been associated with long-term
survival in HGSC [(12)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?o0yhBZ).
Additionally, we quantified the cell-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods
(**Fig.1j**).

To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells, we
clustered the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell
phenotypic markers, mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
l-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods
(**Fig.1j**).

To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells, we
clustered the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell
phenotypic markers, mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell
morphology features. Using unsupervised consensus clustering (see
Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations,
exhibiting distinct morphological and molecular characteristics
(**Fig.1k-m**). The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high
expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the
Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell
area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle
cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers
(**Fig.1k,l; Ext. material - Fig.1,2**).

### **Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles** 

We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the
defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all
molecular groups did not reveal significant differences after multiple
hypothesis correction (**S.Fig.2a, S.Table 3**). However, consistent
with previous reports [(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kxTuxY),
we observed that *BRCA*loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells
and CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (**Fig.2a**).
Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed
exclusively in *BRCA1*loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples
(**Fig.2a**). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found
that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and
macrophages were enriched in IDS samples (**S.Fig.2b**). Interestingly,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0008
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
rs in IDS, but not in PDS samples
(**Fig.2a**). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found
that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and
macrophages were enriched in IDS samples (**S.Fig.2b**). Interestingly,
all lymphocyte populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated
significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (**Fig.2b,
S.Table4,5**). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages,
did not exhibit this co-infiltration (**Fig.2b**). These results imply
that lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated
process potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms.

We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with
clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the
abundance of lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival
(OS) (**Fig.2c**, **S.Fig.2c**) and progression-free survival (PFS)
(**S.Fig.2d**). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells also
showed association with longer PFS (**S.Fig.2d**). Notably, in IDS
tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic significance,
suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to chemotherapy exposure
beyond immune cell abundance are relevant for prognosis, as previously
suggested [(10,13)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Bmv16s). To
note, the highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the
interpretability of individual and multivariable hazard ratios.

For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant
differences across the molecular profiles (**S.Fig.2e**). However, the
EMT phenotype was enriched in BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS
samples (**S.Fig.2e**). The relative abundance of these cancer cell

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0009
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
populations, we did not observe significant
differences across the molecular profiles (**S.Fig.2e**). However, the
EMT phenotype was enriched in BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS
samples (**S.Fig.2e**). The relative abundance of these cancer cell
phenotypes was associated with OS (**Fig.2d**) and survival PFS
(**S.Fig.2f**), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For
example, a higher abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was
associated with shorter OS in IDS tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT
cluster was linked to improved OS in PDS but not in IDS (**Fig.2d**). We
next performed multivariable Cox models using stricter inclusion
criteria for patients in the analyses (minimum of two tumor cores and
more than 5% of cancer cells) (**S.Fig.1b**) further confirming
differential associations with clinical outcomes (**S.Fig.2g**).
Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship between immune
cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the
clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor\'s
exposure to chemotherapy.

**Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
chemotherapy exposure**

To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells
based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We
uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter
referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN) (**Fig.2e-g**).
Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal (RCN01
to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells adjacent to
cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09 and RCN10)
and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial domains

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0010
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
dentified containing stromal (RCN01
to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells adjacent to
cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09 and RCN10)
and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial domains
included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial
cell-rich domain (RCN03) (**Fig.2e-g**). The cancer spatial domains
included the phenotypically most heterogeneous neighbourhood (RCN04),
and four neighbourhoods were predominated by distinct tumor cell
functional states (**Fig.2e-g**). The immune spatial domains included an
immune B cell neighborhood (RCN13), tentatively consisting of tertiary
lymphoid structures (**S.Fig.2h**), and neighborhood of myeloid cells
(RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11) (**Fig.2e-g**). These spatial domains were
validated with visual inspection of the t-CycIF images (**Fig.2f,g;
S.Fig.2h**).\
\
Spatial composition analysis revealed that several of the domains were
intercorrelated (**Fig.2h, S.Table6,7**). For example, two immune
domains (RCN11 - T cells, RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation
with each other, as well as with the immune-infiltrated stroma (RCN02).
In contrast, the immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation
with these immune domains (**Fig.2h**). The cancer cell domains also
revealed significant correlations with other spatial domains. For
instance, the neighborhood of cancer idle phenotype (RCN01) was
positively correlated with the immune-cold tumor-stromal interface
(TSI), whereas the neighborhood of cancer cells displaying immunogenic
phenotypes was negatively correlated with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01)
(**Fig.2h**). These findings suggest that the spatial domains of the TME
are functionally coordinated in HGSC.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0011
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
whereas the neighborhood of cancer cells displaying immunogenic
phenotypes was negatively correlated with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01)
(**Fig.2h**). These findings suggest that the spatial domains of the TME
are functionally coordinated in HGSC.

We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the
clinicomolecular profiles. The overall comparisons did not reveal
significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple
hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most
heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in
*BRCA*loss tumors (**Fig.2i,j**). Interestingly, comparison of the
spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of
stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01 and RCN02), and
immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and
proliferative tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples
(**Fig.2k**). Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was
less abundant in the IDS samples (**Fig.2k**) suggesting phenotypic
convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy,
consistent with our previous observation
[(7)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jfFZaD). These results
highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies associated with the
molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure.

**Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical
outcomes**

Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the
highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, **Fig.2e**), exhibiting
the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical
comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI
contained significantly higher proportions of T cells, myeloid cells,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0012
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
y index: 2.3, **Fig.2e**), exhibiting
the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical
comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI
contained significantly higher proportions of T cells, myeloid cells,
macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence of coordinated immune
infiltration at the TSI (**Fig.2l**). Consistent with our previous
observations [(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?k5VINq), the
*BRCA*loss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated
TSI (**Fig.2j**). By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was
more abundant in the IDS samples compared to PDS (**Fig.2k**), was
significantly enriched in fibroblast stromal cells (**Fig.2l**).
Additionally, the immune-cold TSI showed a higher prevalence of
\'Idle\'-type cancer cells lacking activity-linked phenotypic marker
expressions, indicative of an \'immune-evasive\' TSI where cancer cells
could evade the recognition of the immune system (**Fig.2l**).
Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) had significantly more
active cancer phenotypes, with enriched proliferative, immunogenic and
EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive "immune escape" mechanisms at the
TSI [(14)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ljZVzE).

Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical
outcomes. Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy
sequence (PDS/IDS), we identified three RCNs (RCN03, RCN09, RCN10)
significantly associated with both PFS and OS (**S.Fig.2i,j**). Notably,
the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition emerged as a
key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across the entire
patient cohort (**Fig.2m**). Specifically, a high abundance of the

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0013
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
h both PFS and OS (**S.Fig.2i,j**). Notably,
the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition emerged as a
key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across the entire
patient cohort (**Fig.2m**). Specifically, a high abundance of the
immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved prognosis,
whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated with poorer
outcomes (**Fig.2m**).

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial
domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes
within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse
spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The
intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial
organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a
coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the
necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features
associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.

### **CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features**

To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune
infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature
Importance Identification by RAndom forest), a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) framework (**Fig.3a**). The primary objective of
CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within
high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample
groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and
the identification of critical features (see Methods). Additionally, as
a versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0014
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
t differentiate between two sample
groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and
the identification of critical features (see Methods). Additionally, as
a versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional
data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and
molecular profiles (**Fig.3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig.1e,i; S.Fig.1g**), morphological features and spatial
neighborhoods (**Fig.1j**), resulting in 43 feature representations per
cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features that
differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into
three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (**S.Fig.3a,b**).
We generated CEFIIRA models for either cancer cells or the
immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (*BRCA*loss, HRD) or
HRP-like (HRP, *CCNE1*amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also
including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. This strategy
made it easier to interpret the predictive signal and dissect the
cell-type--specific contributions. As expected, the patient\'s age and
tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the
models (**S.Fig.3c,d**). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited
better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors
in both PDS (**Fig.3b, S.Table8**) and IDS (**Fig.3c, S.Table8**),
indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential
prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell
spatial data. To account for anatomical site heterogeneity, we trained

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
S.Table8**) and IDS (**Fig.3c, S.Table8**),
indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential
prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell
spatial data. To account for anatomical site heterogeneity, we trained
CEFIIRA models exclusively on primary tumor sites (adnex/ovary) and
confirmed that the top predicted features remained consistent (right
panel of **S.Fig.3o**).

### **Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival** 

Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as
one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples
(**S.Fig.3c,d**). Moreover, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed
the highest fold-change enrichment from long-OS patients compared to
short-OS patients in HRP tumors (**Fig.3d,e; S.Table8**). By contrast,
stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint
receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In
addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS
samples (**Fig.3d**). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in
cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring
cells at the TSI. However, the MHCII levels in cancer cells did not
correlate with CD3 or CD11b levels, confirming that this signal is not
due to technical spillover from neighboring T cells or myeloid cells
(**S.Fig.3j**). Indeed, visual inspection of the t-CycIF images
confirmed MHCII expression by cancer cells (**Fig.3f; S.Fig.3e**). We
also performed Xenium single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which
confirmed the co-localization of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII
complex, specifically on the tumor cells (**Fig.3f**). In addition,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0016
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
ion by cancer cells (**Fig.3f; S.Fig.3e**). We
also performed Xenium single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which
confirmed the co-localization of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII
complex, specifically on the tumor cells (**Fig.3f**). In addition,
MHCII was also the top predictive marker in the immune-stromal cells in
IDS samples (**Fig.3e)**. Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII
and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial
proximity, are relevant for OS prediction regardless of therapy
sequence.

Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular
profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of
individual cells. We observed that PDS samples demonstrated superior
classification performance than IDS samples (**Fig.3g**, **S.Fig.3f**).
In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher expression in
the HRD-like tumors (**Fig.3i, S.Fig.3g**). The CEFIIRA model to
classify *BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest predictive
performance (Median F1=0.77, **Fig.3h**), with MHCII being the most
differential feature displaying higher expression in *BRCA*loss cancer
cells (**S.Fig.3g,h**), also confirmed from the t-CycIF images
(**Fig.3i**). These results indicate that cancer-intrinsic MHCII
expression is enriched in the HRD tumors. Characterization of the
heterogeneity of MHCII expression both across and within tumors revealed
that 17% of cancer cells exhibited MHCII expression levels comparable to
or exceeding those of CD11c+MY cells (**S.Fig.3i**). Tumors showed
variable proportions of MHCII+ cancer cells (**Fig.3j**), with these
proportions positively correlated with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer
cells (R = 0.45, p = 1.7e-13; **S.Fig.3k**). However, several tumors

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0017
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
1c+MY cells (**S.Fig.3i**). Tumors showed
variable proportions of MHCII+ cancer cells (**Fig.3j**), with these
proportions positively correlated with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer
cells (R = 0.45, p = 1.7e-13; **S.Fig.3k**). However, several tumors
displayed high MHCII expression coupled with low MHCI expression in a
non-concordant pattern (**S.Fig.3k,l**). This suggests that MHCII
expression in cancer cells can occur independently of MHCI expression.

To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted
Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not
assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA
analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells consistently predicted longer OS (**Fig.3k**), further
strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and
prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy
sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression
by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The
Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YuY7N0), by inferring the
cancer-cell-specific MHCII expression from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM
(RRID:SCR_027136). Notably, the observed MHCII expression was not
associated with genomic alterations (somatic mutations, loss of
heterozygosity, or promoter hypermethylation) in the MHCII gene
(**S.Fig.3m**). However, its expression was higher in *BRCA*loss tumors
compared to HRP and *CCNE1*amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results
(**S.Fig.3n**). Consistently using adjusted Cox models, we observed that
high MHCII expression by cancer cells predicted better OS in this

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0018
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
s expression was higher in *BRCA*loss tumors
compared to HRP and *CCNE1*amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results
(**S.Fig.3n**). Consistently using adjusted Cox models, we observed that
high MHCII expression by cancer cells predicted better OS in this
independent cohort at the mRNA level (**Fig.3l,m**). These findings
confirm that MHCII expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of
improved clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct
tumor-immune interactions within the TME.

### **Cancer MHCII expression associates with immune infiltration** 

To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune
populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor.
This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells
associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and
M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (**Fig.4a**). To corroborate these
findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer
cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted
data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found that tumors with high
MHCII expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T
cells and M1-like macrophages (**S.Fig.4a**). In contrast, tumors with
high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like
macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for
anti-tumor responses (**S.Fig.4a**). Overall, these results demonstrate
that HGSC tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more
immune infiltration in the TME.

To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and
underlying transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0019
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
monstrate
that HGSC tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more
immune infiltration in the TME.

To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and
underlying transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both
from PDS (N=29), and IDS (n=22), from 29 patients using single-cell RNA
sequencing [(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U9R4Iw)
(scRNA-seq) (**Fig.4b**). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which
included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal
cells, and 97,409 immune cells, annotated using canonical phenotypic
markers as described previously
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YINNjV) (see Methods). Using
gene signature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a high MHCII expression
level similar to that of canonical antigen-presenting cells
(**S.Fig.4b**), while showing no expression of immune-related markers
(**S.Fig.4c**), supporting the accuracy of our annotations. The
proportion of MHCII positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable
intra-patient heterogeneity (**S.Fig.4d,e**), prompting us to conduct
sample-based downstream analyses. We observed that samples with a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells had increased proportions of type 17
proinflammatory helper CD4+ T cells, central memory (CM) CD8+ T cells,
related to activation after antigen exposure
[(15)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yQUz4s), and precursor
exhausted (PEX) CD8+ T cells, which have traits of both exhaustion and
memory [(16)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?AHXPJo)
(**Fig.4c-e**). Similar results were observed at the patient level
taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations
(CM CD8+ T cells), statistical significance was not reached, due to the
smaller sample size **(S.Fig.4f**).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0020
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
XPJo)
(**Fig.4c-e**). Similar results were observed at the patient level
taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations
(CM CD8+ T cells), statistical significance was not reached, due to the
smaller sample size **(S.Fig.4f**). Altogether, these findings suggest
that MHCII+ cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting
antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.

### **Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity**

To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial
organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression
across the cancer cell spatial domains (RCN04 to RCN08). Our analysis
revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (**S.Fig.4g**),
abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers,
including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (**Fig.1l**). This indicates that
MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the
cancer cell spatial compartments.

We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII
expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two
TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII
in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to
the immune-cold TSI (RCN9) (**S.Fig.4g**). Consistently, analysis of the
cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells resided
more often in close proximity to immune cells as compared to MHCII-
cancer cells across the diverse clinicomolecular profiles (**Fig.4f,
S.Fig.4h**). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly
neighboring stromal cells. Visual inspection of the tumor tissues
confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0021
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
e diverse clinicomolecular profiles (**Fig.4f,
S.Fig.4h**). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly
neighboring stromal cells. Visual inspection of the tumor tissues
confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune
cells, forming immune 'hotspots', while MHCII- cells were embedded in
immune 'cold' tumor niches (**Fig.4g**).

To expand our analyses to whole tissue sections, we generated
high-dimensional single-cell spatial data using t-CycIF on 15 HRP-IDS
samples (**Fig.4h**) from an independent cohort in the Oncosys-OVA
prospective trial (NCT06117384). We captured and phenotyped 10.5 million
cells using the TRIBUS (**Fig.4i,** **S.Fig.4i**). The abundance of
immune cells did not correlate with the fraction of genome altered,
tumor mutational burden, or the number of HRD scars (**Ext. material -
Fig.4**), indicating that differences in these genomic features are
unlikely to bias our immune infiltration analyses. In line with previous
findings, we observed heterogeneity of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression
within and across the tumors (**S.Fig.4i,j**). Consistently, a higher
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells associated with longer
progression-free survival (PFS) (**Fig.4j**). To note, CD8+ T-cell
infiltration did not associate with PFS (**S.Fig.4l**), in agreement
with the observation in the multiomics cohort, underscoring the presence
of tumor-intrinsic factors influencing prognosis in IDS samples.
Importantly, we observed that the MHCII+ cancer cells (**S.Fig.4j**) had
significantly more neighboring CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and CD11C+ antigen
presenting Myeloid cells as compared to the MHCII- cells, generating
spatial immune hotspots (**Fig.4k**). This additional single-cell

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0022
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
that the MHCII+ cancer cells (**S.Fig.4j**) had
significantly more neighboring CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and CD11C+ antigen
presenting Myeloid cells as compared to the MHCII- cells, generating
spatial immune hotspots (**Fig.4k**). This additional single-cell
spatial analysis further highlights that MHCII+ cancer cells colocalize
in immunoreactive spatial domains, enriched in T-cells and antigen
presenting cells.

To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer
cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the matched tumor and adjacent
stromal regions TSI in 11 HGSC-IDS tumors (**Fig.4l**). Using
deconvolution, we observed that tumor MHCII expression was associated
with a significantly higher inferred proportion of particularly
dendritic cells and T cells in the adjacent stroma of the TSI
(**Fig.4m,n**). Altogether, these findings demonstrate that MHCII+
cancer cells colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains and associate
with spatial immune infiltration and antigen presentation at the TSI.

### **Cancer-intrinsic MHCII associates with single-cell and spatial immune activation**

Next, we set out to uncover transcriptional programs that underpin the
immune infiltration associated with the MHCII+ cancer cells.
Interestingly, tumors with a high MHCII expression exhibited upregulated
genes related to immune activation, including chemokines, cytokines,
immune checkpoints, granzymes, and immune receptors, compared to tumors
with low MHCII expression in the TCGA dataset using DESeq2
(RRID:SCR_015687) (**S.Fig.5a,b, S.Table9**). Notably, we observed a
significantly augmented expression of the immune checkpoint molecules
TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+ tumors. Gene overrepresentation

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0023
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
ression in the TCGA dataset using DESeq2
(RRID:SCR_015687) (**S.Fig.5a,b, S.Table9**). Notably, we observed a
significantly augmented expression of the immune checkpoint molecules
TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+ tumors. Gene overrepresentation
analysis with PANTHER (RRID:SCR_004869) revealed 14 enriched pathways
related to immune activation (**Fig.5a**), including both PD-1 and
chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways were enriched
within the under-expressed genes. Consistently, in DEG analysis of
cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (**Fig.4b**), we found that MHCII
positivity was associated with upregulation of the tumor-suppressors
*RARRES3* and *CLU* associated with immune infiltration
[(17--19)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?iw8uhD) (**S.Fig.5c**).

Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort.
For this, we utilized expression data of 335 genes profiled with the
Nanostring technology
[(20)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?P6xWPt). Consistently, a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of
chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors
(**S.Fig.5d, S.Table10**). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11
chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with IFN-γ activation
[(21--23)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jDdLG3), were among the
top overexpressed genes as in TCGA, suggesting that IFN activation plays
a key role in MHCII-associated immune activation, as previously
suggested also in other cancers
[(24--26)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5AXFHa).

To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ
signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously
available spatial transcriptomic dataset

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0024
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
in other cancers
[(24--26)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5AXFHa).

To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ
signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously
available spatial transcriptomic dataset
[(27)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?o8Pw8e) incorporating expert
pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses
(**Fig.5b**, **S.Fig.5e**). The annotated TME areas exhibited distinct
transcriptomic profiles visualized in differential clusters in a UMAP
projection (**S.Fig.5f**). Using MHCII and IFN-γ signatures (see
Methods), we found that the MHCII expression was significantly
correlated with IFN-γ signature expression across distinct tumor areas,
including cancer and TSI regions (**Fig.5c**). Moreover, a higher MHCII
signature and increased IFN-γ in both cancer and TSI spatial regions
predicted improved clinical responses to chemotherapy at spot level
(**S.Fig.5g**,**h**), and similar trends were observed at the patient
level, although statistical significance was not reached due to the
smaller sample size (**S.Fig.5i**,**j**). Interestingly, in our t-CycIF
spatial data (multiomics cohort), statistical analysis revealed
significantly higher expression of phospho-STAT1 (pSTAT1), indicative of
IFN activation in CD8+ and CD4+ T cells and antigen-presenting (CD11c+)
myeloid cells neighboring MHCII+ cancer cells at the TSI (**Fig.5d**),
confirmed via visual inspection (**Fig.5e, S. Fig.5k**). Moreover, CD8+
T-cells neighboring MHCII+ cancer cells display a higher expression of
PD-1 in indicating increased tumor reactivity independent of MHCI
(**Fig.5f**). These findings suggest that cancer cell MHCII associates
with spatially coordinated activation of antigen presentation and T

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0025
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
cancer cells display a higher expression of
PD-1 in indicating increased tumor reactivity independent of MHCI
(**Fig.5f**). These findings suggest that cancer cell MHCII associates
with spatially coordinated activation of antigen presentation and T
cells via IFN signaling at the TSI. Further, using the scRNA-seq
dataset, we observed that the MHCII expression in cancer cells
correlated with *IFNG* gene expression specifically in CD4+ memory
helper and CD8+ central memory (CM) T cells, which are related to
activation after antigen exposure (**Fig.5g**). Overall, these findings
reinforce the link between cancer-intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation
and highlight the role of cancer cell MHCII in spatial immune
activation, also independent of MHCI.

To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in
MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict
ligand--receptor pairs (**Fig.5h, Ext. material - Fig.5**). We
identified the top seven ligand--receptor interactions across CD8+ T
cells, CD4+ T cells, Tregs, and DCs (**Fig.5i**). Interestingly, IFN
signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in
CD8+ and CD4+ T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and
APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions
across the cell populations, which may indicate the induction of immune
tolerance [(28)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dKHGmS). Similarly,
in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (**Fig.5j**),
we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII
ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL,
IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (**Fig.5k**). In addition, VEGFA, one of

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0026
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
mics dataset from the TSI (**Fig.5j**),
we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII
ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL,
IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (**Fig.5k**). In addition, VEGFA, one of
the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative
correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell
interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at
the TSI (**Fig.5k**). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells
elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the
surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with
activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.

### **Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity** 

Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when
antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous
misfolded proteins [(29)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?p6Fnkj).
To explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with
specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified
antigens detected via HLA ligandome analysis in HGSC
[(30)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?HGxoFz) (**Fig.6a**). We
found that the deconvoluted expression of MHCII in cancer cells is
indeed associated with the expression of most antigen-encoding genes in
the TCGA dataset (**Fig.6b**). However, in our scRNA-seq dataset, only
four specific antigen genes (UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN) were expressed
in the cancer cells, and associated with tumor MHCII expression
(**Fig.6c**). In our GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also
observed positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0027
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
es (UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN) were expressed
in the cancer cells, and associated with tumor MHCII expression
(**Fig.6c**). In our GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also
observed positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment
with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent
stroma at the TSI (**Fig.6d**).

To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in
HGSC, we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides
alongside bulkRNAseq and additional deconvolution and TCR repertoire
analysis (**Fig.6e**). The predicted immunopeptides were consistently
observed across the patients (**Fig.6f**). Further examination revealed
that genes enriched in samples with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells were significantly associated with cellular components such as
junctions, adhesion molecules, the extracellular matrix, and cell--cell
contact zones. These genes were also enriched in biological processes
related to protein localization and regulation (**S.Fig.6a,b**). To
exclude peptides that reflect normal-tissue antigen presentation, we
filtered these based on their presence in the ligandome of normal
ovarian tissues matching the gyneco-anatomical location and antigen
environment [(31)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?2VymrH),
identifying 35 malignancy-associated peptides. Next, we assessed the
expression of these peptides (projected to corresponding genes) in the
deconvoluted tumor signal from the bulk RNA-seq data (see Methods). We
found that most of these were specifically expressed in MHCII-high
tumors (**Fig.6g**) and showed high specificity towards the tumor
compartment (**Fig.6h**). Projection of both MHCII and tumor specificity

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0028
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
rom the bulk RNA-seq data (see Methods). We
found that most of these were specifically expressed in MHCII-high
tumors (**Fig.6g**) and showed high specificity towards the tumor
compartment (**Fig.6h**). Projection of both MHCII and tumor specificity
(**Fig.6i**) revealed a tumor-associated landscape of peptides presented
on MHCII complexes. Interestingly, many of these peptides were linked to
cytoskeleton and adhesion (*YAP, SNGC,* *TNS1, PKP2*). Finally, to
investigate the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen
presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We
observed a significant increase in the number of TCR clones --
specifically TRAD and TRB -- within samples showing a high proportion of
MHCII+ cancer cells (**Fig.6j**).

### **Tumor MHCII associates with responses to anti-PD1 and is abrogated upon MHCII blockade *ex vivo***

We functionally investigated the role of tumor MHCII in modulating
T-cell responses to immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) using our
established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional
immune-competent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs)
[(32)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gx2upR). Specifically, we
analyzed the effects of PD-1 and TIGIT inhibition, as well as the impact
of MHCII blockade on PD-1 inhibition-induced immune cell phenotypes and
cytokine secretion across 13 iPDCs (**Fig.7a, S.Fig.7a,b**). Notably,
MHCII-high tumors exhibited significantly enhanced CD8+ T-cell
cytotoxicity in response to pembrolizumab (**Fig.7b, S.Table11)**. A
sample-by-sample analysis revealed a statistically significant increase
in cytotoxicity (p \< 0.05) between baseline and pembrolizumab-treated
conditions within the MHCII-high group **(Fig.7c**), confirming that

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0029
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
brolizumab (**Fig.7b, S.Table11)**. A
sample-by-sample analysis revealed a statistically significant increase
in cytotoxicity (p \< 0.05) between baseline and pembrolizumab-treated
conditions within the MHCII-high group **(Fig.7c**), confirming that
MHCII expression contributes to the response to PD-1 blockade. To
further assess whether MHCII is essential for this response, we utilized
a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII. Notably, MHCII blockade abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**Fig.7d**) and Granzyme B
(**Fig.7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,
multiplexed cytokine profiling of conditioned media from the iPDCs
confirmed the loss of extracellular cytokine production to the
supernatant after MHCII blockade (**Fig.7f**). These functional assays
demonstrate the critical role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and
local immune responses, supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell
activation on MHCII.

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen
presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of
distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to
T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore
contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context
of PD-1 ICB.

## **DISCUSSION**

We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem
using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280
real-world clinical samples. Using our machine learning framework
CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered
MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of
\~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0030
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
ing our machine learning framework
CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered
MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of
\~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+
cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods
generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated
immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer
cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells.
Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer
cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune
surveillance [(33,34)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?xJGUyt), and
the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells was
reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer
[(35)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zvb4CI). While IFN expression
in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent
evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
[(36)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7U5fHN). Through a robust
spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially
cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and
the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII
correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory
T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present
antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that
MHCII and *IFN-γ* expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated
that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of
neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at
the TSI.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0031
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
E. Furthermore, we identified that
MHCII and *IFN-γ* expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated
that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of
neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at
the TSI. These findings, summarized in **Fig.7g**, support the
hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and
CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at
the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC (**Fig.7h**).

MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under
evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in
antigen presentation [(37)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gyJgQT).
The presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved
response to ICBs in various tumor types
[(25,35,38,39)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?tvb3GL). However,
clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected
patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated disappointing
results [(40)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?o4hcvc). We showed
that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides linked to
cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common subjects to
post translational modifications
[(41)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?puVazg) with increased
immunopeptide potential
[(33)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?niDHpJ). The proximity to
MHCII tumor cells associated with CD8+T cell tumor reactivity,
independent of MHCI, and higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of
MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. Our *ex vivo* analysis of immunotherapy
responses in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on
MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0032
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
CR clonality, supporting the role of
MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. Our *ex vivo* analysis of immunotherapy
responses in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on
MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC
by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in
MHCII-high tumor samples. Importantly, we show that MHCII positive
tumors respond to PD-1 inhibition which is functionally dependent on
MHCII. This mechanistic interplay is illustrated in **Fig 7h.**
Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints, such as
TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII
expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical
exploration of ICBs beyond PD-1/PD-L1
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?3kkZkD). In conclusion, our
single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with
immune surveillance across the clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC,
providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient
stratification.

**Study limitations**

We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the
opportunities for future refinement and exploration. The retrospective
design and reliance on archival tissues on a TMA may introduce biases
related to tissue and patient heterogeneity, limiting some of the
statistical comparisons, and limiting conclusions on larger tissue
structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our
real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to
comorbidities or extent of disease
[(42)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KFRWH4). Our protein marker
panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types.
While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0033
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
due to
comorbidities or extent of disease
[(42)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KFRWH4). Our protein marker
panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types.
While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent
datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution
methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and
transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating
robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve
predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable
hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger
prospective patient cohorts.

## **MATERIALS & METHODS**

### ***Cohort description***

We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV
tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek
Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and
Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and
December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive
surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval
debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based
chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or
PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0038
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
t chemotherapy followed by interval
debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based
chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or
PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from
the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained
from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).

Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking
surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by
three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were
extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE
blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with
one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from
the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
described [(43)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?EFqcuS) and used
for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected
with t-CycIF.

### ***Whole slide cohort description***

We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the
ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). All patients received
neoadjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, followed by
interval debulking surgeries (IDS), and none of them received PARP
inhibitors. The samples were obtained from the IDS. The selection
criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status according to
ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138)
[(44)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1asBP0) and absence of

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0039
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
them received PARP
inhibitors. The samples were obtained from the IDS. The selection
criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status according to
ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138)
[(44)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1asBP0) and absence of
*BRCA1* or *BRCA2* germline or somatic mutation. In addition, a tumor
purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival
(PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R
\> 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that
underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova
clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to
define long-PFS (\>=12 months) and short-PFS (\<12 months) in the
survival analysis.

For genomic alteration and number of HRD scar calculations, DNA was
extracted from FFPE blocks and profiled using the Illumina SNP-array
GSAv3. Allelic imbalances were detected using GenomeStudio
(RRID:SCR_010973) and ASCATv3 (RRID:SCR_016868). The fraction of the
genome altered was defined as the number of base pairs in segments
larger than 1 kb with both scaled major and minor copy numbers different
from 1, divided by the total length of the human genome. The tumor
mutational burden was adopted from the iMTB reports (see iMTB report
subsection).

### ***Molecular profile assignment***

For the TMA cohort, we first identified *BRCA1/2* mutation and *BRCA1*
promoter methylation status of each sample as previously described
[(43)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6q4x6b). In brief, *BRCA1/2*
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the
Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a
*BRCA1/2* germline mutation, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0040

vector_chunks.text:
otero.org/google-docs/?6q4x6b). In brief, *BRCA1/2*
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the
Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a
*BRCA1/2* germline mutation, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and
Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent
probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation.
Details of the number of tumors with *BRCA1* or *BRCA2* mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter methylation are reported in **S. Table 1**.

Next, tumors without a *BRCA1/2* mutation or *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using
Illumina HiSeq2500 at 0.5X to determine a *BRCA*-like CNV profile
[(45)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kjFEn9) and
*CCNE1*-amplification status as described previously
[(43)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yPimxb). In addition, using
the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software
shallowHRD (RRID:SCR_027714). Finally, we categorized the tumors as
*BRCAloss*, HRD, HRP, and *CCNE1*amp using a hierarchical approach
described in **S. Fig. 1a**. Samples that failed QC for *BRCA1/2*
targeted DNA-sequencing, *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV
analysis, were labeled as "Unassigned" (or Other). In addition, samples
with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the *BRCA-like* CNV
profile [(45)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7upU1l) were labeled
as "Unassigned".

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar

addition, samples
with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the *BRCA-like* CNV
profile [(45)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7upU1l) were labeled
as "Unassigned".

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
(RRID:SCR_027138) [(44)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?C0E3Rf).
For all analyses, only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological
grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
included. For the whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically
validated HRD classification performed at the Helsinki University
Hospital, Finland, which uses ovaHRDscar
[(44)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JEr6nL), and the detection of
somatic and germline mutations and gene deletions in *BRCA1/2* genes.

### ***Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing***

To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor
tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the
whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and
the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots.
The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel
(**S.Table2**) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following
the t-CycIF protocol [(46)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ocOLEU).
The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the
main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images' illumination
was corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371). The per-image
tiles were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266,
[[https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar]{.underline}](https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar)).
Individual TMA's cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0042
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
1). The per-image
tiles were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266,
[[https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar]{.underline}](https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar)).
Individual TMA's cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox
on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts,
United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
(RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 and CellProfiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 using
2-pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and
morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter,
and Roundness were quantified using MATLAB (RRID:SCR_001622). Quality
filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity
across cycles
([[https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite]{.underline}](https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite)),
image regions with artifacts, and TMA's cores (n=64) with less than 1000
high-quality cells.

For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections
were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex
validated antibody panel (**S.Table12**) and scanned with a RareCyte
CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol. The image tiles were
stitched with ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266). For WSI, we developed a more
scalable analysis pipeline
([[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)).
Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei
segmentation in StarDist (RRID:SCR_027715), implementing the pre-trained
model '*2D_versatile_flu*o\'\'. Then, the mean intensity of each channel
was quantified by our in-house script based on scikit-image package
(RRID:SCR_021142).

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were
clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers
(Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the
R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and
cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the
0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel\'s signal
was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by
marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized
map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM
node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S. Fig. 1c**) by
using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md::chunk_0044
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
vector_chunks.text:
was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using
unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367)
[(47)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?OgzEse). Outliers were
removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity,
normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5
[(48)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?38spBh), and scaled by
Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S.Table13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are
two images, which were manually gated with scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) due
to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our
annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by
expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)
staining, showing agreement across samples (**S.Fig.4m**). The MHCII+
cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the
annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### ***Cancer cell phenotype clustering***

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.md
