To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in
MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict
ligand--receptor pairs(**Fig.5h, Ext.material Fig.5**). We identified
the top seven ligand--receptor interactions across CD8+T cells, CD4+T
cells, Tregs, and DCs(**Fig.5i**). Interestingly, IFN signaling and
MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8+ and
CD4+T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated
lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions across the
cell populations, which may indicate the induction of immune
tolerance[(28)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dKHGmS). Similarly,
in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI(**Fig.5j**),
we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII
ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL,
IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands(**Fig.5k**). In addition, VEGFA, one of
the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative
correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell
interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at
the TSI(**Fig.5k**). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells elicit
distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the
surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with
activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.

### **Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity** 

Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when
antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous
misfolded proteins[(29)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?p6Fnkj). To

tation, increased TCR diversity** 

Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when
antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous
misfolded proteins[(29)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?p6Fnkj). To
explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with specific
antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified antigens
detected via HLA ligandome
analysis[(30)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?HGxoFz) in
HGSC(**Fig.6a**). We found that the deconvoluted expression of MHCII in
cancer cells is indeed associated with the expression of most
antigen-encoding genes in the TCGA dataset(**Fig.6b**). However, in our
scRNA-seq dataset, only four specific antigen genes(*UBC, RARRES1,
ADRG2, MSLN*) were expressed in the cancer cells, and associated with
tumor MHCII expression(**Fig.6c**). In our GeoMx spatial transcriptomics
dataset, we also observed positive associations of MHCII expression in
the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes
in the adjacent stroma at the TSI(**Fig.6d**).

To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in
HGSC, we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides
alongside bulkRNAseq deconvolution and TCR repertoire
analysis(**Fig.6e**). Distinct immunopeptides were heterogeneously
presented across the patients(**Fig.6f**), with MHCII enriched antigens
enriched in biological processes related to protein localization and
regulation(**S.Fig.6a,b**). To exclude antigens that reflect
normal-tissue antigen presentation, we filtered these based on their
presence in the ligandome of normal ovarian tissues matching the
gyneco-anatomical location and antigen
environment[(31)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?2VymrH), and

t reflect
normal-tissue antigen presentation, we filtered these based on their
presence in the ligandome of normal ovarian tissues matching the
gyneco-anatomical location and antigen
environment[(31)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?2VymrH), and
identified 35 malignancy-associated antigens. Next, we assessed the
expression of these antigens in the deconvoluted tumor signal from the
bulk RNA-seq data (see Methods). We found that most of these were
specifically expressed in MHCII-high tumors(**Fig.6g**) and showed high
specificity towards the tumor compartment(**Fig.6h**). Projection of
both MHCII and tumor specificity(**Fig.6i**) revealed a tumor-associated
landscape of antigens presented on MHCII complexes(**S.Table11**).
Interestingly, many of these genes were linked to cytoskeleton and
adhesion (*YAP, SNGC,* *TNS1, PKP2*). Finally, to investigate the
immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen presentation, we
assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We observed a
significant increase in the number of TCR clones within samples showing
a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells(**Fig.6j**).

### **Tumor MHCII associates with responses to anti-PD1 and is abrogated upon MHCII blockade *ex vivo***

We functionally investigated the role of tumor MHCII in modulating
T-cell responses to immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) using our
established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional
immune-competent patient-derived cultures
(iPDCs)[(32)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gx2upR). Specifically,
we analyzed the effects of PD-1 and TIGIT inhibition, as well as the
impact of MHCII blockade on PD-1 inhibition-induced immune cell
phenotypes and cytokine secretion across 13
iPDCs(**Fig.7a,S.Fig.7a,b**). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited

ally,
we analyzed the effects of PD-1 and TIGIT inhibition, as well as the
impact of MHCII blockade on PD-1 inhibition-induced immune cell
phenotypes and cytokine secretion across 13
iPDCs(**Fig.7a,S.Fig.7a,b**). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited
significantly enhanced CD8+ T-cell cytotoxicity in response to
pembrolizumab(**Fig.7b,S.Table12)**. A sample-by-sample analysis
revealed a statistically significant increase in cytotoxicity (p \<
0.05) between baseline and pembrolizumab-treated conditions within the
MHCII-high group**(Fig.7c**), confirming that MHCII expression
contributes to the response to PD-1 blockade. To further assess whether
MHCII is essential for this response, we utilized a blocking antibody to
inhibit MHCII. Notably, MHCII blockade abolished PD-1-induced
intracellular IFN-γ(**Fig.7d,S.Fig.7c**) and Granzyme
B(**Fig.7e,S.Fig.7d**) expression in multiple T cell subsets.
Additionally, multiplexed cytokine profiling of conditioned media from
the iPDCs confirmed the loss of extracellular cytokine production to the
supernatant after MHCII blockade(**Fig.7f**). These functional assays
demonstrate the critical role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and
local immune responses, supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell
activation on MHCII.

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen
presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of
distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to
T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore
contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context
of PD-1 ICB.

## **DISCUSSION**

We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem

ributing to
T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore
contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context
of PD-1 ICB.

## **DISCUSSION**

We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem
using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280
real-world clinical samples. Using our machine learning framework
CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered
MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of
\~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+
cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods
generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated
immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer
cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells.
Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer
cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune
surveillance[(33,34)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?xJGUyt), and
the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+T cells was
reported recently in triple-negative breast
cancer[(35)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zvb4CI). While IFN
expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune
surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune
escape mechanisms[(36)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7U5fHN).
Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells
preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell
neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the
cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced

nalysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells
preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell
neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the
cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced
CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can
actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we
identified that MHCII and *IFN-γ* expression are spatially correlated,
and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN
activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+T, and antigen-presenting
myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in **Fig.7g**,
support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated
CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune
activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC.

MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under
evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in
antigen presentation [(37)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?gyJgQT).
The presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved
response to ICBs in various tumor
types[(25,35,38,39)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?tvb3GL).
However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in
unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated
disappointing results[(40)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?o4hcvc).
We showed that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides
linked to cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common
subjects to post translational
modifications[(31)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?puVazg) with
increased immunopeptide
potential[(41)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?niDHpJ). The

cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common
subjects to post translational
modifications[(31)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?puVazg) with
increased immunopeptide
potential[(41)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?niDHpJ). The
proximity to MHCII tumor cells associated with CD8+T cell tumor
reactivity, independent of MHCI, and higher TCR clonality, supporting
the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. Our *ex vivo* analysis of
immunotherapy responses in iPDCs suggests that stratification of
patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of
immunotherapies in HGSC by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of
CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. Importantly, we show that
MHCII positive tumors respond to PD-1 inhibition which is functionally
dependent on MHCII. This mechanistic interplay is illustrated in **Fig
7h.** Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints,
such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high
MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical
exploration of ICBs beyond
PD-1/PD-L1[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?3kkZkD). In
conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms
associated with immune surveillance across the clinicomolecular subtypes
of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and
patient stratification.

**Study limitations**

We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the
opportunities for future refinement and exploration. Regardless of the
validation in external datasets including whole slide images, the
multiomics cohort on a TMA limits the conclusions on larger tissue
structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our

ture refinement and exploration. Regardless of the
validation in external datasets including whole slide images, the
multiomics cohort on a TMA limits the conclusions on larger tissue
structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our
real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to
comorbidities or extent of
disease[(42)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KFRWH4). Our protein
marker panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell
types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent
datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution
methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and
transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating
robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve
predictive performance. Lastly, our conclusions encourage future studies
in prospective patient cohorts.

## **MATERIALS & METHODS**

### ***Cohort description***

We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV
tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek
Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and
Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and
December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive
surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval
debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based
chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or
PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from
the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained
from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).

ce therapy. Clinical data was extracted from
the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained
from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).

Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking
surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by
three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were
extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE
blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with
one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from
the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four
cores per patient's sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done
on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as
described [(43)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?EFqcuS) and used
for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected
with t-CycIF.

### ***Whole slide cohort description***

We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the
ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). All patients received
neoadjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, followed by
interval debulking surgeries (IDS), and none of them received PARP
inhibitors. The samples were obtained from the IDS. The selection
criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status according to
ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138)
[(44)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1asBP0) and absence of
*BRCA1* or *BRCA2* germline or somatic mutation. In addition, a tumor
purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival
(PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R
\> 0cm) after surgery.

d absence of
*BRCA1* or *BRCA2* germline or somatic mutation. In addition, a tumor
purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival
(PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R
\> 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that
underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova
clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to
define long-PFS (\>=12 months) and short-PFS (\<12 months) in the
survival analysis.

For genomic alteration and number of HRD scar calculations, DNA was
extracted from FFPE blocks and profiled using the Illumina SNP-array
GSAv3. Allelic imbalances were detected using GenomeStudio
(RRID:SCR_010973) and ASCATv3 (RRID:SCR_016868). The fraction of the
genome altered was defined as the number of base pairs in segments
larger than 1 kb with both scaled major and minor copy numbers different
from 1, divided by the total length of the human genome. The tumor
mutational burden was adopted from the iMTB reports (see iMTB report
subsection).

For the TMA cohort, we first identified *BRCA1/2* mutation and *BRCA1*

[(43)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6q4x6b). In brief, *BRCA1/2*
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the
Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a
*BRCA1/2* germline mutation, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from
FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and

on, both *BRCA1/2* somatic mutations and

FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA
MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and
Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent
probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation.
Details of the number of tumors with *BRCA1* or *BRCA2* mutations and
*BRCA1* promoter methylation are reported in **S. Table 1**.

Next, tumors without a *BRCA1/2* mutation or *BRCA1* promoter
hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using
Illumina HiSeq2500 at 0.5X to determine a *BRCA*-like CNV profile
[(45)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kjFEn9) and
*CCNE1*-amplification status as described previously
[(43)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yPimxb). In addition, using
the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software
shallowHRD (RRID:SCR_027714). Finally, we categorized the tumors as
*BRCAloss*, HRD, HRP, and *CCNE1*amp using a hierarchical approach
described in **S.Fig.1a**. Samples that failed QC for *BRCA1/2* targeted
DNA-sequencing, *BRCA1* promoter hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV
analysis, were labeled as "Unassigned" (or Other). In addition, samples
with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the *BRCA-like* CNV
profile [(45)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7upU1l) were labeled
as "Unassigned".

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar

For all analyses, only tumors with a *TP53* mutation, histological
grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
included.

### ***Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing***

To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor
tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the
whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and
the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots.
The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel
(**S.Table2**) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following
the t-CycIF protocol [(46)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ocOLEU).
The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the
main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images' illumination
was corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371). The per-image
tiles were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266,
[[https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar]{.underline}](https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar)).
Individual TMA's cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox
on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts,
United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
(RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 and CellProfiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 using

e cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox
on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts,
United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
(RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 and CellProfiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 using
2-pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and
morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter,
and Roundness were quantified using MATLAB (RRID:SCR_001622). Quality
filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity
across cycles
([[https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite]{.underline}](https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite)),
image regions with artifacts, and TMA's cores (n=64) with less than 1000
high-quality cells.

For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections
were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex
validated antibody panel (**S.Table13**) and scanned with a RareCyte
CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol. The image tiles were
stitched with ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266). For WSI, we developed a more
scalable analysis pipeline
([[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)).
Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei
segmentation in StarDist (RRID:SCR_027715), implementing the pre-trained
model '*2D_versatile_flu*o\'\'. Then, the mean intensity of each channel
was quantified by our in-house script based on scikit-image package
(RRID:SCR_021142). Image quality control was performed by CyLinter
(RRID:SCR_021157).

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were

RRID:SCR_021142). Image quality control was performed by CyLinter

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were
clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers
(Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the
R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and
cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the
0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel\'s signal
was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by
marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized
map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM
node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S.Fig.1c**) by using
the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S.Fig.1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using

inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using
unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367)
[(47)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?OgzEse). Outliers were
removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity,
normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5
[(48)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?38spBh), and scaled by
Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S.Table13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are
two images, which were manually gated with scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) due
to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our
annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by
expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)
staining, showing agreement across samples (**S.Fig.4m**). The MHCII+
cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the
annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### ***Cancer cell phenotype clustering***

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed
outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method
section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based
on the expression levels of the markers: \"Ki67\", \"Vimentin\",

marker signals by the TMA slide and performed
outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method
section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based
on the expression levels of the markers: \"Ki67\", \"Vimentin\",
\"pSTAT1\", \"MHC-I\", \"ECadherin\", \"yH2AX\", \"pTBK1\", \"cPARP1\",
\"CK7\", \"PDL1_488\", and morphological features: \"Area\",
\"Eccentricity\", and \"Roundness\" into a 2500-node self-organized map
(SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were
clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954).
For this, the number of clusters (*k*) was assessed from 2 to 12. For
each value of *k* assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of
the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and
morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm
"partitioning around medoids" (pam) was used to cluster the selected
dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best
number of cancer meta-clusters, *k*, by assessing the resultant
consensus-clustering matrix for each value of *k* and its cumulative
density distribution (**Ext.material Fig. 3**). The cancer meta-clusters
were named according to the expression of relevant markers in each one
(**Ext.material Fig. 2**).

### ***Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis***

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality
control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius
of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751). We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood
information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by
using k-means and a k**=**40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function

ion tl.spatial_count of the package scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751). We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood
information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by
using k-means and a k**=**40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function
of the package scimap. Every cell was assigned to only one type of RCN.
To select the value of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the
Python package YellowBrick (RRID:SCR_027716) (**Ext.material Fig. 3**).
We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods of cells
that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis using a
radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the
robustness of our approach (**Ext.material Fig. 3**). The proportion of
each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of
cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.

### ***Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis***

To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity
of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial
transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different
responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data,
we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341). For the QC,

IDS HGSC samples with different
responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data,
we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341). For the QC,
we excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes
detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of
hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using
Seurat\'s SCTransform function.

Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an
expert pathologist as 'Cancer', 'TSI' (tumor-stroma interface with
partial tumor and stromal morphology), 'Immune-Stromal' and 'other'.
Spots annotated as 'other' have been excluded from the analysis. Within
'Immune-Stromal' spots, we have distinguished an additional type -
'TSI2', defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to
either 'Cancer' or 'TSI' spots. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109). The MHCII signature
comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed
MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the
median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to
chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor
response was adopted from Böhm et al, 2015
[(49)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?hhEDW9). An IFN-γ signature,
obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene
set collection (RRID:SCR_016863), was also quantified per spot.

### ***CEFIIRA: Machine learning supervised classification***

CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the
identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter
tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training
and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent
overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a
proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3)
Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen
data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to
minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features
using feature elimination and feature permutations to elucidate how
accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly
shuffled, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested
cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation.

The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had
the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker
expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); 2)
three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled
using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in
a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when
the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS

eccentricity and roundness), scaled
using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in
a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when
the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS
the model included patients' clinical information -- FIGO stage and age
at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological
feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In
addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or
Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine
learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was
employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into
the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.

To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm
for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the
PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695,
[[https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret]{.underline}](https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret)).
We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of:
BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected
Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance
(weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (**S.Table
15**). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to determine the
number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S.Table 16**).

AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S.Table 16**).

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python
scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) for binary classification of
cells into clinicomolecular groups
([https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA]{.underline}). The models\'
training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop
iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using
the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 30 data
partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model
robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve
similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based
on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (**S.Fig.3e**).
For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian
optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model
(*min_samples_split*, *min_samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of
hyperparameters' values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean
weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each
iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three
independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a
cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on
classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting
the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target

rsive feature elimination (RFECV) based on
classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting
the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target
variable through the computation of the mean decrease accuracy scores.
By randomly shuffling the values of each feature and observing the
resulting change in model performance, MDA scores quantify the
contribution of individual features to the model\'s accuracy. Finally,
we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set
partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell
level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard
deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported.
Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells,
Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified
the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of
overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below
the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

### ***RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment***

For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read
counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463) from the
Genomics Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was
also obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository. We stratified the
tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1
in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We
assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group
with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33)
expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with
less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used
the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and
differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change
\>=2, FDR \< 1\^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or
sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or
sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal PantherDB
v19.0 (RRID:SCR_004869), we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as
reference the Reactome database (RRID:SCR_003485).

For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=202), we
first stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups according to
the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells.

the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression
analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed
groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the
difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest
and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold
change \>=1 or Log2-fold change \<=1 and FDR\<1\^-3. Overexpressed genes
are reported in **S.Table10**.

### **GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis** 

We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors
obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qB1YRI), and is publicly
available at

transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors
obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qB1YRI), and is publicly
available at
[[(https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672)).
The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the
tumor--stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and
stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were
initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and
IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the
TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data
had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed
normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by
variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using
the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206).

Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic
deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene
expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
([[https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism]{.underline}](https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism)),
a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial
transcriptomics datasets (RRID:SCR_027499). This method not only
estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles
for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw
scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577,
along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we
downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final

rence for the deconvolution, we used raw
scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577,
along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we
downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final
dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in
resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were
grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T
cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B
cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90
endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late
erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient.
Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with
low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both
'cell types' and 'cell states'.

We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA)
scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific
deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess
the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we
correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from
BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y \~
exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating 'Patient' as a covariate (y
\~ exp(x) \| Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the
same tumor.

### ***Ligand-receptor analysis***

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing
them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq
analysis). MultiNicheNetR
([[https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git]{.underline}](https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high
and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the
analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of
five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were
required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion
of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates
were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log
fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized
ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions
enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These
candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics
dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We
correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor
deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma
segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma
segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

### ***Immunopeptidomics***

We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC
samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to
the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the
interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more
information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was
performed as previously described
[(50)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7Lf2IK) with minor
modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed
on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by
mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected
to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer
composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors
(Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich,
I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at
500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at
25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was
applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher),
pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of
anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.

esin covalently coupled to 1 mg of
anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.
The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the
following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM
NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were
dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges
(Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in
0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were
eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1%
TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The
eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using
the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
into a single sample.

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto
an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1
min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min.

over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),
with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs
cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired
with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the
minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at \< 700 m/z
or 3 Th \>800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000,
considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was
lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59
eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard
polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to
exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using
a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard
(ThermoFisher Scientific).

Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational
suite (RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.
MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC
peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications:
enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and 'null', allowing up to
two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and

MSFragger) was employed for MHC
peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications:
enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and 'null', allowing up to
two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and
30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da, and charge states from +1
to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a
variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches
and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher
(https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher), applying a false discovery
rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and
peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant
(https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run
normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with
default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1,
while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides
confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis.
Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting
from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286,
[https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant]{.underline}) with
default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For
the analysis, peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with
MHCII tumor antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial
transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did
not have mapped peptides in the dataset), and patients were stratified

es were mapped to significantly correlated with
MHCII tumor antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial
transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did
not have mapped peptides in the dataset), and patients were stratified
by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CycIF imaging into high
(patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest
of patients) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by
comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as
a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of
uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using Enrichr
(RRID:SCR_001575) (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component
2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms were recorded. These genes were also
filtered based on their presence in the ligandome of healthy ovary
tissues [(31)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LIHkNn). Resulting 35
genes' expression was studied in deconvoluted bulk RNAseq of the same 12
samples from iCAN study.

### ***Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation***

Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the
samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq. We especially
focus on the somatic mutation module (PCGR,
[[https://github.com/sigven/pcgr]{.underline}](https://github.com/sigven/pcgr)),
which contains tumor purity and ploidy estimation, variant
classification (somatic SNVs/InDels and copy number aberrations), and
tumor mutational burden (TMB) estimation. TMB quantifies a tumor\'s
total somatic mutations per megabase (Mb), and was computed with command
"*-estimate_tmb*", with other settings of "*tmb_dp_min*", "*tmb_af_min*"
and "*tmb_ad_min*" as default. Among the output of iMTB reports, tumor

ation. TMB quantifies a tumor\'s
total somatic mutations per megabase (Mb), and was computed with command
"*-estimate_tmb*", with other settings of "*tmb_dp_min*", "*tmb_af_min*"
and "*tmb_ad_min*" as default. Among the output of iMTB reports, tumor
purity was used to verify the result of PRISM deconvolution. TMB was
used to show the genomic alterations related to immune proportions.

### ***Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data***

RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for
immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with the
Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than
100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data
were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the iCAN
study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM
(RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale
factors, and cell-type--specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor,
stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for
PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell
RNA-seq dataset from [(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?s0IsHW),
which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29
patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the
tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After
decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate
an immune--stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and
each compartment (tumor and immune--stromal) were computed using the
PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such

l components were combined to generate
an immune--stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and
each compartment (tumor and immune--stromal) were computed using the
PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such
as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition
weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment
within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the
expression level expected for each cell type independent of its
abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted
for the 35 genes identified from immunopeptidomics. Patients were
stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched
t-CycIF imaging (as described in the Immunopeptidomics section). For
each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled
expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity
of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by
comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the
median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune--stromal
compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values
closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled
expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean
scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low
samples.

We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC
samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples
(total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study (as described in previous
section).

**TCR repertoire analysis***

We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC
samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples
(total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study (as described in previous
section). TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7,
14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted
using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725), following the RNA-seq workflow
([[https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/]{.underline}](https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/))
with default settings. We used 'mixcr analyze rnaseq', followed by
'mixcr exportQc align' and 'mixcr exportQc chainUsage' for quality
control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq
(non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully
aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones,
with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in
7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group).
Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly
represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal
diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R
package (RRID:SCR_023089). We employed the 'repExplore' function with
the \"volume\" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups
stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification
done in Immunopeptidomics): 1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone
counts for TRAD and TRB); 2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts
for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers
between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon
rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the
Holm method.

amples (12 clone counts
for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers
between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon
rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the
Holm method.

### ***iPDCs flow cytometry analysis***

To study how tumor MHCII affects the response to ICB, the dataset
generated in Launonen et al., 2024
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?DjZST4) was used. Briefly,
tumor tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort
was dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an
omentum-derived matrix
[(32)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RTw0XO). Cultures were
treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was
performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting
dataset was made publicly available at
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?F11Oj9). In this study, we
reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells (single cells and
aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on
baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45⁺)
and myeloid cells (CD11c⁺ and CD14⁺) (**S.Fig.6**). MHCII-high tumors
were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR
distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid
cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was quantified
as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells
relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between
MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was
assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed
using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and

treated controls and compared between
MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was
assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed
using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and
custom R scripts.

To reveal how the blockade of MHCII affects the response to ICB, frozen
tumor-derived samples from three patients with high HLA-DPB1 expression
were selected from the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective HGSC cohort based on RNA
expression in deconvoluted with PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) tumor fraction.
The tumor tissue had been enzymatically dissociated prior to
cryopreservation. For iPDC generation, thawed cells were embedded as
droplets in an omentum-derived extracellular matrix
[(32)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?bpgwyb). Cultures were
treated with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination
treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) (RRID:AB_395939) or isotype
control was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently
added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were
harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions.
Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3,
CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ)
markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and
doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating. Data were
acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT
voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in
FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a
consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in
FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and
subsequently analyzed in R.

eam analysis was performed in
FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a
consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in
FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and
subsequently analyzed in R. Statistical analyses were performed in
RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build 418, RRID:SCR_000432) using R (version
4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from FlowJo were processed
for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFN-γ, Granzyme B)
along with associated metadata, such as patient id and condition.
Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical comparisons
between conditions. For each patient, the median fluorescent intensity
for the IFN-γ, and Granzyme B was calculated across all cells within
each cell type and experimental condition. The resulting median
intensity values were then used to calculate the log2 fold change for
each patient, cell type, and marker. Three distinct log2 fold change
comparisons were performed: pembrolizumab vs. control, anti-HLA +
pembrolizumab vs. pembrolizumab, and anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs.
control. Finally, the mean of these individual patient log2 fold change
values was calculated for each comparison across all patients to
determine the pooled treatment effect.

Supernatants from the cultures used to assess the effect of MHC-II

Secreted cytokines were quantified using the LEGENDplex™ Human CD8/NK
Panel (13-plex) (BioLegend, catalog #741187, lot #B409342). The assay

Secreted cytokines were quantified using the LEGENDplex™ Human CD8/NK

Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa^TM^ cell analyzer (BD

### ***Survival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival
(RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by
calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total
cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more
than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, **S.Fig.1b**) were selected for
survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell
phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune
populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core
post-preprocessing (n=244, **S.Fig.1b**). Patient stratification based
on cell population used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above
median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split
into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66,
and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same
approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular
profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only

below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66,
and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same
approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular
profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only
high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.

### ***Statistical analyses***

We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3
(RRID:SCR_001905). The difference in abundance between the two groups
was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated
that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different
groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The
correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations.
For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and
Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were
considered statistically significant.

## 

## **FIGURE LEGENDS**

### **Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.**

**a** Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics
tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC
profiles: *BRCA*loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-*BRCA*wt),
homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification
(*CCNE1amp*), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).
High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract
single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline
(CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical
covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and
molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical
methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation,
both incorporating external cohorts. **b** Antibody panel employed for
t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. **c** A bar plot displaying the
composition of cancer cells, stromal cells, myeloid cells, lymphocytes,
and unassigned cells per tumor. The bar plot is sorted by cancer cell
abundance.

correspond to their main cell type group assignment. **e** Example of
t-CycIF image. **f** Cell type annotation of the image in panel d**.**
**g** Bar plot illustrating the composition of immune cells per tumor.
The bar plot is sorted according to the CD11c+ myeloid cell proportion.

### **Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC** 

**a** A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline 'CEFIIRA'.
Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example,
predicting if cancer cells belong to either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors.
Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target
(for example, *BRCA*loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation
iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their
corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the
remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to
predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to
either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are
calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested
cross-validations, the model's median accuracy and consensus critical
features are identified. **b-c** Heatmap displaying the median accuracy
(F1 score) of CEFIIRA models in predicting whether cells belong to
tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated
molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations (columns). The 95%

The entropy difference between HRD-like and HRP-like tumors was assessed using a permutation test (50 folds), and the resulting p-value is reported.
