We sincerely thank the reviewers for their encouraging and invaluable
comments. We have now addressed all the comments and believe that the
manuscript has been significantly strengthened. Below are the
point-by-point responses (in blue).

The main improvements in the revised manuscript:

1. Improved resolution of tumor-specific expression of MHCII - with
 > **Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data, additional spatial
 > proteomics representations, scRNAseq and imaging data analysis**
 > excluding the possibility of artefacts/spill over and
 > demonstrating true MHCII expression in the tumor cells.

2. Additional compelling evidence supporting the role of tumor MHCII in
 > HGSC taking into account **tumor genomic features, anatomical site
 > variation and associated immune cell signatures.**

3. Additional validation on the effect of tumor MHCII independently of
 > MHCI.

4. New analysis demonstrating the **technical robustness of CEFIIRA**,
 > excluding the effect of overall feature variability explaining the
 > model performance.

5. Improved malignant-compartment specific immunopeptidomic analysis
 > showing **MHCII antigens linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion**
 > providing the mechanistic background of MHCII expression on tumor
 > cells.

6. **New data from functional ex vivo experiments confirming the
 > mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation**. using a novel
 > platform of patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we
 > demonstrated that MHCII-high tumors exhibit enhanced CD8+ T-cell
 > cytotoxicity in response to PD-1 inhibition, and that MHCII
 > blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production.
 > These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency of T-cell
 > activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for optimizing

icity in response to PD-1 inhibition, and that MHCII
 > blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production.
 > These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency of T-cell
 > activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for optimizing
 > immunotherapy strategies and improving patient outcomes in cancer
 > treatment.

This work presents several key novelties and impactful findings. Unlike
prior studies, which have only associated immune hotspot activation with
prognosis, we establish a direct link between intra-tumoral immune
hotspots and MHCII expression in cancer cells with single-cell spatial
resolution. We show, for the first time, that tumor MHCII expression is
spatially connected to localized immune activation and that immune cells
adjacent to MHCII+ cancer cells are tumor-reactive and more activated.
Additionally, our research reveals the spatially confined effects of
tumor-stroma interface composition, independent of tumor molecular
features. Crucially, we provide mechanistic evidence that tumors with
high MHCII expression respond better to immune checkpoint blockade, with
this effect abrogated by MHCII blockade. These findings open new
therapeutic avenues, such as engineered immunopeptides or vaccines, and
highlight the importance of MHCII as a biomarker for more effective
patient stratification in HGSC. This work offers strong support for
future clinical validation in both retrospective and prospective trials.

**[Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):]{.underline}**

Anniina Farkkila and colleagues report a single-cell spatial analysis of
metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer from 280 patients, including
929 single-cell maps. The authors identified heterogeneous
immune-environments at the tumor-stroma interface that are predictive of

ues report a single-cell spatial analysis of
metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer from 280 patients, including
929 single-cell maps. The authors identified heterogeneous
immune-environments at the tumor-stroma interface that are predictive of
clinical outcomes. Among these predictive factors, the authors newly
identified tumor cell MHCII expression as a major predictor of superior
outcomes and validated their findings with independent datasets.

In addition to a technological tour of force and the use of an extensive
cohort of patients, the main strength of the study lies in the
identification of tumor-intrinsic IFNG-dependent MHCII expression as
marker of T cell-mediated immune control of malignant progression in
human ovarian cancer. This is novel and biologically interesting. In
addition, the finding that lymphocyte (T and B cells) infiltration loses
predictive value after chemotherapy opens new avenues to investigate the
immunobiology of human ovarian cancer, the basis of which have been
established for treatment-naïve tumors before clinical practices
changed. Increased frequencies of immunologically cold tumors after
chemotherapy suggest a deleterious effect of chemotherapy on existing
anti-tumor immune responses.

MAJOR COMMENTS

-The small fraction of patients responding to ICIs in ovarian cancer has
been associated with the degree of genomic alterations, but not BRCAness
or HDR (PMID: 32923884, 40749672). Since the authors have DNA seq data,
how are genomic alterations associated with the immune signatures?

We thank the reviewer for this interesting question. To address this we
quantified the following genomic features: fraction of genome altered
(FCA), Tumor mutational burden (TMB), and number of HRD associated scars

iated with the immune signatures?

We thank the reviewer for this interesting question. To address this we
quantified the following genomic features: fraction of genome altered
(FCA), Tumor mutational burden (TMB), and number of HRD associated scars
(RRID:SCR_027138, [(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?T8YiQb))
from our Whole Exome Sequencing and SNP-array data from our prospective
Oncosys-OVA trial samples. We associated these genomic features to the
immune infiltration patterns. Notably, we did not detect statistically
significant correlations in immune infiltration patterns related to the
above genomic alterations (**see new Ext. material - Fig.** **4**). In
our large multiomic cohort, the shallow whole-genome sequencing depth
was on average 0.5×, which provides a reliable estimate of HRD using
specialized bioinformatic tools
[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RyfLB6), however is not
sufficient to report mutations or CNVs or compute more detailed genomic
metrics, as previously reported
[(3)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9QZhQZ). We have now added
these new results into the manuscript (**new** **Ext. material - Fig.**
**4**), manuscript lines **428 - 432**.

-The authors identified conglomerates of B cells and T cells compatible
with tertiary Lymphoid Structures. How is the presence of TLS impacted
by neoadjuvant chemotherapy?

The reviewer points out a very interesting point. In our previous
analysis shown in **Figure 2k**, we compared the abundances of recurrent
cellular neighbourhoods (RCNs) between PDS and IDS samples. The
abundance of RCN13, associated with B cells and T cells, and consistent
with tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS; see **Supplementary Fig. 2h and
Fig. 2e**), did not differ significantly between PDS and IDS samples.

s) between PDS and IDS samples. The
abundance of RCN13, associated with B cells and T cells, and consistent
with tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS; see **Supplementary Fig. 2h and
Fig. 2e**), did not differ significantly between PDS and IDS samples.
However, it is important to note that the spatial profiling of TLSs in
our study is constrained by the size of the TMA cores analyzed, which
does not allow for reliable capturing of larger TME structures such as
TLSs, as also highlighted in a previous work
[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dTIM0C). We have now clarified
this limitation in the Discussion in "Study limitations" lines
**629-633**.

-Intrinsic expression of MHC-II in tumor cells opens several questions.
For instance, is there a predictive signature associated with CD4 T
cells specifically in tumors enriched in MHC-II+ tumor cells?
particularly in MHC-I-negative tumors?

We thank the reviewer for this insightful question. To address it, we
first examined our scRNA-seq dataset and compared CD4+ T cells from
MHCII-high versus MHCII-low tumors. This analysis did not, however,
reveal any differentially expressed genes between the groups (data not
shown).

Next, we evaluated whether the functional states of T-cells are affected
by MHCII positivity of tumor cells with and without concurrent MHCI
expression. Interestingly, we observed significant differences in both
CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell states in the proximity of MHCII+ cancer cells
compared to those neighboring MHCII- cancer cells (upper panel, **new
Fig. 5f**). Both CD8+ and CD4+T cells showed significantly higher PD-1
expression in close proximity to MHCII+ tumor cells. Importantly, after
filtering out MHCI+ tumor cells, CD8+T-cells showed significantly higher
PD-1 expression (lower panel, **new Fig.

f**). Both CD8+ and CD4+T cells showed significantly higher PD-1
expression in close proximity to MHCII+ tumor cells. Importantly, after
filtering out MHCI+ tumor cells, CD8+T-cells showed significantly higher
PD-1 expression (lower panel, **new Fig. 5f**) **indicating tumor
reactivity of CD8+T cells associated specifically with proximity to
MHCII+ tumor cells without MHCI+ expression.** These findings emphasize
the spatial relevance of tumor intrinsic MHCII expression and its
association with CD8+T-cell tumor reactivity specifically associated
with MHCII and irrespective of MHCI. These new results have now been
added to the manuscript (lines **490-494**).

-Also, increased frequencies of Th17 cells in MHC-II+ tumors, which show
better outcomes, demands more extensive analyses. How are Th17 responses
associated with outcomes in this cohort? While many studies have
identified Th17 T cell infiltration with tumor-promoting activity, other
authors found a protective role specifically in ovarian cancer (PMID:
19470694). This could be a distinctive feature of this disease.

Thank you for raising this point. In our scRNA-seq dataset, we
identified 91 Th17 cells. Although we observed abundance differences as
noted previously in Fig. 4e, the small number of cells limits the
statistical power of survival analysis. To further investigate this
question, we utilized scRNAseq-based deconvolution and explored the TCGA
cohort (n = 253 tumors). Using scRNAseq, we constructed a Th17 specific
signature UCell score within the immune compartment consisting of 22
genes (*IL17A, IL17F, IL21, IL22, IL26, RORC, STAT3, BATF, IRF4, IL23R,
IL6R, IL1R1, IL6ST, CCR6, CCL20, RORA, AHR, IKZF3, CTSH, GZMB, IL2RA,
CD4*). Samples were then stratified into two equal-sized groups (low and

mmune compartment consisting of 22
genes (*IL17A, IL17F, IL21, IL22, IL26, RORC, STAT3, BATF, IRF4, IL23R,
IL6R, IL1R1, IL6ST, CCR6, CCL20, RORA, AHR, IKZF3, CTSH, GZMB, IL2RA,
CD4*). Samples were then stratified into two equal-sized groups (low and
high) based on the Th17 signature. However, we did not observe a
significant association between Th17 score and patient survival. The
fact that tumor MHCII was predictive of survival in TCGA supports the
notion that tumor MHCII, and not the infiltration patterns of individual
immune populations, is the key feature predicting survival in HGSC.

-Also, trogocytosis often results in the incorporation of chunks of the
membrane of immune cells after long-term interactions (it could go in
both directions). It is unlikely that the frequencies of MHC-II+ tumor
cells identified by the authors are the result of this activity, but
expression of CIITA in tumor cells in association with MHC-II expression
at the protein level would further support intrinsic MHC-II
upregulation. Co-staining with a myeloid or B cell marker in the photos
presented in Figure 3 would also rule out co-localization of tumor cells
and MHC-II+ immune cells, if data are available.

We thank the reviewer for raising this question. To assess the
robustness of our MHCII+ cancer cell annotations, we first examined our
scRNA-seq dataset and evaluated the expression of immune markers
specifically within cancer cells (a **new** **Sup. Fig 4c)**. We did not
detect immune marker expression in any cancer cell cluster, including
those with high MHC-II expression. A small subset of cancer cells
expressing CIITA (60 cells) also lacked immune marker expression.

r cells (a **new** **Sup. Fig 4c)**. We did not
detect immune marker expression in any cancer cell cluster, including
those with high MHC-II expression. A small subset of cancer cells
expressing CIITA (60 cells) also lacked immune marker expression.
However, it is well established that transcription factor activity
quantification requires network level inference and typically does not
correlate with individual gene expressions
[(5)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?GuDIOl). Consistently, we did
not observe a correlation between CIITA and MHC-II expression at the
single-cell RNA-seq level. These results are now added also to the
manuscript in lines **391-393**.

To enable visual verification of tumor MHCII expression and its
distinction from immune cells, we have now also **updated** **Fig. 3f**
and included myeloid (CD11c) and B cell (CD20) markers in representative
MHC-II high tumor samples. In addition, we have now produced **Xenium
single-cell transcriptomics** from a high-MHCII tumor followed by
post-xenium tCycIF staining for tumor marker Pan-CK, and show that MHCII
transcripts are robustly expressed in the tumor cells (**new Fig. 3f**).
These are now added in the manuscript in lines **325-327**.

**[Reviewer #2 (Comments to the Author):]{.underline}**

cancer. This is an overall well written manuscript which relies heavily
on omics analysis. Main strengths include a large cohort of patients

ellular neighborhoods, cell types and
ultimately MHCII expression as prognostic in high grade serous ovarian
cancer. This is an overall well written manuscript which relies heavily
on omics analysis. Main strengths include a large cohort of patients
with important clinical variables and well annotated, use of multiple
omics approaches and some in vitro validation.

Major weaknesses include:

1\) Unclear what disease sites are being analyzed or how tissue site
(ovary, fallopian tube, omentum, etc) impact the down stream analysis.
This is a major concern given high levels of heterogeneity based on

We thank the reviewer for raising this important question. In response,
we have clarified the provided anatomical localization of all analyzed
samples in Supplementary Table 1. To address concerns regarding
potential confounding by diverse metastatic sites, we performed a
stratified sub-analysis focusing exclusively on samples collected from
primary tumor sites (ovary and fallopian tube). The performance of
CEFIIRA across 30 different splits of training and test sets from
primary sites only (**new S. Fig 3f**), for predicting long or short OS
in HRD samples, demonstrates:

i\) that the model's performance closely aligns with the mixed-site
dataset results in Supplementary Fig. 3f.

ii\) the top predictive features remain consistent, with MHCI, MHCII,
and E-cadherin emerging as the most informative.

**These findings indicate that our conclusions are robust and not
confounded by anatomical heterogeneity.** We have now added this
information into the Results section (lines **307 - 310**).

2\) While there is one in vitro study performed to validate the
differences in MHCII high vs low tumor cells, the vast majority of this

y anatomical heterogeneity.** We have now added this
information into the Results section (lines **307 - 310**).

2\) While there is one in vitro study performed to validate the
differences in MHCII high vs low tumor cells, the vast majority of this
work is in silico without mechanistic investigation. There is no
postulated mechanism by which MHCII is upregulated in certain tumor
cells nor why stromal-tumor cell interactions may dampen this.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. To address this,
we have now conducted additional in vitro experiments (**new Fig.7a**)
demonstrating that blocking MHCII abrogates T-cell activation and
cytokine production using our unique platform of patient-derived
immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) (**new Fig 7d-f**). These findings
suggest a mechanistic role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and
offer valuable insights for improving immunotherapy strategies to
optimize patient outcomes in HGSC.

Additionally, our new immunopeptidomic analysis demonstrates that MHCII
high tumors present antigens related to cytoskeletal and adhesion
proteins on their MHCII molecules. These proteins commonly generate
antigens likely via posttranslational modifications, providing a
mechanistic explanation to the MHCII expression in the tumor cells
**(new Figs 6e-i)**.

We have added these new data to the manuscript (lines **532-550,
553-571**) and the discussion (lines **611-613,** **619-620**). We also
display the mechanism supported by the data in our manuscript in a **new
Fig 7h**.

3\) The overall conclusions regarding hot-spots of immune activation
being prognostic in HGSOC is not novel nor are the findings that stromal
cells modulate the immune response.

We appreciate the reviewer's comment. We would like to clarify the main

\) The overall conclusions regarding hot-spots of immune activation
being prognostic in HGSOC is not novel nor are the findings that stromal
cells modulate the immune response.

We appreciate the reviewer's comment. We would like to clarify the main
novelties of our work. While prior studies have merely reported
associations between immune "hotspot" activation and prognosis in HGSC,
our work provides an important novel contribution by **linking
intra-tumoral immune hotspots to MHCII expression** **by cancer cells
with single-cell spatial resolution** (Fig. 4f, 4g, 4k). To our
knowledge, we are showing for the first time the spatially resolved
connection between cancer cell--intrinsic MHCII expression and localized
immune activation. We show that the tumor MHCII-adjacent immune cells
are tumor reactive and more activated. We also demonstrate spatially
confined effects of tumor-stroma interface composition **independent of
the tumor molecular features, which has not been shown before.**

Importantly, with our new experiments using our ex-vivo platform, **we
provide mechanistic evidence that these tumors are more responsive to
immune checkpoint blockade, and that this effect is abrogated upon MHCII
blockade.** This is an important observation, opening **new therapeutic
avenues for more effective patient stratification** for the existing
immune checkpoint therapies in HGSC, and **exploiting MHCII expression
using e.g. engineered immunopeptides.** Our work provides a solid
foundation for prospective patient stratification for immune checkpoint
blockade therapies.

4\) In the multivariate analysis in figure 3K, the strongest positive
prognostic factor is primary debulking which does not align with the
strong clinical data of non-inferiority for neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

r immune checkpoint
blockade therapies.

4\) In the multivariate analysis in figure 3K, the strongest positive
prognostic factor is primary debulking which does not align with the
strong clinical data of non-inferiority for neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
The authors should discuss this.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. We agree that in
several prospective, randomized trials
[(6)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5oOgER), and also recently in
the TRUST trial [(7)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?LGcEg7), no
consistent OS differences have been shown for PDS and IDS surgical
approaches. However in the real-world setting, patients referred to
neoadjuvant treatment and IDS tend to have shorter OS due to the same
comorbidities or extent of disease that lead to the clinical treatment
decision [(8)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yKmUOB). This has
also been observed in several large observational cohorts
[(8,9)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yZnDyJ). As our multiomics
cohort is a retrospective, real world dataset, our findings are in line
with these findings. We have added a short discussion about this to the
manuscript (lines **631-633**).

5\) It is unclear what definition of homologous recombination deficiency
is being used.

We apologise for this unclarity. We utilized two validated metrics for
defining HRD based on CNV profiles detected using shallow whole genome
sequencing [(2,10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JsfAg3). A
description of this method for HRD can be found in the Methods section
"Molecular profile assignment" and **Sup. Fig. 1a**.

**[Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ):]{.underline}**

**[Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ):]{.underline}**

In this manuscript, the authors employ multiplexed imaging along with
other datasets to profile a large number of HGSC samples. They identify
various immune populations and define cellular neighborhoods. Several
lymphocyte populations are found to be linked to better OS and PFS.
Using their newly developed CEFIIRA tool, they discover HLA-II
expression on tumor cells as a key feature associated with longer
patient survival. HLA/MHCII expression is localized to specific
neighborhoods and associates with IFN signalling. In addition, it comes
with distinct MHCII peptidomes, differences in TCR clonality and
response to checkpoint inhibition in an co-culture screen.

The manuscript is strong in its scale and the use of a wide variety of
datasets, 253 patients are profiled in depth. The presented findings are
consistent with each other and align with previous observations from
other tumor entities. Overall, the presented analyses are of high
quality and presented in an appropriate manner. Most of the claims are
well supported by the presented data, with suggestions for further
improvements outlined below. Overall though I find this to be a strong
manuscript that will be of interest to the HGSC and spatial biology
communities.

Specific comments

Tumor cell expression of MHCII emerges as a top feature predicting OS,
however it remains elusive how much of this prognostic value is
independent of other associated variables. The authors should report an
adjusted model that adjusts for age, stage, therapy and molecular
profile. Another way of showing this would be to run their CEFIIRA
analysis leaving out feature classes (e.g. MHCII expression) and

associated variables. The authors should report an
adjusted model that adjusts for age, stage, therapy and molecular
profile. Another way of showing this would be to run their CEFIIRA
analysis leaving out feature classes (e.g. MHCII expression) and
reporting loss of predictive performance.

We thank the reviewer for this comment. The adjusted Cox model for OS,
adjusting for age, stage, therapy and molecular profiles is shown in
**Fig 3k,** showing that tumor MHCII remains predictive with HR 0.76 (CI
0.62-0.93). To further clarify our approach, we confirm that CEFIIRA
uses an established, cross-validated recursive feature elimination
(RFECV) to **identify the most informative predictors while adjusting
for the clinicomolecular factors**. After feature selection, we compute
permutation-based mean decrease in accuracy (MDA) scores, which quantify
the contribution of each variable by measuring the drop in model
performance when that variable is randomly permuted. This approach
leverages out-of-bag samples to provide an unbiased estimate of variable
importance. While CEFIIRA is not designed to produce traditional
regression-based adjusted effect estimates (e.g., hazard ratios), the
combination of RFECV and permutation importance allows us to assess the
relative contribution of each feature **while accounting for
correlations among variables.** We have now further described our
feature selection and interpretation steps in the manuscript\'s Methods
section (lines **934-935, 982-985**).

In my opinion, the paper would benefit from a more in-depth analysis of
tumor cell specificity of MHCII expression. Cancer cells have been
reported to express HLAII previously and the authors present a lot of
supporting evidence, however a lot of this data is also consistent with

benefit from a more in-depth analysis of
tumor cell specificity of MHCII expression. Cancer cells have been
reported to express HLAII previously and the authors present a lot of
supporting evidence, however a lot of this data is also consistent with
the alternative explanation: bleed-over of MHCII signal from immune
cells into cancer cells. For example, if one was to assume such
spillover, one would expect the following: increase in signal in other
immune markers in cancer cells (this is actually the case), more MHCII
signal in highly infiltrated tumors (also the case) and close spatial
proximity of immune cells with MHCII higher cancer cells (also this is
reported here). I do not think that the presented deconvolution analysis
can clarify this issue as there are a lot of other potential sources of
inferred class-II expression from bulk data. scRNA data presented
supports the notion that cancer cells can express HLA-II but stops short
of showing that this explains the association with OS.

I would request a few additional QC metrics to rule out non-cancer cell
MHCII expression contributing here. For example, is there a correlation
between clearly immune related features (e.g. CD3 or myeloid markers)
and MHCII within cancer cells on the single cell level? There might also
be value in performing pixel-level analysis of MHCII signal with these
immune markers and potentially excluding immune-related pixels from
cancer cell masks.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. We are very
committed to ensuring that the cancer cell--specific signals we report
are not confounded by spillover from immune cells. To address this, we
have performed several new **analyses in our scRNAseq and spatial
proteomics data, and also produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics

he cancer cell--specific signals we report
are not confounded by spillover from immune cells. To address this, we
have performed several new **analyses in our scRNAseq and spatial
proteomics data, and also produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics
data.**

First, we quantified the degree of potential spillover in cancer cells
and assessed its correlation with MHC-II signal, as suggested. We did
not detect any correlation between CD3 or CD11b expression and MHC-II
levels in cancer cells, and we now include these results in a new
Supplementary Fig. 3g. Because high MHC-II expression could
theoretically arise from a combined spillover of CD3+ and CD11b+ immune
cells, we also tested the correlation between MHCII and the maximum
signal of these two markers. Again, no association was observed (see top
panel below).

In a complementary analysis, we examined the correlation between CD11b
signal in cancer cells and CD11b signal in neighboring immune cells
(within 20 μm) and found a strong correlation (bottom left panel),
confirming the sensitivity of our approach to detect spillover when it
occurs. In contrast, this level of correlation was not observed between
MHC-II in neighboring immune cells and cancer cells (bottom right
panel), indicating that cancer cell MHC-II expression is not solely
explained by spillover. Moreover, in sc-RNAseq, we demonstrated that
there are no expressions of immune related genes in cancer cells
expressing MHCII (new Supplementary Fig. 4c).

Finally, we have now produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data
from a high-MHCII tumor followed by post-xenium tCycIF staining, and
show that CD74, a transcript of the MHCII complex is robustly expressed
in the tumor cells (**new Figure 3f**).

nally, we have now produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data
from a high-MHCII tumor followed by post-xenium tCycIF staining, and
show that CD74, a transcript of the MHCII complex is robustly expressed
in the tumor cells (**new Figure 3f**).

Although robust pixel-level spillover removal methods are not yet
available and are impacted due to the close proximity of immune and
cancer cells, **our multi-modal analyses collectively support that a
substantial component of the MHC-II signal detected in cancer cells
reflects a true cancer-intrinsic expression.** We have now also added
these new results to the manuscript lines **321-323**).

We thank the reviewer for this crucial point. Unfortunately, for MHCII
specific immunopeptidomics, a substantially large amount of cells (1-3g
of tissue corresponding to 1-3x10^9^ (billion) cells) would be needed.
Upon processing of the last 15 samples in our prospective cohort (from
1x2cm tumor piece) we have yielded on average 13 x 10^6^ (million)
cancer cells thus making this approach unfeasible.

To mitigate this limitation, we have now implemented a **new analytical
pipeline to enrich malignancy-associated MHCII peptides.** First, we
filtered the 60 genes mapped from the immunopeptidomic dataset by
excluding those present in the "HLA-II ligandome" of healthy ovarian
tissue [(11)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?tusUQL). We
acknowledge that this is not exactly the same as the fallopian tube, but
it does represent the same immuno-anatomical region to assess which
peptides are potentially presented on MHCII by tissue-resident APCs in
the female pelvic region.

This approach left us with 35 candidate genes with **no detectable
presentation in healthy tissue on peptide level**. We then used PRISM
deconvolution of bulk RNA-seq from matching 12 samples in iCAN study to
quantify expression specifically in tumor vs. immune--stromal
compartments (new **Fig. 6e**). This allowed us to evaluate (i)
tumor-fraction-specific expression (new **Fig.6g**), (ii) enrichment in
samples with high MHCII+ cancer-cell proportions (defined by matched
t-CycIF imaging) (new **Fig.6h**), and (iii) fold changes relative to
MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples (new **Fig.6i**). **This analysis
revealed which malignancy-associated peptides are presented on MHCII
complexes.** Interestingly, many of these antigens were linked to
cytoskeleton and adhesion (*YAP, SNGC,* *TNS1, PKP2*), which are common
subjects to post translational modifications
[(12)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qJCONK) with increased
immunopeptide potential
[(13)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?r3hfM0). This has been added

Prediction models performed better in HRP-like tumors than in HRD-like
counterparts. Is this explained by technical or biological factors?

We thank the reviewer for pointing out this observation. We evaluated
both technical and biological explanations for the observed better
performance of prediction models in HRP-like tumors. CEFIIRA was built
to minimise technical bias through i) strict sample-wise data
partitioning (preventing leakage), ii) stratified splits balanced by
therapy and molecular subtype, iii) nested cross-validation with
parameter tuning, iv) independent validation on unseen data, and v)
repeated (30×) consensus estimation. These controls make it unlikely
that technical artifacts explain the performance difference.

We also examined whether intrinsic biological heterogeneity might
contribute to the performance gap in OS predictions. To assess this, we
quantified the variability of CEFIIRA input features within HRP-like and
HRD-like tumors by computing Shannon entropy and the Jensen--Shannon
(JS) divergence between the two groups (see graph of Shannon entropy and
Table with below). Using permutation testing (50 permutations), we did
not detect high differences in entropy or JS divergence for these
features, suggesting that **overall feature variability does not explain
the performance discrepancy supporting the robustness of our approach.**

These results strongly suggest that technical factors are unlikely to

tropy or JS divergence for these
features, suggesting that **overall feature variability does not explain
the performance discrepancy supporting the robustness of our approach.**

These results strongly suggest that technical factors are unlikely to
account for the observed differences in predictive performance. The most
compelling explanation points to intrinsic biological differences
between HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Remarkably, MHCII stood out as the
top feature, exhibiting the highest entropy difference between the HRD
and HRP groups, with significantly elevated values in HRD-like tumors.
**This underscores the substantial biological heterogeneity within
HRD-like tumors, further reinforcing the power of our integrative
approach in uncovering critical, clinically relevant features from our
multi-omics dataset.**

We have also added this result into the manuscript and Extended
materials (lines **335-336**, Ext. Materials Table 1).

height="4.083333333333333in"}

The authors should explain why they created separate CEFIIRA models for
tumor and immune cells? Can their approach handle multiple cell types at
once or is this information not retained when multiple cell types are
entered into the model? If the approach only works on one cell type at a
time, are there additional restrictions that have to be kept in mind
when performing multiple runs with potentially correlated features?

We thank the reviewer for this question. CEFIIRA can technically
accommodate correlated features and does so through RFECV-based feature
selection and permutation importance, both of which evaluate feature
contributions while accounting for multicollinearity. However, we chose

is question. CEFIIRA can technically
accommodate correlated features and does so through RFECV-based feature
selection and permutation importance, both of which evaluate feature
contributions while accounting for multicollinearity. However, we chose
to build separate models for tumor and immune cells primarily for
interpretability. When multiple cell types are combined into a single
feature table, CEFIIRA identifies the most predictive features across
the entire dataset without distinguishing whether a given feature
originates from tumor or immune compartments. This makes it difficult to
attribute the source of the predictive signal and to draw biologically
meaningful conclusions about cell-type--specific contributions. We have
now added this justification into the manuscript in lines **300-302**.

Lastly, the authors included a functional experiment involving iPDC
co-cultures to determine the effects of HLA-II expression on tumor cells
on immune cells. However, this functional link could be further
strengthened by blocking HLA-II to show that this has functional
consequences for immune cell behavior.

We thank the reviewer for this excellent suggestion. Using our ex-vivo
model [(14)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?lCHYa3), we now
utilized a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII to investigate the
dependency of T cell activation on MHCII signalling. Prior to the
experiment, we confirmed that the iPDCs contained minimal contributions
of myeloid cells on the day of readout (day 5, Figure below shown for
three iPDCs).

Notably, in our new data, MHCII blockade completely abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**new Fig. 7d**) and Granzyme B
(**new Fig. 7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,

(day 5, Figure below shown for
three iPDCs).

Notably, in our new data, MHCII blockade completely abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**new Fig. 7d**) and Granzyme B
(**new Fig. 7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,
multiplexed cytokine profiling of supernatant from the iPDCs confirmed
the loss of extracellular cytokine production after MHCII blockade
(**new Fig. 7f**). **These functional assays demonstrate the critical
role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and local immune responses,
supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII.**
This has been added also to manuscript lines **563-571**).

## **Abstract**

The tumor microenvironment in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC)
is a complex network of malignant--host cell interactions, yet its
orchestration remains poorly understood. We present a single-cell
spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating
high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Analyzing 929
single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with diverse cell
compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the
tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find
that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged
survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct

w that immune cell co-infiltration at the
tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find
that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged
survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct
spatial transcriptomic datasets, along with immunopeptidomic analysis,
confirms that MHCII expression correlates with immune activation,
antigen presentation, and TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived
immuno-oncology platform, we demonstrate that tumor MHCII expression
associates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade,
while blocking MHCII inhibits this activation. Our atlas offers new
insights into immune activation, potentially improving patient
stratification in HGSC.

## **Statement of significance**

This study delivers the first large-scale single-cell spatial atlas of
HGSC, revealing how tumor--immune organization shapes outcomes. We
identify tumor-intrinsic MHCII as a key driver of local immune
activation and immunotherapy responsiveness, providing a mechanistic
biomarker that can immediately inform improved patient stratification
and therapeutic decision-making in ovarian cancer.

**INTRODUCTION**

The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions[(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nfS5Pn).
The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding
microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes,
cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade
serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of
ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic
instability[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UliKHh) and

cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade
serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of
ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic
instability[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UliKHh) and
significant tumor
heterogeneity[(3)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1lvJx8). The
genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For
instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair
deficiency, such as those with *BRCA1/2* mutations, have been suggested
to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte
infiltration[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jcEirr) and better
responses to combinations with
immunotherapies[(5,6)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9vmQCT).
Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant
chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the
intricate interactions within the immune
compartment[(7--9)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yzi8KV),
resulting in spatially confined T-cell
exhaustion[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?EjZUnX). Therefore,
it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and
molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve
patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for
patients with HGSC.

We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1
million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with
comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging
DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a
tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the
single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that

multiomic molecular profiling leveraging
DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a
tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the
single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that
associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning
tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to
uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of
cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at
the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of
immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate
immune hotspots marked by increased activation, and tumor reactivity
impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles.
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition
ex-vivo. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune
cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the
way for targeted interventions in HGSC.

## **RESULTS**

### **Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors**

To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography,
clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic
profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing,
gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex
imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (**Fig.1a**). The
tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from
treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after
platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS
n=158).

vanced HGSCs (**Fig.1a**). The
tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from
treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after
platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS
n=158). Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes,
were collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67
months) (**Table S1**). Most patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and
paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free
survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). We conducted a hierarchical
multidimensional analysis to categorize the 174 tumors (**Fig.S1a**)
into four clinically and molecularly relevant groups (*BRCAloss, HRD,
Homologous Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1
amplification*)[(11)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dchEuz). The
rest (n=91) of the tumors were left "unassigned" as they could not be
reliably categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see
Methods).

To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used
hyperplexed imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)
targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing tissue
microarrays (TMAs, **Fig.1b, Table S2**). Image processing and quality
filtering produced 929 single-cell maps of HGSC from 253 patients
(**Fig.1a, Fig.S1b**). In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485
high-quality single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for each
tumor. The cell phenotyping was based on the multidimensional protein
expression (**Fig.S1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell
phenotypes **(Fig.1c,d)** with visual assessment (**Fig.1e,f**). The
annotated cell phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles

phenotyping was based on the multidimensional protein
expression (**Fig.S1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell
phenotypes **(Fig.1c,d)** with visual assessment (**Fig.1e,f**). The
annotated cell phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles
corresponding to their respective cell lineages (**Fig.S1d**). The deep
cell phenotype annotation comprised two non-immune stromal populations
(annotated stromal) constituting 40% of the cells (CD31+ endothelial
cells and Vimentin+, α-SMA+ fibroblasts) and 15 immune populations
corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells, memory and
regulatory T cells, and Myeloid lineages such as macrophages,
neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells (**Fig.1g,h**). The per-sample
abundance of CD8+ T and CD20+ B cells aligned with the expert
pathologists' annotations based on immunohistochemistry (IHC)
(**Fig.S1e,f**). The cells formed distinct visual clusters that aligned
with our cell type annotations (**Fig.1d,h**). Following cell
phenotyping, we quantified functional cell states, including Ki67
(**Fig.1i, Fig.S1g**) and PDL1 expression (**Fig.S1g**), some of which
have been associated with long-term survival in
HGSC[(12)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?o0yhBZ). Additionally, we
quantified the cell-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods (**Fig.1j**).

To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells, we
clustered the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell
phenotypic markers, mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell
morphology features. Using unsupervised consensus clustering (see
Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations,
exhibiting distinct morphological and molecular characteristics
(**Fig.1k-m**). The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high

g unsupervised consensus clustering (see
Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations,
exhibiting distinct morphological and molecular characteristics
(**Fig.1k-m**). The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high
expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the
Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell
area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle
cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers
(**Fig.1k,l; Fig.S2,3**).

### **Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles** 

We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the
defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all
molecular groups did not reveal significant differences after multiple
hypothesis correction (**Fig.S4a, Table S3**). However, consistent with
previous reports [(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kxTuxY), we
observed that *BRCA*loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and
CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (**Fig.2a**).
Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed
exclusively in *BRCA1*loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples
(**Fig.2a**). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found
that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and
macrophages were enriched in IDS samples (**Fig.S4b**). Interestingly,
all lymphocyte populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated
significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (**Fig.2b;
Tables S4,5**). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages,
did not exhibit this co-infiltration (**Fig.2b**). These results imply

d CD11c.MY populations demonstrated
significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (**Fig.2b;
Tables S4,5**). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages,
did not exhibit this co-infiltration (**Fig.2b**). These results imply
that lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated
process potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms.

We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with
clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the
abundance of lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival
(OS) (**Fig.2c**, **Fig.S4c**) and progression-free survival (PFS)
(**Fig.S4d**). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells also
showed association with longer PFS (**Fig.S4d**). Notably, in IDS
tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic significance,
suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to chemotherapy exposure
beyond immune cell abundance are relevant for prognosis, as previously
suggested[(10,13)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Bmv16s). To note,
the highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the
interpretability of individual and multivariable hazard ratios.

For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant
differences across the molecular profiles (**Fig.S4e**). However, the
EMT phenotype was enriched in BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS
samples (**Fig.S4e**). The relative abundance of these cancer cell
phenotypes was associated with OS (**Fig.2d**) and survival PFS
(**Fig.S4f**), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For
example, a higher abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was
associated with shorter OS in IDS tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT

ith OS (**Fig.2d**) and survival PFS
(**Fig.S4f**), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For
example, a higher abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was
associated with shorter OS in IDS tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT
cluster was linked to improved OS in PDS but not in IDS (**Fig.2d**). We
next performed multivariable Cox models using stricter inclusion
criteria for patients in the analyses (**Fig.S1b**) further confirming
differential associations with clinical outcomes (**Fig.S4g**).
Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship between immune
cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the
clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor\'s
exposure to chemotherapy.

**Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
chemotherapy exposure**

To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells
based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We
uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter
referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN; **Fig.2e-g**).
Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal (RCN01
to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells adjacent to
cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09 and RCN10)
and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial domains
included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial
cell-rich domain (RCN03; **Fig.2e-g**). The cancer spatial domains
included the phenotypically most heterogeneous neighbourhood (RCN04),
and four neighbourhoods were predominated by distinct tumor cell
functional states (**Fig.2e-g**). The immune spatial domains included an

**Fig.2e-g**). The cancer spatial domains
included the phenotypically most heterogeneous neighbourhood (RCN04),
and four neighbourhoods were predominated by distinct tumor cell
functional states (**Fig.2e-g**). The immune spatial domains included an
immune B cell neighborhood (RCN13), tentatively consisting of tertiary
lymphoid structures (**Fig.S4h**), and neighborhood of myeloid cells
(RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11; **Fig.2e-g**). These spatial domains were
validated with visual inspection of the t-CycIF images (**Fig.2f,g;
Fig.S4h**).\
\
Spatial composition analysis revealed that several of the domains were
intercorrelated (**Fig.2h; Tables S6,7**). For example, two immune
domains (RCN11 - T cells, RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation
with each other, as well as with the immune-infiltrated stroma (RCN02).
In contrast, the immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation
with these immune domains (**Fig.2h**). The cancer cell domains also
revealed significant correlations with other spatial domains. For
instance, the neighborhood of cancer idle phenotype (RCN01) was
positively correlated with the immune-cold tumor-stromal interface
(TSI), whereas the neighborhood of cancer cells displaying immunogenic
phenotypes was negatively correlated with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01;
**Fig.2h**). These findings suggest that the spatial domains of the TME
are functionally coordinated in HGSC.

We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the
clinicomolecular profiles. The overall comparisons did not reveal
significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple
hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most
heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in
*BRCA*loss tumors (**Fig.2i,j**).

d not reveal
significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple
hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most
heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in
*BRCA*loss tumors (**Fig.2i,j**). Interestingly, comparison of the
spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of
stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01, RCN02), and immune-cold
TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and proliferative
tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples (**Fig.2k**).
Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less abundant
in the IDS samples (**Fig.2k**) suggesting phenotypic convergence of
cancer cell populations following chemotherapy, consistent with our
previous observation[(7)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jfFZaD).
These results highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies
associated with the molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure.

**Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical
outcomes**

Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the
highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, **Fig.2e**), exhibiting
the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical
comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI
contained significantly higher proportions of T cells, myeloid cells,
macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence of coordinated immune
infiltration at the TSI (**Fig.2l**). Consistent with our previous
observations[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?k5VINq), the
*BRCA*loss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated
TSI (**Fig.2j**). By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was

**Fig.2l**). Consistent with our previous
observations[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?k5VINq), the
*BRCA*loss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated
TSI (**Fig.2j**). By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was
more abundant in the IDS samples compared to PDS (**Fig.2k**), was
significantly enriched in fibroblast stromal cells (**Fig.2l**).
Additionally, the immune-cold TSI showed a higher prevalence of
\'Idle\'-type cancer cells lacking activity-linked phenotypic marker
expressions, indicative of an \'immune-evasive\' TSI where cancer cells
could evade the recognition of the immune system (**Fig.2l**).
Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) had significantly more
active cancer phenotypes, with enriched proliferative, immunogenic and
EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive "immune escape" mechanisms at the
TSI[(14)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ljZVzE).

Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical
outcomes. Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy
sequence (PDS/IDS), we identified three RCNs (RCN03, RCN09, RCN10)
significantly associated with both PFS and OS (**Fig.S4i,j**). Notably,
the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition emerged as a
key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across the entire
patient cohort (**Fig.2m**). Specifically, a high abundance of the
immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved prognosis,
whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated with poorer
outcomes (**Fig.2m**).

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial
domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes
within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse

with poorer
outcomes (**Fig.2m**).

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial
domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes
within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse
spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The
intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial
organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a
coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the
necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features
associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.

### **CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features**

To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune
infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature
Importance Identification by RAndom forest), a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) framework (**Fig.3a**). The primary objective of
CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within
high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample
groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and
the identification of critical features (see Methods). Additionally, as
a versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional
data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and
molecular profiles (**Fig.3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig.1e,i; Fig.S1g**), morphological features and spatial
neighborhoods (**Fig.1j**), resulting in 43 feature representations per
cell (Materials and Methods).

of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig.1e,i; Fig.S1g**), morphological features and spatial
neighborhoods (**Fig.1j**), resulting in 43 feature representations per
cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features that
differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into
three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (**Fig.S5a,b**).
We generated CEFIIRA models for either cancer cells or the
immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (*BRCA*loss, HRD) or
HRP-like (HRP, *CCNE1*amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also
including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. This strategy
made it easier to interpret the predictive signal and dissect the
cell-type--specific contributions. As expected, the patient\'s age and
tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the
models (**Fig.S5c-e**). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited
better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors
in both PDS (**Fig.3b, Table S8**) and IDS (**Fig.3c, Table S8**), which
was not explained by overall feature variability (**Table S9**). These
results indicate that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with
differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal
single-cell spatial data. To account for anatomical site heterogeneity,
we trained CEFIIRA models exclusively on primary tumor sites
(adnex/ovary) and confirmed that the top predicted features remained
consistent (**Fig.S5f**).

### **Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival** 

Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as
one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples

(**Fig.S5f**).

### **Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival** 

Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as
one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples
(**Fig.S5c-e**). Moreover, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed
the highest fold-change enrichment from long-OS patients compared to
short-OS patients in HRP tumors (**Fig.3d,e; Table S8**). By contrast,
stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint
receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In
addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS
samples (**Fig.3d**). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in
cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring
cells at the TSI. However, the MHCII levels in cancer cells did not
correlate with CD3 or CD11b levels, confirming that this signal is not
due to technical spillover from neighboring T cells or myeloid cells
(**Fig.S5g**). Indeed, visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed
MHCII expression by cancer cells (**Fig.3f**). We also performed Xenium
single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which confirmed the co-localization
of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII complex, specifically on the
tumor cells (**Fig.3f**). In addition, MHCII was also the top predictive
marker in the immune-stromal cells in IDS samples (**Fig.3e)**.
Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII and immune-stromal
markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial proximity, are relevant
for OS prediction regardless of therapy sequence.

profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of

Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular
profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of
individual cells. We observed that PDS samples demonstrated superior
classification performance than IDS samples (**Fig.3g**; **Fig.S5h).**
In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher expression in
the HRD-like tumors (**Fig.3h; Fig.S5i**). The CEFIIRA model to classify
*BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest predictive performance
(Median F1=0.77, **Fig.3g**), with MHCII being the most differential
feature displaying higher expression in *BRCA*loss cancer cells
(**Fig.S5i,j**), also confirmed from the t-CycIF images (**Fig.3i**).
These results indicate that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression is
enriched in the HRD tumors. Characterization of the heterogeneity of
MHCII expression both across and within tumors revealed that 17% of
cancer cells exhibited MHCII expression levels comparable to or
exceeding those of CD11c+MY cells (**Fig.S5k**). Tumors showed variable
proportions of MHCII+ cancer cells (**Fig.3j**), with these proportions
positively correlated with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer cells (R =
0.45, p = 1.7e-13; **Fig.S5l**). However, several tumors displayed high
MHCII expression coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant
pattern (**Fig.S5l,m**). This suggests that MHCII expression in cancer
cells can occur independently of MHCI expression.

To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted
Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not

ccur independently of MHCI expression.

To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted
Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not
assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA
analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer
cells consistently predicted longer OS (**Fig.3k**), further
strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and
prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy
sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression
by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The
Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YuY7N0), by inferring the
cancer-cell-specific MHCII expression from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM
(RRID:SCR_027136). Notably, the observed MHCII expression was not
associated with genomic alterations (somatic mutations, loss of
heterozygosity, or promoter hypermethylation) in the MHCII gene
(**Fig.S5n**). However, its expression was higher in *BRCA*loss tumors
compared to HRP and *CCNE1*amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results
(**Fig.S5o**). Consistently using adjusted Cox models, we observed that
high MHCII expression by cancer cells predicted better OS in this
independent cohort at the mRNA level (**Fig.3l,m**). These findings
confirm that MHCII expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of
improved clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct
tumor-immune interactions within the TME.

### **Cancer MHCII expression associates with immune infiltration** 

To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on
overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune
populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor.
This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells
associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and
M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY; **Fig.4a**). To corroborate these
findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer
cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted
data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found that tumors with high
MHCII expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T
cells and M1-like macrophages (**Fig.S6a**). In contrast, tumors with
high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like
macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for
anti-tumor responses (**Fig.S6a**). Overall, these results demonstrate
that HGSC tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more
immune infiltration in the TME.

To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and
underlying transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both
from PDS (N=29), and IDS (n=22), from 29 patients using single-cell RNA
sequencing[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U9R4Iw)
(scRNA-seq;**Fig.4b**). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which
included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal
cells, and 97,409 immune cells, annotated using canonical phenotypic
markers as described

.org/google-docs/?U9R4Iw)
(scRNA-seq;**Fig.4b**). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which
included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal
cells, and 97,409 immune cells, annotated using canonical phenotypic
markers as described
previously[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YINNjV) (see
Methods). Using gene signature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a high
MHCII expression level similar to that of canonical antigen-presenting
cells (**Fig.S6b**), while showing no expression of immune-related
markers (**Fig.S6c**), supporting the accuracy of our annotations. The
proportion of MHCII positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable
intra-patient heterogeneity (**Fig.S6d,e**), prompting us to conduct
sample-based downstream analyses. We observed that samples with a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells had increased proportions of type 17
proinflammatory helper CD4+T cells, central memory (CM) CD8+T cells,
related to activation after antigen
exposure[(15)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?yQUz4s), and
precursor exhausted (PEX) CD8+T cells, which have traits of both
exhaustion and memory [(16)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?AHXPJo)
(**Fig.4c-e**). Similar results were observed at the patient level
taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations
(CM CD8+T cells), statistical significance was not reached, due to the
smaller sample size **(Fig.S6f**). Altogether, these findings suggest
that MHCII+ cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting
antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.

### **Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity**

To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial
organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression
across the cancer cell spatial domains (RCN04 to RCN08). Our analysis
revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (**Fig.S6g**),
abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers,
including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (**Fig.1l**). This indicates that
MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the
cancer cell spatial compartments.

We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII
expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two
TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII
in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to
the immune-cold TSI (RCN9; **Fig.S6g**). Consistently, analysis of the
cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells resided
more often in close proximity to immune cells as compared to MHCII-
cancer cells across the diverse clinicomolecular profiles (**Fig.4f;
Fig.S6h**). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly
neighboring stromal cells. Visual inspection of the tumor tissues
confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune
cells, forming immune 'hotspots', while MHCII- cells were embedded in
immune 'cold' tumor niches (**Fig.4g**).

To expand our analyses to whole tissue sections, we generated
high-dimensional single-cell spatial data using t-CycIF on 15 HRP-IDS
samples (**Fig.4h**) from an independent cohort in the Oncosys-OVA

edded in
immune 'cold' tumor niches (**Fig.4g**).

To expand our analyses to whole tissue sections, we generated
high-dimensional single-cell spatial data using t-CycIF on 15 HRP-IDS
samples (**Fig.4h**) from an independent cohort in the Oncosys-OVA
prospective trial (NCT06117384). We captured and phenotyped 10.5 million
cells using the TRIBUS (**Fig.4i; Fig.S7a**). The abundance of immune
cells did not correlate with the fraction of genome altered, tumor
mutational burden, or the number of HRD scars (**Fig.S7b,c**),
indicating that differences in these genomic features are unlikely to
bias our immune infiltration analyses. In line with previous findings,
we observed heterogeneity of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression within
and across the tumors (**Fig.S7d,e**). Consistently, a higher proportion
of MHCII+ cancer cells associated with longer progression-free survival
(PFS; **Fig.4j**). To note, CD8+ T-cell infiltration did not associate
with PFS (**Fig.S7f**), in agreement with the observation in the
multiomics cohort, underscoring the presence of tumor-intrinsic factors
influencing prognosis in IDS samples. Importantly, we observed that the
MHCII+ cancer cells (**Fig.S7d**) had significantly more neighboring
CD4+T, CD8+T, and CD11C+antigen presenting Myeloid cells as compared to
the MHCII- cells, generating spatial immune hotspots (**Fig.4k**). This
additional single-cell spatial analysis further highlights that MHCII+
cancer cells colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains, enriched in
T-cells and antigen presenting cells.

To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer
cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the matched tumor and adjacent

To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer
cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the matched tumor and adjacent
stromal regions TSI in 11 HGSC-IDS tumors (**Fig.4l**). Using
deconvolution, we observed that tumor MHCII expression was associated
with a significantly higher inferred proportion of particularly
dendritic cells and T cells in the adjacent stroma of the TSI
(**Fig.4m,n**). Altogether, these findings demonstrate that MHCII+
cancer cells colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains and associate
with spatial immune infiltration and antigen presentation at the TSI.

Table S10**). Notably, we observed a significantly augmented expression
of the immune checkpoint molecules TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+
tumors. Gene overrepresentation analysis with PANTHER revealed 14
enriched pathways related to immune activation (**Fig.5a**), including
both PD-1 and chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways
were enriched within the under-expressed genes. Consistently, in DEG
analysis of cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (**Fig.4b**), we found
that MHCII positivity was associated with upregulation of the

signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways

infiltration[(17--19)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?iw8uhD).

For this, we utilized expression data of 335 genes profiled with the
Nanostring technology
[(20)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?P6xWPt). Consistently, a high
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of

(**Fig.S8d; Table S11**). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11

[(21--23)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jDdLG3), were among the
top overexpressed genes as in TCGA, suggesting that IFN activation plays
a key role in MHCII-associated immune activation, as previously
suggested[(24--26)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5AXFHa).

transcriptomic profiles visualized in differential clusters in a UMAP

independent of MHCI. Further, using the scRNA-seq dataset, we observed
that the MHCII expression in cancer cells correlated with *IFNG* gene
expression specifically in CD4+ memory helper and CD8+ central memory
(CM) T cells, which are related to activation after antigen exposure
(**Fig.5g**). Overall, these findings reinforce the link between
cancer-intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation and highlight the role of
cancer cell MHCII in spatial immune activation, also independent of

To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in
MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict
ligand--receptor pairs (**Fig.5h, Fig.S9a**). We identified the top
seven ligand--receptor interactions across CD8+T cells, CD4+T cells,
Tregs, and DCs (**Fig.5i**). Interestingly, IFN signaling and
MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8+ and
CD4+T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated
lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions across the
cell populations, which may indicate the induction of immune
tolerance[(28)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dKHGmS). Similarly,
in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (**Fig.5j**),
we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII
ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL,
IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (**Fig.5k**). In addition, VEGFA, one of
the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative
correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell
interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at
the TSI (**Fig.5k**). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells
elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the
surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with
activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.
