## Sheet: Index and legends of tables
List of Supplementary tables
Index Legend
Table S1 Description of clinical information in the multidimensional cohort. For seven tumors, there was not matched clinical information.
Table S2 List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the multidimensional cohort (N=265). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
Table S3 The median of the median proportions of each immune population per tumor across molecular profiles. First, the median proportion of each immune population was calculated per tumor (across cores); then, for each molecular profile, the median of these tumor-level medians was computed. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis. N of each group is reported in Supplementary Fig. S4a.
Table S4 Pearson correlation values of the median immune infiltration population across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per population a median immune infiltration was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)
Table S5 False discovery rate (FDR) of the correlation test of the median immune infiltration correlation (Table S4)
Table S6 Pearson correlation values of the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN.

f the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251).
Table S7 False discovery rate (FDR) from the correlation test of the median RCN proportion across tumors (Table S6)
Table S8 Top predictive markers (from Supplementary Fig. 5c&d) for distinguishing long vs short overall survival (OS) HRDs and HRPs Cancer or Immune-Stromal cells, with reflected across corresponding predicted class median value of min-max scaled expression values after CEFIIRA preprocessing to highlight differences within the cell population. In addition, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.
Table S9 Quantified variability of CEFIIRA input features for OS models within HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Entropy indices in HRD and HRP tumors were calculated using Shannon entropy and Jensen–Shannon (JS) divergence. Each row represents the single-cell features used in the CEFIIRA models. The entropy difference between HRD-like and HRP-like tumors was assessed using a permutation test (50 folds), and the resulting p-value is reported.
Table S10 List of overexpressed genes in OVA-TCGA when comparing tumors with high cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.

gh cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.
Table S11 Results of differential expression analysis comparing tumors with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells in t-CycIF (N = 66) to those with a low proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells (N = 67) using the multidimensional cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented. Significance was assessed using a t-test, and p-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the false discovery rate (FDR). Genes were considered differentially expressed based on the following criteria: log2 fold change ≥ 1 or ≤ –1 and FDR < 10⁻³.
Table S12 Summary table of analysis performed on deconvoluted bulk RNAseq for 35 genes enriched in immunopeptidomic samples with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells and filtered based on healthy tissue HLA-II ligandome across three patient groups: with low (four samples), mid (four samples), and high (four samples) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Includes:
 - Median values of scaled expression for tumor and immune-stromal fractions in high group.
 - Median z-scores for tumor and immune-stromal fractions in high group.
 - Specificity of gene expression to the tumor fraction in the high group, assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor fraction with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal fractions combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
 - Fold change of tumor fraction expression in high vs.

scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal fractions combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
 - Fold change of tumor fraction expression in high vs. the mean tumor fraction expression in low and mid groups.
Table S13 Granzyme B response of CD8⁺ T cells in iPDCs following treatment with pembrolizumab (PE, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (TI, 10 µg/mL), or their combination (PE+TI). Responses per sample were calculated as the mean difference between treated (days 2–5) and control (d0) samples and analyzed separately for MHCII-high and MHCII-low cancer sample groups. The mean log2FC across samples in each group is reported in the corresponding column. Statistical significance was assessed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Averaged changes and pembrolizumab-induced (PE) changes in Granzyme B response are also shown.
Table S14 List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the whole slide validation cohort (N=15). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
Table S15 Logic table to define cell phenotypes using TRIBUS. A value of one (1) is considered a marker expected in the cell phenotype. A value of zero (0) is considered as a not relevant value to define the given cell phenotype. A value of minus one (-1) corresponds to expected negative markers in the corresponding cell phenotype.
Table S16 Performance for different types of models for the classification of individual cancer cells in to the molecular profiles using the entire multidimensional dataset after quality filter (N=253).

e markers in the corresponding cell phenotype.
Table S16 Performance for different types of models for the classification of individual cancer cells in to the molecular profiles using the entire multidimensional dataset after quality filter (N=253).

## Sheet: Index and legends of tables
List of Supplementary tables
Index Legend
Table S1 Description of clinical information in the multidimensional cohort. For seven tumors, there was not matched clinical information.
Table S2 List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the multidimensional cohort (N=265). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
Table S3 The median of the median proportions of each immune population per tumor across molecular profiles. First, the median proportion of each immune population was calculated per tumor (across cores); then, for each molecular profile, the median of these tumor-level medians was computed. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis. N of each group is reported in Supplementary Fig. S4a.
Table S4 Pearson correlation values of the median immune infiltration population across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per population a median immune infiltration was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)
Table S5 False discovery rate (FDR) of the correlation test of the median immune infiltration correlation (Table S4)
Table S6 Pearson correlation values of the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN.

f the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251).
Table S7 False discovery rate (FDR) from the correlation test of the median RCN proportion across tumors (Table S6)
Table S8 Top predictive markers (from Supplementary Fig. 5c&d) for distinguishing long vs short overall survival (OS) HRDs and HRPs Cancer or Immune-Stromal cells, with reflected across corresponding predicted class median value of min-max scaled expression values after CEFIIRA preprocessing to highlight differences within the cell population. In addition, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.
Table S9 Quantified variability of CEFIIRA input features for OS models within HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Entropy indices in HRD and HRP tumors were calculated using Shannon entropy and Jensen–Shannon (JS) divergence. Each row represents the single-cell features used in the CEFIIRA models. The entropy difference between HRD-like and HRP-like tumors was assessed using a permutation test (50 folds), and the resulting p-value is reported.
Table S10 List of overexpressed genes in OVA-TCGA when comparing tumors with high cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.

gh cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.
Table S11 Results of differential expression analysis comparing tumors with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells in t-CycIF (N = 66) to those with a low proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells (N = 67) using the multidimensional cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented. Significance was assessed using a t-test, and p-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the false discovery rate (FDR). Genes were considered differentially expressed based on the following criteria: log2 fold change ≥ 1 or ≤ –1 and FDR < 10⁻³.
Table S12 Summary table of analysis performed on deconvoluted bulk RNAseq for 35 genes enriched in immunopeptidomic samples with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells and filtered based on healthy tissue HLA-II ligandome across three patient groups: with low (four samples), mid (four samples), and high (four samples) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Includes:
 - Median values of scaled expression for tumor and immune-stromal fractions in high group.
 - Median z-scores for tumor and immune-stromal fractions in high group.
 - Specificity of gene expression to the tumor fraction in the high group, assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor fraction with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal fractions combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
 - Fold change of tumor fraction expression in high vs.

scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal fractions combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
 - Fold change of tumor fraction expression in high vs. the mean tumor fraction expression in low and mid groups.
Table S13 Granzyme B response of CD8⁺ T cells in iPDCs following treatment with pembrolizumab (PE, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (TI, 10 µg/mL), or their combination (PE+TI). Responses per sample were calculated as the mean difference between treated (days 2–5) and control (d0) samples and analyzed separately for MHCII-high and MHCII-low cancer sample groups. The mean log2FC across samples in each group is reported in the corresponding column. Statistical significance was assessed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Averaged changes and pembrolizumab-induced (PE) changes in Granzyme B response are also shown.
Table S14 List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the whole slide validation cohort (N=15). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
Table S15 Logic table to define cell phenotypes using TRIBUS. A value of one (1) is considered a marker expected in the cell phenotype. A value of zero (0) is considered as a not relevant value to define the given cell phenotype. A value of minus one (-1) corresponds to expected negative markers in the corresponding cell phenotype.
Table S16 Performance for different types of models for the classification of individual cancer cells in to the molecular profiles using the entire multidimensional dataset after quality filter (N=253).

vector_chunks.text:
REVISION [[Fernando Perez]{.underline}](mailto:frpvillatoro@gmail.com)

Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ):

The authors have addressed most of my comments to satisfaction, I only
have one remark.

The authors present new data supporting notion that MHCII is expressed
by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging. While
this is appreciated and supported by their functional experiments, I am
wondering why in Supp 3g and in the point-by-point response, the values
for MHCII on cancer cells don't line up between the different
correlation analyses (i.e. the correlations with CD11b, MHCII and CD3).
In the MHCII in cancer vs. neighbor plot there are lots of cancer MHCII
values between 11 and 15 while these are missing from the cancer MHCII
vs max(CD3d or CD11b). Where different input cells used here? The
authors should show this data for all tumor cells.

We appreciate the reviewer\'s comment, and we agree that we did not add
important descriptions of our analysis before and this can lead to
confusion. We added a clarification in the figure legend according to
our next comments.

Our previous analysis was performed using a random downsampling of 500
cancer cells. We adopted this approach because when all cancer cells
were included (N ≈ 2 million), the high dispersion in the data precluded
reliable visualization of correlated trends, as shown in the two figures
below that include all the cancer cells. Importantly, according to the
central limit theorem for correlated data, random sampling preserves the
statistical properties of the original distribution. This principle has
also been discussed and demonstrated in the context of spatial tumor
profiling by Jia-Ren Lin et al. (2023; PMID: 36669472), specifically in
Figures 2F and 2H.

vector_chunks.text:
ling preserves the
statistical properties of the original distribution. This principle has
also been discussed and demonstrated in the context of spatial tumor
profiling by Jia-Ren Lin et al. (2023; PMID: 36669472), specifically in
Figures 2F and 2H. To assess the robustness of this downsampling
strategy, we performed the following in-silico experiment. Downsampling
was conducted 10,000 times, with 500 cancer cells sampled per replicate.
For each replicate, the Pearson correlation coefficient (R) was
calculated between the expression of the indicated markers and MHCII in
the sampled cancer cells. The mean R across the 10,000 replicates is
reported together with the corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CI)
in the next table. In addition, the mean p-value and its confidence
intervals for testing the deviation of R from zero. As observed, the
mean correlations and confidence intervals across replicates fall within
the range of correlations previously reported in Supplementary Figure
S5g and are consistent with results obtained using the full dataset. We
therefore consider that displaying a downsampling is a more faithful
representation of the data for visualization purposes. Now in
Supplementary Figure S5g we added further details that the image
corresponds to a downsampling and added in addition the R calculated
using all cancer cells.

Shabanova]{.underline}](mailto:alexandras.emailbox@gmail.com) please add
this new figure legend to Supp Figure 5:*

**g** Scatter plot showing the intensity of immune markers (CD3d and
CD11b) versus MHCII intensity in cancer cells. The figure displays the
Pearson correlation coefficient (R), the correlation test p-value, and
the linear regression line (blue) based on a downsample of 500 cells.
Downsampling was preferred for visualization purposes, when all cancer
cells are included (N ≈ 2 million), the correlation with MHCII is R =
0.26 for CD11b and R = 0.25 for CD3d, respectively.

## Slide 2
Objectives of the study & their impact
The project aims to discover how the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and MHC dysregulation facilitate immune escape in ovarian cancer:
Objective 1. Identify MHC patterns in HGSC subtypes and determine their clinical significance. 
Using t-CycIF, we profiled single-cell spatial landscapes of HGSC in 265 samples and characterized cellular communities and their interactions. Through machine-learning–guided feature extraction, we identified distinct MHC dysregulation patterns associated with specific HGSC molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.
ImpactThis work demonstrates that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is a key determinant of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness. The findings, validated across four independent cohorts, were submitted to Cancer Discovery (second author contribution) and provide strong evidence that spatially resolved antigen presentation programs influence prognosis and therapeutic response.
Objective 2. Elucidate molecular mechanisms of TME-dependent MHC dysregulation and tumor progression. 
To investigate how chemotherapy and TME remodeling influence MHC regulation, we integrated spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (NanoString GeoMx) in 41 tumor tissues (including rare paired pre- and post-chemotherapy samples). Across 1,180 transcriptomic segments, we are identifying transcriptional programs associated with spatial immunophenotypes and resistance mechanisms.
Impact:This integrative approach enables the identification of molecular pathways underlying resistant spatial niches. By linking spatial immune architecture with gene-expression programs, we aim to reveal targetable vulnerabilities in chemotherapy-resistant HGSC.

egrative approach enables the identification of molecular pathways underlying resistant spatial niches. By linking spatial immune architecture with gene-expression programs, we aim to reveal targetable vulnerabilities in chemotherapy-resistant HGSC.
Collectively, these objectives provide mechanistic insight into immune evasion in ovarian cancer and establish spatial MHC dysregulation as a biomarker-driven framework for personalized therapy and drug development.

## Slide 5
Objective 2: Spatial MHC Dysregulation & Resistance Mechanisms
Data integration is performed across 41 tumors and 1,180 segments using tCycIF and GeoMx, with explicit ROI identification.
SPACEStat analyzes cell networks, including direct niche diagrams, molecular programs via heatmap, and validation.
Direct niche diagrams contrast an active immune niche (MHCII+ tumor cells and activated T cells) with a resistant niche (MHCII- tumor cells and excluded T cells).
Molecular programs, presented in a mini-heatmap, show differential gene/pathway expression between these niche types.
Direct visual validation includes a bar chart demonstrating a significant difference in patient outcome scores between active and resistant signatures, as well as a validation shield with a checkmark.
The key result concludes that the research identified transcriptional programs driving resistant niches.

## Sheet: Index and legends of tables
List of Supplementary tables
Index Legend
Table 1 Description of clinical information in the multidimensional cohort. For seven tumors, there was not matched clinical information.
Table 2 List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the multidimensional cohort (N=265). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
Table 3 The median of the median proportions of each immune population per tumor across molecular profiles. First, the median proportion of each immune population was calculated per tumor (across cores); then, for each molecular profile, the median of these tumor-level medians was computed. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis. N of each group is reported in Supplementary Fig. 2a.
Table 4 Pearson correlation values of the median immune infiltration population across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per population a median immune infiltration was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)
Table 5 False discovery rate (FDR) of the correlation test of the median immune infiltration correlation (Table 3)
Table 6 Pearson correlation values of the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN.

f the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251).
Table 7 False discovery rate (FDR) from the correlation test of the median RCN proportion across tumors (Table 6)
Table 8 Top predictive markers (from Supplementary Fig. 3c&d) for distinguishing long vs short overall survival (OS) HRDs and HRPs Cancer or Immune-Stromal cells, with reflected across corresponding predicted class median value of min-max scaled expression values after CEFIIRA preprocessing to highlight differences within the cell population. In addition, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.
Table 9 List of overexpressed genes in OVA-TCGA when comparing tumors with high cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.
Table 10 Results of differential expression analysis comparing tumors with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells in t-CycIF (N = 66) to those with a low proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells (N = 67) using the multidimensional cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented. Significance was assessed using a t-test, and p-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the false discovery rate (FDR).

cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented. Significance was assessed using a t-test, and p-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the false discovery rate (FDR). Genes were considered differentially expressed based on the following criteria: log2 fold change ≥ 1 or ≤ –1 and FDR < 10⁻³.
Table 11 Granzyme B response of CD8⁺ T cells in iPDCs following treatment with pembrolizumab (P10, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (T10, 10 µg/mL), or their combination (P10T10). Responses per sample were calculated as the mean difference between treated (days 2–5) and control (d0) samples and analyzed separately for MHCII-high and MHCII-low cancer sample groups. The mean log2FC across samples in each group is reported in the corresponding column. Statistical significance was assessed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Averaged changes and pembrolizumab-induced (P10) changes in Granzyme B response are also shown.

**22_NKI_project**

Analysis of a large collection of 265 tumors, using tCycif and 30
panels, and using 10 tissue microarrays, using four cores per tumor.
Further information is in the publication:
[[https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492)
